<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bioelectric Amplifiers — Multiple-Input Circuits

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)
A **Bioelectric Multiple-Input Amplifier** is a specialized analog front-end circuit designed to extract low-amplitude bioelectric signals (typically in the range of **1 µV to 5 mV**) that are superimposed on large common-mode interferences such as the **50/60 Hz power-line pickup** and DC electrode half-cell potentials. Multiple-input configurations — particularly the **Difference Amplifier**, **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)**, and **Driven-Right-Leg (DRL) circuit** — exploit the spatial separation of electrode pairs to achieve high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**, typically **≥ 80 dB** in medical-grade equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Multiple-input bioelectric amplifiers form the **front-end of every clinical biopotential recorder** (ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG). The three "canonical" circuits — *Difference Amplifier*, *Instrumentation Amplifier*, and *Driven-Right-Leg Circuit* — are the most frequently tested topics in Module 2.

---

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Ear Hearing Trick"
Imagine you are standing in a noisy marketplace trying to listen to a faint whisper from a friend who is **5 metres away**.

- If you cup **one ear**, you hear the whisper, but it is drowned out by the market noise.
- If you cup **both ears** and your brain **subtracts the common noise** while **amplifying the difference** (your friend is closer to one ear than the other), the whisper becomes crystal clear.

This is exactly what a multiple-input bioelectric amplifier does:
- **Two electrodes** pick up the same environmental noise (common-mode).
- The amplifier **subtracts** the two signals, **cancelling the noise**, and **amplifies** the small differential bioelectric signal that differs between electrodes.

| Real-World Element | Circuit Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Two ears | Two input electrodes (V₁, V₂) |
| Background market noise | Common-mode interference (50 Hz mains) |
| Whisper from a friend | Differential biopotential (ECG/EEG) |
| Brain's subtraction | Difference / Instrumentation Amplifier |
| Active "ear cupping" focus | High input impedance op-amp buffers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Bioelectric signals (ECG ≈ 1 mV, EEG ≈ 50 µV, EMG ≈ 100 µV) are **100× to 1000× smaller** than the interfering 50 Hz mains pickup that may be several volts at the body surface. Therefore, the amplifier must *reject* the common-mode noise by factors of **10³ to 10⁵** while *amplifying* the differential signal by a factor of **100 to 10,000**.

---

### Why Single-Input Amplifiers Fail for Biopotentials
A simple non-inverting or inverting op-amp cannot be used directly because:

1. **Electrode impedance mismatch** — Skin-electrode contact impedance differs at the two electrode sites, converting common-mode noise into differential noise.
2. **Patient safety** — Direct connection to mains-isolated power is unsafe; isolation is mandatory.
3. **Loading effect** — Source impedance of electrodes can be **5 kΩ to 50 kΩ**; a low-impedance input is required.
4. **DC offset rejection** — Half-cell potentials up to **±300 mV** must be blocked while preserving sub-Hz bioelectric content.

These limitations necessitate the **multiple-input topology** — a cascade of buffering, differencing, and active common-mode feedback.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Basic Difference Amplifier

The foundational building block is the **single-op-amp difference amplifier**. It uses **four precision resistors** and produces an output proportional to the difference of two inputs.

### Operating Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Input stage:** Two input voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$ are applied to the inverting ($R_1$) and non-inverting ($R_2$) paths.
2. **Resistor network:** External resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ form a balanced bridge.
3. **Superposition principle:** Output is the linear combination of amplified versions of $V_1$ and $V_2$.
4. **Ideal matched condition:** When $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$, the common-mode gain becomes zero.
5. **Output expression:** $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$

### Limitation: Low Input Impedance
The basic difference amplifier draws current from the source through $R_1$ and $R_2$. Input impedance is **only $R_1 + R_2$** (typically 10 kΩ–100 kΩ), which is *insufficient* for high-impedance electrode sources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The basic difference amplifier is the **mathematical core** of every ECG lead selector. However, due to low input impedance, it is *never* used as the first stage in patient-connected equipment.

---

## 2.2 The Improved Difference Amplifier (Buffered / Super-Difference)

To overcome the loading problem, **two unity-gain voltage followers (buffers)** are added at the inputs. This gives an **extremely high input impedance** (>10¹² Ω) but does not yet solve the gain-adjustment problem.

### Why a Single Difference Stage is Not Enough
If we want to *change the gain*, we must vary **two matched resistors simultaneously** — practically difficult because resistor mismatch destroys CMRR.

This motivates the **three op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier**.

---

## 2.3 The Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (IA / INA)

This is the **gold-standard topology** for all biopotential front-ends.

### Topology Description

- **Stage 1:** Two op-amps (A1, A2) configured as non-inverting amplifiers sharing a single gain-setting resistor $R_G$.
- **Stage 2:** A unity-gain difference amplifier (A3) with four precision resistors removes the common-mode signal and provides single-ended output.

### How the Gain is Set

The gain is controlled by **one single external resistor $R_G$**, leaving the matching of the four difference-amplifier resistors untouched. This preserves CMRR even when the gain is changed.

### Key Advantages

| Property | Value / Comment |
|---|---|
| Differential gain (A_d) | Adjustable via $R_G$ (1 to 10,000) |
| Common-mode gain (A_c) | Ideally zero (only resistor mismatch limits it) |
| Input impedance | ≥ 10¹² Ω (set by JFET/MOSFET op-amp inputs) |
| CMRR (DC to 1 kHz) | 80 dB to 120 dB in modern INA chips |
| Bandwidth | 0.05 Hz to 10 kHz typical for biopotential use |

---

## 2.4 The Driven-Right-Leg (DRL) Circuit

The DRL circuit is an *active common-mode feedback loop* that **returns the common-mode noise back to the patient body** through a third electrode, forcing the body potential to track the amplifier's common-mode reference.

### Operating Logic
1. The common-mode voltage is sensed (typically by averaging the two electrode signals).
2. This common-mode signal is **inverted and amplified** by an auxiliary op-amp.
3. The inverted signal is fed back to the patient via a third electrode (the "right-leg" or "ground" electrode in ECG).
4. The negative feedback forces the body potential to follow the amplifier reference, **drastically reducing 50 Hz mains pickup** at the source rather than relying solely on the amplifier's CMRR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** The DRL circuit **complements** the instrumentation amplifier. The IA rejects common-mode noise; the DRL **prevents the common-mode noise from building up** in the first place. Together, they achieve **> 100 dB effective CMRR** — a standard KTU problem involves calculating total system CMRR from the two contributions.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ in tables** to avoid breaking the markdown pipe. Here, all equations are placed *inside the cells using LaTeX*, not the markdown table syntax pipes.

**Table 2.1 — Key Formulas for Multiple-Input Bioelectric Amplifiers**

| # | Circuit | Key Equation | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Difference Amplifier (matched) | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\,(V_2 - V_1)$ | $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ in Ω; require $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}=\dfrac{R_4}{R_3}$ |
| 2 | Basic Difference Amplifier (general) | $V_{out} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3}\,V_2 - \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\,V_1$ | Holds for arbitrary resistors |
| 3 | Common-Mode Gain (mismatched) | $A_{cm} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{R_4}{R_3} - \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | Mismatch $\Delta R / R$ sets $A_{cm}$ |
| 4 | Differential Gain | $A_d = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{R_4}{R_3} + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$ | For balanced source impedance |
| 5 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right)$ | Expressed in **dB** |
| 6 | CMRR due to Resistor Mismatch | $\text{CMRR} \approx 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{A_d}{4\,\Delta R/R}\right)$ | $\Delta R / R$ = fractional mismatch |
| 7 | Instrumentation Amplifier Gain | $A_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right)$ | $R_G$ = gain-setting resistor |
| 8 | INA — Simplified (when R2 = R3, R1 = R1′) | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}$ | Single-resistor gain control |
| 9 | DRL Loop Gain | $A_{DRL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Negative feedback through body electrode |
| 10 | Total System CMRR | $\text{CMRR}_{tot} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{\text{CMRR}_{INA}} + \dfrac{1}{\text{CMRR}_{DRL}}}$ | Combined in dB (use rule of 1/CMRR) |
| 11 | Input-Referred Noise | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k T R\, \Delta f + e_n^2 \Delta f}$ | $e_n$ = op-amp voltage noise density (nV/√Hz) |
| 12 | Noise Figure (multi-stage) | $F = F_1 + \dfrac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \dfrac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}$ | Friis' formula; $G$ = power gain |
| 13 | Bandwidth Lower Cutoff | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{in} C_{in}}$ | High-pass filter at input |
| 14 | Skin-Electrode Impedance | $Z_{skin} \approx 5\text{ k}\Omega \text{ to } 50\text{ k}\Omega$ | Varies with electrode type and skin prep |
| 15 | ECG Signal Amplitude | $\approx 0.5 \text{ mV to } 5 \text{ mV}$ | Measured across standard lead pairs |

---

## 2.6 Practical Engineering Utility

| Application | Typical Gain | Bandwidth | Front-End Topology |
|---|---|---|---|
| ECG (12-lead) | 1000 (60 dB) | 0.05–150 Hz | 3-Op-Amp INA + DRL |
| EEG | 20,000 (86 dB) | 0.1–100 Hz | INA + chopper-stabilized |
| EMG (surface) | 1000 (60 dB) | 20–500 Hz | INA with AC coupling |
| EOG | 1000 (60 dB) | DC–30 Hz | DC-coupled INA |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output of the Basic Difference Amplifier

### Circuit Description
A single op-amp has:
- $V_1$ connected to the inverting input through $R_1$, with feedback resistor $R_2$ from output to the inverting node.
- $V_2$ connected to the non-inverting input through $R_3$, with $R_4$ from the non-inverting node to ground.

### Goal
Derive $V_{out}$ as a function of $V_1$ and $V_2$ (no other constraints).

### Step 1 — Voltage at the Non-Inverting Input
The non-inverting node $V^+$ is a simple resistive divider of $V_2$ through $R_3$ and $R_4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{+} &= V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** No current flows into the ideal op-amp input, so all $V_2$ current passes through $R_3$ then $R_4$ to ground. The voltage at the divider midpoint is $V_2$ scaled by the divider ratio.

### Step 2 — Apply the Virtual Short
For an ideal op-amp in negative feedback:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{-} &= V^{+} = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The high open-loop gain forces the differential input voltage to zero in steady state (assuming negative feedback is present through $R_2$).

### Step 3 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node
Sum of currents leaving the inverting node equals zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_1 - V^{-}}{R_1} + \frac{V_{out} - V^{-}}{R_2} &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The current from $V_1$ through $R_1$ plus the current from $V_{out}$ through $R_2$ must sum to zero (no current into op-amp input).

### Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$ in Terms of $V_1$ and $V^{-}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{out} - V^{-}}{R_2} &= \frac{V^{-} - V_1}{R_1} \\[4pt]
V_{out} - V^{-} &= \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,(V^{-} - V_1) \\[4pt]
V_{out} &= V^{-} + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,V^{-} - \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,V_1 \\[4pt]
V_{out} &= V^{-}\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,V_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** We algebraically isolate $V_{out}$ by multiplying both sides by $R_2$ and rearranging.

### Step 5 — Substitute $V^{-}$ from Step 2

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \left[V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right]\!\left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,V_1 \\[6pt]
V_{out} &= V_2 \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1} - \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,V_1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Matched-Resistor Condition
For ideal difference-amplifier behavior, choose $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= V_2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1} - \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,V_1 \\[6pt]
V_{out} &= V_2 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1} - \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,V_1 \\[6pt]
\boxed{\,V_{out} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,(V_2 - V_1)\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:** A clean, gain-controlled difference between the two input voltages.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Common-Mode Gain of Mismatched Difference Amplifier

### Goal
Determine the common-mode gain $A_{cm}$ when the resistor ratio has a small mismatch.

### Setup
Let the resistor ratios be nominally equal but with a small error $\epsilon$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_2}{R_1} &= A_d\,(1 + \epsilon), \qquad \frac{R_4}{R_3} = A_d\,(1 - \epsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $A_d = R_2/R_1$ is the nominal differential gain. Common-mode input is $V_{cm} = V_1 = V_2$.

### Step 1 — Substitute into the General Output Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \frac{R_4}{R_3}\,V_2 - \frac{R_2}{R_1}\,V_1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Set $V_1 = V_2 = V_{cm}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \left(\frac{R_4}{R_3} - \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) V_{cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Mismatched Ratios

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \big[\,A_d(1-\epsilon) - A_d(1+\epsilon)\,\big]\,V_{cm} \\[4pt]
&= A_d\,(1-\epsilon - 1 - \epsilon)\,V_{cm} \\[4pt]
&= -2 A_d\, \epsilon\, V_{cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Common-Mode Gain

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{cm} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{cm}} = -2 A_d\, \epsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — CMRR in dB

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMRR} &= 20\,\log_{10}\!\left|\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right| = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left|\frac{A_d}{2 A_d \epsilon}\right| = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2\epsilon}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,\text{CMRR} \approx -20\,\log_{10}(2\epsilon)\;\text{dB}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** If resistor matching is 0.1 % ($\epsilon = 0.001$), then $\text{CMRR} = -20\log_{10}(0.002) \approx 54$ dB. To achieve 80 dB CMRR, we need matching better than **0.01 %**, which is why INA chips use **laser-trimmed thin-film resistors** on-chip.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier Gain

### Circuit Topology
- **Op-amp A1** has inverting input connected to $V_1$ via $R_1$, and feedback to its output via $R_1$ (call this $R_1$).
- **Op-amp A2** has inverting input connected to $V_2$ via $R_1$, and feedback to its output via $R_1$ (the same $R_1$).
- **Resistor $R_G$** connects the inverting inputs of A1 and A2.
- **Op-amp A3** is a unity-gain difference amplifier with resistors $R_2, R_3, R_4, R_5$ (matched as $R_2 = R_4$ and $R_3 = R_5$).

### Step 1 — Apply Virtual Short at A1 and A2 Inputs
Let the voltages at the inverting nodes of A1 and A2 be $V_a$ and $V_b$ respectively. By the virtual-short principle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_a &= V_1, \qquad V_b = V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Current Through $R_G$
The same current $I_G$ flows through $R_G$ (from node b to node a) and through both $R_1$ feedback resistors (a closed loop):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_G &= \frac{V_b - V_a}{R_G} = \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_G}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Output of A1
The current through $R_1$ (between $V_a$ and $V_{o1}$) equals $I_G$ (KCL at inverting node of A1):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_G &= \frac{V_a - V_{o1}}{R_1} = \frac{V_1 - V_{o1}}{R_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $V_{o1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{o1} &= V_1 - I_G\,R_1 = V_1 - R_1 \cdot \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_G}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Output of A2
Similarly, the current through the other $R_1$ is $I_G$ but flowing in the opposite sense:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{o2} &= V_2 + I_G\,R_1 = V_2 + R_1 \cdot \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_G}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Difference $V_{o2} - V_{o1}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{o2} - V_{o1} &= \left(V_2 - V_1\right) + 2 R_1 \cdot \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R_G} \\[4pt]
&= (V_2 - V_1)\!\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply Unity-Gain Difference Stage (A3)
The third op-amp A3 amplifies the difference $V_{o2} - V_{o1}$ by the ratio $R_3 / R_2$ (assuming matched $R_2 = R_4$, $R_3 = R_5$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out} &= \frac{R_3}{R_2}\,(V_{o2} - V_{o1}) = \frac{R_3}{R_2}\,(V_2 - V_1)\!\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Final Compact Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{\,A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_2 - V_1} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\!\left(\frac{R_3}{R_2}\right)\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)
Let $R_1 = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_G = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \left(1 + \frac{2 \times 25\text{k}}{1\text{k}}\right)\!\left(\frac{10\text{k}}{10\text{k}}\right) = (1 + 50)(1) = 51
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gain = 51 (≈ 34 dB).** By simply changing $R_G$ to 100 Ω, the gain becomes **501**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Combined System CMRR with DRL Feedback

### Setup
The INA provides $\text{CMRR}_{INA}$ (in linear units, not dB), and the DRL provides an additional active common-mode reduction equivalent to $\text{CMRR}_{DRL}$. They act in series on the common-mode signal.

### Step 1 — Express Each in Linear Units

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMRR}_{INA}^{lin} &= 10^{\text{CMRR}_{INA}^{dB}/20} \\[4pt]
\text{CMRR}_{DRL}^{lin} &= 10^{\text{CMRR}_{DRL}^{dB}/20}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Combined Effect (1/x Addition Rule for Series Stages)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\text{CMRR}_{tot}^{lin}} = \frac{1}{\text{CMRR}_{INA}^{lin}} + \frac{1}{\text{CMRR}_{DRL}^{lin}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert Back to dB

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMRR}_{tot}^{dB} = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\text{CMRR}_{tot}^{lin}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example
Let $\text{CMRR}_{INA} = 80$ dB and $\text{CMRR}_{DRL} = 40$ dB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CMRR}_{INA}^{lin} &= 10^{80/20} = 10^4 = 10{,}000 \\[4pt]
\text{CMRR}_{DRL}^{lin} &= 10^{40/20} = 10^2 = 100 \\[4pt]
\frac{1}{\text{CMRR}_{tot}} &= \frac{1}{10{,}000} + \frac{1}{100} = 0.0001 + 0.01 = 0.0101 \\[4pt]
\text{CMRR}_{tot}^{lin} &= \frac{1}{0.0101} \approx 99 \\[4pt]
\text{CMRR}_{tot}^{dB} &\approx 20\,\log_{10}(99) \approx 39.9 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The DRL's contribution of 40 dB is **negligible** compared to the INA's 80 dB. The *worse* stage dominates the system CMRR. To improve the system, the **INA must be upgraded**; DRL alone cannot save a poor INA. This is a frequent KTU viva question.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Input-Referred Noise of an INA Front-End

The total input-referred voltage noise of a three-op-amp INA is dominated by the *input stage* op-amps (A1, A2), since their noise is amplified by the full gain of the IA, while A3's noise appears after the gain.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{n,in}^{total} = \sqrt{2\,e_n^2 + 2\,i_n^2\,R_s^2 + 4 k T R_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $e_n$ = voltage noise density of each input op-amp (nV/√Hz)
- $i_n$ = current noise density (pA/√Hz)
- $R_s$ = source resistance (electrode + skin)
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant = $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $T$ = absolute temperature (310 K for body)

The factor of 2 appears because *two* input op-amps (A1 and A2) contribute independently and uncorrelated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Body temperature is 310 K, not 300 K** — this is a small but important correction for medical-grade amplifier design.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of the Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

The following **Mermaid block diagram** shows the *signal flow* through the IA — the most critical KTU Module 2 diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeV1["V1 ELECTRODE IN+"] --> nodeR1a["R1 BUFFER INPUT A1"]
    nodeV2["V2 ELECTRODE IN-"] --> nodeR1b["R1 BUFFER INPUT A2"]
    nodeR1a --> nodeA1["OPAMP A1 NON-INVERTING BUFFER"]
    nodeR1b --> nodeA2["OPAMP A2 NON-INVERTING BUFFER"]
    nodeRG["R_G GAIN SET RESISTOR"] --> nodeA1
    nodeRG --> nodeA2
    nodeA1 --> nodeVo1["V_O1 STAGE 1 OUTPUT"]
    nodeA2 --> nodeVo2["V_O2 STAGE 1 OUTPUT"]
    nodeVo1 --> nodeR3a["R3 DIFF STAGE INPUT TOP"]
    nodeVo2 --> nodeR3b["R3 DIFF STAGE INPUT BOTTOM"]
    nodeR3a --> nodeA3["OPAMP A3 UNITY GAIN DIFFERENCE"]
    nodeR3b --> nodeA3
    nodeR2["R2 R4 MATCHED FEEDBACK"] --> nodeA3
    nodeA3 --> nodeVout["V_OUT SINGLE ENDED OUTPUT"]
    nodeVcm["V_CM COMMON MODE NOISE"] -.common to both inputs.-> nodeV1
    nodeVcm -.common to both inputs.-> nodeV2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Two patient electrodes feed $V_1$ and $V_2$ to op-amps A1 and A2.
- A *single* resistor $R_G$ between the two op-amp inverting nodes sets the gain.
- Stage-1 outputs $V_{O1}$ and $V_{O2}$ differ by an amplified version of $(V_2 - V_1)$.
- A3 rejects the common-mode component and produces a clean single-ended $V_{out}$.

---

## 4.2 Complete Bioelectric Amplifier System with DRL

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BODY["PATIENT BODY ELECTRODE INTERFACE"]
        nodeE1["ELECTRODE 1 V1"]
        nodeE2["ELECTRODE 2 V2"]
        nodeE3["ELECTRODE 3 DRL RETURN"]
    end

    subgraph INASTAGE["STAGE 1 INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER"]
        nodeB1["BUFFER OPAMP A1"]
        nodeB2["BUFFER OPAMP A2"]
        nodeGAIN["R_G GAIN RESISTOR"]
        nodeDA["DIFF OPAMP A3"]
    end

    subgraph DRLSTAGE["STAGE 2 DRIVEN RIGHT LEG LOOP"]
        nodeSUM["COMMON MODE SUMMER"]
        nodeINV["INVERTING OPAMP A4"]
        nodeRF["FEEDBACK RESISTOR R_F"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["STAGE 3 OUTPUT PROCESSING"]
        nodeHPF["HIGH PASS FILTER 0.05 HZ"]
        nodeLPF["LOW PASS FILTER 150 HZ"]
        nodeAMP["POST AMPLIFIER GAIN 10"]
    end

    nodeE1 --> nodeB1
    nodeE2 --> nodeB2
    nodeB1 --> nodeDA
    nodeB2 --> nodeDA
    nodeGAIN -.controls gain.-> nodeB1
    nodeGAIN -.controls gain.-> nodeB2
    nodeDA --> nodeHPF
    nodeHPF --> nodeLPF
    nodeLPF --> nodeAMP
    nodeAMP --> nodeECGOUT["ECG OUTPUT TO ADC"]

    nodeB1 --> nodeSUM
    nodeB2 --> nodeSUM
    nodeSUM --> nodeINV
    nodeINV --> nodeRF
    nodeRF --> nodeE3
    nodeE3 -.active feedback to body.-> nodeSUM
```

**Key signal flows shown:**
- **Differential path (top):** Electrode → INA → HPF → LPF → Post-Amp → ADC.
- **Common-mode feedback (loop):** INA inputs → Summer → Inverter → Patient body electrode 3.
- The DRL loop **closes the negative feedback** around the patient body, *actively* reducing mains pickup at its source.

---

## 4.3 CMRR vs Frequency Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDC["DC 0 HZ CMRR 120 DB"] --> node100["100 HZ CMRR 80 DB"]
    node100 --> node1k["1 KHZ CMRR 60 DB"]
    node1k --> node10k["10 KHZ CMRR 40 DB"]
    node10k --> node100k["100 KHZ CMRR 20 DB"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** CMRR is **not constant with frequency**. It rolls off above the op-amp's dominant pole. Above ~1 kHz, layout parasitic capacitances and source-impedance asymmetry further degrade CMRR. This is why ECG/EEG systems use **chopper stabilization** or **capacitively-coupled flying-capacitor techniques** for high-frequency common-mode rejection.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Comparison of Three Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TOPO1["BASIC DIFFERENCE AMPLIFIER"]
        nodeT1V1["V1"] --> nodeT1R1["R1"]
        nodeT1R1 --> nodeT1OP["OPAMP"]
        nodeT1V2["V2"] --> nodeT1R3["R3"]
        nodeT1R3 --> nodeT1OP
        nodeT1OP --> nodeT1OUT["V_OUT"]
    end

    subgraph TOPO2["IMPROVED DIFF AMP BUFFERED"]
        nodeT2V1["V1"] --> nodeT2B1["BUFFER A1"]
        nodeT2V2["V2"] --> nodeT2B2["BUFFER A2"]
        nodeT2B1 --> nodeT2OP["DIFF OPAMP A3"]
        nodeT2B2 --> nodeT2OP
        nodeT2OP --> nodeT2OUT["V_OUT"]
    end

    subgraph TOPO3["INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER"]
        nodeT3V1["V1"] --> nodeT3A1["GAIN OPAMP A1"]
        nodeT3V2["V2"] --> nodeT3A2["GAIN OPAMP A2"]
        nodeT3A1 --> nodeT3A3["DIFF OPAMP A3"]
        nodeT3A2 --> nodeT3A3
        nodeT3A3 --> nodeT3OUT["V_OUT"]
    end
```

| Topology | Input Z | Gain Adjust | CMRR | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Basic Difference Amp | Low (10–100 kΩ) | Two matched resistors | Poor (mismatch-limited) | Educational only |
| Buffered Difference Amp | Very High | Two matched resistors | Better | Lab instruments |
| Instrumentation Amplifier | Very High | **One resistor** | **Excellent** | **All biopotential front-ends** |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a bioelectric amplifier. Why is high CMRR essential in ECG amplifiers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
CMRR is defined as the ratio of differential-mode gain $A_d$ to common-mode gain $A_{cm}$, expressed in decibels:
$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert}\right)$$
High CMRR is essential in ECG amplifiers because the differential ECG signal (~1 mV) is superimposed on a large common-mode 50 Hz mains interference that may be several volts at the body. Without high CMRR, the mains pickup would swamp the ECG. Standards (IEC 60601) require at least 80 dB for diagnostic ECG.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is a Driven-Right-Leg (DRL) circuit? State two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Driven-Right-Leg circuit is an active common-mode feedback network used in biopotential amplifiers. The common-mode voltage sensed at the patient electrodes is inverted and fed back to the patient body through a third electrode, forcing the body potential to follow the amplifier reference.
**Advantages:** (1) Reduces 50/60 Hz mains interference at the source, (2) improves patient safety by limiting the body current to a safe value, (3) relaxes the CMRR requirement of the instrumentation amplifier.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Format:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** (with both sub-parts) from a choice of two.

---

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`
**(a) With a neat circuit diagram, derive the output voltage expression of a basic single-op-amp difference amplifier. State the condition required for it to function as an ideal difference amplifier.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) An ECG amplifier uses an instrumentation amplifier with $R_1 = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = R_4 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_3 = R_5 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate the value of $R_G$ needed to obtain a gain of 1000. If the resistor matching tolerance is 0.05 %, calculate the resulting CMRR in dB.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-A (a)

**Step 1 — Define the circuit:** A single op-amp with $V_1$ at inverting input via $R_1$ and feedback $R_2$; $V_2$ at non-inverting input via $R_3$ and grounded via $R_4$.

**Step 2 — Apply voltage divider at non-inverting input:**
$$V^{+} = V_2\,\dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}$$

**Step 3 — Virtual short:** $V^{-} = V^{+}$

**Step 4 — KCL at inverting node:**
$$\dfrac{V_1 - V^{-}}{R_1} = \dfrac{V^{-} - V_{out}}{R_2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**
$$V_{out} = V^{-}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) - \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}V_1$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $V^{+}$ and rearrange:**
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)V_2 - \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}V_1$$

**Step 7 — Apply the matching condition:** $\dfrac{R_2}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_4}{R_3} = k$. Then:
$$V_{out} = k\,(V_2 - V_1)$$

**Condition for ideal difference behavior:** The resistor ratios must be *exactly* matched, i.e., $R_2/R_1 = R_4/R_3$. In practice, this is hard to achieve with discrete components, motivating the instrumentation amplifier.

**(Marks distribution: 1 for circuit diagram, 4 for derivation, 1 for matching condition, 1 for conclusion.)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-A (b)

**Step 1 — Use the INA gain formula:**
$$A_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values** (note: $R_3/R_2 = 10\text{k}/10\text{k} = 1$):
$$1000 = \left(1 + \dfrac{2 \times 25{,}000}{R_G}\right)\!(1)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_G$:**
$$1 + \dfrac{50{,}000}{R_G} = 1000 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dfrac{50{,}000}{R_G} = 999 \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_G = \dfrac{50{,}000}{999} \approx 50.05\,\Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_G \approx 50.05\,\Omega}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate CMRR due to 0.05 % resistor mismatch:**
With $\epsilon = 0.05\% = 5 \times 10^{-4}$:

$$\text{CMRR} = -20\,\log_{10}(2\epsilon) = -20\,\log_{10}(2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}) = -20\,\log_{10}(10^{-3})$$

$$\text{CMRR} = -20 \times (-3) = 60\,\text{dB}$$

$$\boxed{\text{CMRR} \approx 60\,\text{dB}}$$

**Note:** This 60 dB CMRR is *worse* than the open-loop CMRR of a modern INA chip (typically 100+ dB) because resistor matching of discrete components limits the practical CMRR. This is why **monolithic INA chips** (e.g., AD620, INA128) use **laser-trimmed on-chip resistors**.

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]`
**(a) Explain the working of a three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier with a block diagram. Derive its overall gain expression.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) With reference to a Driven-Right-Leg (DRL) circuit, explain how it reduces common-mode interference in an ECG amplifier. An INA has CMRR = 90 dB and the DRL provides an additional 30 dB common-mode reduction. Calculate the total system CMRR.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B-B (a)

**Step 1 — Describe the topology** (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):
- Stage 1: Two op-amps A1, A2 in non-inverting configuration, sharing $R_G$ between their inverting inputs.
- Stage 2: Difference amplifier A3 with four matched resistors.

**Step 2 — Apply virtual short:**
$$V_a = V_1, \quad V_b = V_2$$

**Step 3 — Current through $R_G$:**
$$I_G = \dfrac{V_2 - V_1}{R_G}$$

**Step 4 — Output voltages of A1 and A2:**
$$V_{o1} = V_1 - I_G R_1, \quad V_{o2} = V_2 + I_G R_1$$

**Step 5 — Difference:**
$$V_{o2} - V_{o1} = (V_2 - V_1)\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply difference amplifier A3 (matched resistors):**
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\,(V_{o2} - V_{o1}) = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right)(V_2 - V_1)$$

**Step 7 — Final gain expression:**
$$\boxed{A_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right)}$$

**(Marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for derivation, 1 for final expression, 1 for comment on gain control.)**

**Key feature:** The gain can be varied by changing *only* $R_G$, without disturbing the matched resistor ratio $R_3/R_2$ — this preserves high CMRR.

---

#### Model Solution for B-B (b)

**DRL working principle:**
- The common-mode voltage $V_{cm}$ is sensed by *averaging* the two electrode signals.
- This is inverted and amplified by an auxiliary op-amp (A4) and applied to the patient through a third electrode.
- Negative feedback forces the patient's body potential to follow the amplifier's common-mode reference, *actively cancelling* mains pickup at the source.

**Step 1 — Convert CMRRs to linear units:**
$$\text{CMRR}_{INA} = 90 \text{ dB} \;\Rightarrow\; 10^{90/20} = 10^{4.5} \approx 31{,}623$$
$$\text{CMRR}_{DRL} = 30 \text{ dB} \;\Rightarrow\; 10^{30/20} = 10^{1.5} \approx 31.62$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 1/x addition rule for series stages:**
$$\dfrac{1}{\text{CMRR}_{tot}} = \dfrac{1}{31{,}623} + \dfrac{1}{31.62} \approx 3.16 \times 10^{-5} + 3.16 \times 10^{-2} \approx 3.163 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute total CMRR in linear units:**
$$\text{CMRR}_{tot} \approx \dfrac{1}{0.03163} \approx 31.61$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to dB:**
$$\text{CMRR}_{tot}^{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(31.61) \approx 30 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total System CMRR} \approx 30 \text{ dB}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **The "worse stage dominates" trap:** Many students *add* the dB values (90 + 30 = 120 dB) — this is **wrong**. The *inverse* sum rule must be used.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** A few students convert dB to linear using $10^{dB/10}$ (which is for *power* ratios). For voltage ratios, the correct factor is $10^{dB/20}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the DRL's role:** The DRL improves the **system-level** common-mode performance, but it *cannot* rescue a poor INA. Always identify the bottleneck stage.
> 4. **Sign errors in INA derivation:** $V_{o2} - V_{o1}$ sign must be consistent. If the difference amplifier A3 inverts, the sign of $V_{out}$ flips — note this when computing gain polarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 — Multiple-Input Bioelectric Amplifiers.**

- The **basic difference amplifier** has a low input impedance and requires a *matched resistor ratio* $R_2/R_1 = R_4/R_3$ for ideal behavior.
- The **instrumentation amplifier (INA)** uses three op-amps and a *single* external resistor $R_G$ for gain control, preserving CMRR.
- **INA gain formula:** $A_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_G}\right)\!\left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}\right)$.
- **CMRR definition:** $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_d \vert / \vert A_{cm} \vert)$ dB.
- **CMRR due to mismatch:** $\text{CMRR} \approx -20 \log_{10}(2\epsilon)$ dB, where $\epsilon$ is the fractional resistor mismatch.
- **DRL circuit** is an *active* common-mode negative feedback loop that returns common-mode noise to the patient body through a third electrode.
- **System CMRR rule:** When stages are in series, use $\dfrac{1}{\text{CMRR}_{tot}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{CMRR}_1} + \dfrac{1}{\text{CMRR}_2}$ (in linear units).
- The **worse CMRR stage dominates** the overall system CMRR.
- **Input-referred noise** of an INA is dominated by the *first stage* op-amps; their voltage noise density $e_n$ and current noise $i_n$ must be selected carefully.
- **Body temperature** for noise calculations is **310 K**, not 300 K.
- **ECG signal amplitude** is **0.5–5 mV**; the front-end must handle this with a gain of **500 to 2000**.
- **EEG** requires gain of **~20,000** and bandwidth **0.1–100 Hz**; uses chopper-stabilized INAs.
- The **lower cutoff frequency** (typically 0.05 Hz) is set by AC coupling to block electrode DC offsets up to **±300 mV**.
- The **upper cutoff frequency** (150 Hz for ECG, 100 Hz for EEG) prevents RF and muscle-noise aliasing.
- **Patient safety** mandates **galvanic isolation** — either through transformer isolation, optocoupler isolation, or capacitively-coupled isolation amplifiers (e.g., AD215).
- **Modern INA chips** (AD620, INA128, INA333) integrate all three op-amps and laser-trimmed resistors for **100+ dB CMRR** in a single 8-pin package.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Processing Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Signal Processing Circuits # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Signal Processing Circuits** in biomedical electronics are analog and mixed-signal electronic networks that condition, filter, amplify, and transform physiological signals (such as ECG, EEG, EMG, and PPG) into a form suitable for further analysis, digitization, or display. These circuits are the *backbone* of any biomedical instrumentation system, sitting between the bioelectrode/transducer front-end and the analog-to-digital converter (ADC) or recording device. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PECAT747 Module 2)**, signal processing circuits encompass: 1. **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) based building blocks** – Instrumentation amplifiers, active filters, precision rectifiers, comparators, integrators, and differentiators. 2. **Active filter design** – Low-pass (LPF), High-pass (HPF), Band-pass (BPF), and Band-stop (BSF / Notch) filters using op-amps. 3. **Signal conditioning stages** – Level shifting, impedance matching, isolation, and linear amplification. 4. **Specialized biomedical processors** – Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuits, peak detectors, and averaging circuits for noise reduction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Module 2 of PECAT747 explicitly focuses on the *analog* signal processing chain — **NOT** digital DSP. Examiners will test op-amp based topologies, transfer function derivations, frequency response (Bode plots), and biomedical applications (e.g., notch filter for 50 Hz power-line interference rejection in ECG). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a biomedical signal processing circuit as a **hospital's quality-control department for water**. - The *raw water from a river* = noisy, weak physiological signal (e.g., ECG of 1 mV amplitude buried in 50 Hz mains hum and muscle noise). - The *coarse sand filter* = **passive filtering** (R-C low-pass at the input). - The *chemical purifier* = **active filter / instrumentation amplifier** (selectively removes specific contaminants — 50 Hz noise — and boosts the desired signal). - The *UV sterilization unit* = **isolation amplifier** (protects the patient from the recording device). - The *cleaned, bottled water* = the conditioned signal sent to the ADC/computer. Just as a patient cannot drink unfiltered river water, an ADC cannot reliably digitize a raw bioelectric signal — it must be processed first. > [!NOTE] > **Core Metrics to Memorize:** > - **Gain (A):** dimensionless ratio, often expressed in dB → $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A)$ > - **Cut-off Frequency (f_c):** frequency at which output power drops to half (−3 dB point) > - **Quality Factor (Q):** sharpness of a band-pass/band-stop filter → $Q = f_0 / BW$ > - **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** ability to reject signals common to both inputs → typically **> 80 dB** for biomedical instrumentation amplifiers > - **Input Impedance:** should exceed **10 MΩ** to avoid loading the high-impedance bioelectrode source # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Amplifier as the Universal Building Block An **Ideal Op-Amp** has the following properties (often tested in KTU Part A): - Infinite open-loop gain ($A_{OL} \to \infty$) - Infinite input impedance ($Z_{in} \to \infty$) - Zero output impedance ($Z_{out} = 0$) - Infinite CMRR - Infinite bandwidth - Zero offset voltage and bias current **Practical Op-Amp (e.g., µA741, OP07, AD620, INA128):** Used extensively in biomedical circuits. The **AD620 / INA128** is a classic *integrated instrumentation amplifier* with CMRR **> 100 dB** at DC. ## 2.2 The Three Core Configurations | Configuration | Transfer Function | Key Use in Biomedical | |---|---|---| | **Inverting Amplifier** | $A_v = -R_f / R_{in}$ | Phase-inverting pre-amplifier | | **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_{in}$ | Buffer / high-input-impedance stage | | **Differential Amplifier** | $A_v = R_f/R_{in}$ (when matched) | Single-op-amp difference amplifier (limited CMRR) | > [!NOTE] > **Why an Instrumentation Amplifier (IA) is preferred over a simple difference amplifier in biomedical systems:** A discrete difference amplifier has CMRR that **degrades** when source impedances are mismatched. An IA uses *three op-amps* with symmetric input buffers, achieving high CMRR **independent of source impedance** — critical when bioelectrodes have skin-contact impedances that vary (typically 5 kΩ – 50 kΩ per electrode). ## 2.3 Instrumentation Amplifier (Three-Op-Amp Topology) — Working Logic **Step-by-step operation:** 1. **Input buffer stage (A1 & A2):** Two non-inverting op-amps share a gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between their inverting inputs. They provide high input impedance to the bioelectrodes. 2. **Difference stage (A3):** A standard difference amplifier with unity gain (typically $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$) subtracts the two buffered signals and rejects common-mode noise. 3. **Overall differential gain** is set by a single external resistor $R_G$, allowing the user to adjust gain without breaking symmetry. ## 2.4 Active Filters — First and Second Order ### 2.4.1 First-Order Low-Pass Filter (LPF) - **Topology:** Non-inverting op-amp with R-C network at input. - **Transfer function:** $H(s) = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + sRC}$ - **Cut-off frequency:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ - **Roll-off:** −20 dB/decade above $f_c$ ### 2.4.2 First-Order High-Pass Filter (HPF) - **Topology:** C-R network at input of non-inverting op-amp. - **Transfer function:** $H(s) = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot sRC}{1 + sRC}$ - **Cut-off frequency:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ - **Roll-off:** +20 dB/decade below $f_c$ ### 2.4.3 Second-Order Active Filters (Sallen-Key Topology) The Sallen-Key topology uses a single op-amp with positive feedback and is the most commonly asked second-order filter in KTU exams. **Standard second-order transfer function:** $$H(s) = \dfrac{K \cdot \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \left(\dfrac{\omega_0}{Q}\right)s + \omega_0^2}$$ Where: - $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ — natural (resonant) frequency in rad/s - $Q$ — quality factor (defines the filter response: Butterworth Q = 0.707, Chebyshev Q > 0.707, Bessel Q < 0.707) - $K$ — DC gain of the filter ### 2.4.4 The 50 Hz Notch Filter (Twin-T) - **Purpose:** Reject 50 Hz power-line interference from ECG/EEG. - **Topology:** Twin-T network (two T-networks in parallel: one R-C-R, one C-R-C) followed by a non-inverting op-amp. - **Notch frequency:** $f_N = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ - **Notch depth:** Approaches ∞ with perfect component matching; typically 30–50 dB in practice. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Biomedical Notch Filter Application:** ECG signals lie in the 0.05 Hz – 150 Hz range. The 50 Hz mains hum falls *inside* this band, so a band-stop (notch) filter centered at **f_N = 50 Hz** (or 60 Hz for US systems) is mandatory in clinical ECG machines. ## 2.5 Precision Rectifier (Super Diode) - **Problem with ordinary diode rectifier:** Forward voltage drop of ~0.6 V makes rectification of small bioelectric signals (< 10 mV) impossible. - **Solution:** Place the diode inside the op-amp feedback loop. The op-amp's high open-loop gain forces the diode to conduct as if it had **zero forward drop**. - **Use:** Detecting the peak of an EMG burst, half-wave/full-wave rectification of bioelectric signals for envelope detection. ## 2.6 Integrator and Differentiator | Circuit | Output | Biomedical Application | |---|---|---| | **Op-amp Integrator** | $v_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\int v_{in}(t)\,dt$ | Generating ramp signals for pacing; averaging noisy signals | | **Op-amp Differentiator** | $v_{out}(t) = -RC\dfrac{d v_{in}(t)}{dt}$ | Edge detection in QRS complex of ECG | ## 2.7 Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit - **Function:** Samples an analog input at a precise instant and *holds* that voltage steady for the ADC conversion time. - **Components:** Input buffer (op-amp) → analog switch (FET/JFET) → hold capacitor $C_H$ → output buffer (high-input-impedance op-amp to prevent $C_H$ discharge). - **Key parameters:** Acquisition time, aperture time, droop rate $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$, and hold-mode feedthrough. ## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Inverting amplifier gain | $A_v = -R_f / R_{in}$ | dimensionless | Inverting pre-amp | | 2 | Non-inverting amplifier gain | $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_{in}$ | dimensionless | Buffer/amp | | 3 | Instrumentation amplifier gain | $A_v = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | dimensionless | ECG/EEG front-end | | 4 | CMRR (in dB) | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | dB | Noise rejection | | 5 | 1st-order LPF cut-off | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | Low-pass design | | 6 | 1st-order HPF cut-off | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | High-pass design | | 7 | Sallen-Key natural frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | rad/s | 2nd-order filter | | 8 | Quality factor (Sallen-Key) | $Q = \dfrac{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}{(R_1 C_2 + R_2 C_2) \cdot (1-K)}$ | dimensionless | Selectivity | | 9 | Twin-T notch frequency | $f_N = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | 50 Hz removal | | 10 | Quality factor of BPF | $Q = f_0 / \Delta f$ | dimensionless | Bandwidth | | 11 | Integrator output | $v_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\int v_{in}\,dt$ | V | Integration | | 12 | Differentiator output | $v_{out} = -RC\dfrac{d v_{in}}{dt}$ | V | Differentiation | | 13 | Decibel conversion | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(A)$ | dB | Gain in dB | | 14 | Roll-off (2nd order) | −40 dB/decade | dB/dec | Steepness | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world engineering utility:** A modern patient monitor (e.g., Mindray, Philips IntelliVue) contains **all** these blocks in cascade: bioelectrode → IA → LPF (anti-alias) → HPF (DC block) → 50 Hz notch → S/H → ADC. Understanding each block is essential for biomedical engineers in R&D, calibration, and troubleshooting roles. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Gain of the Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier **Given:** Standard three-op-amp IA with input buffer stage (A1, A2) sharing $R_G$, followed by unity-gain difference stage (A3) with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$. **Step 1 — Define inputs and output of input stage:** Let the two bioelectric inputs be $v_1$ and $v_2$, applied to the non-inverting terminals of A1 and A2 respectively. **Step 2 — Apply the virtual-short concept:** Because of negative feedback and infinite open-loop gain, the inverting inputs of A1 and A2 are at the same potential as the non-inverting inputs: $$v_{-}(A1) = v_1 \quad \text{and} \quad v_{-}(A2) = v_2$$ **Step 3 — Voltage across the gain-setting resistor $R_G$:** Since both ends of $R_G$ sit at $v_1$ and $v_2$ (via the virtual short), the current through $R_G$ is: $$i_{R_G} = \dfrac{v_1 - v_2}{R_G}$$ This same current must flow through the feedback resistors $R$ of A1 and A2 (by KCL at the inverting nodes, since the op-amp input draws no current). **Step 4 — Output voltages of the input stage:** The output of A1 is one feedback-R drop *above* its inverting input: $$v_{o1} = v_1 + i_{R_G} \cdot R = v_1 + \dfrac{(v_1 - v_2)R}{R_G}$$ Similarly: $$v_{o2} = v_2 - i_{R_G} \cdot R = v_2 - \dfrac{(v_1 - v_2)R}{R_G}$$ **Step 5 — Differential gain of the input stage:** $$v_{o1} - v_{o2} = (v_1 - v_2) + \dfrac{2R(v_1 - v_2)}{R_G} = (v_1 - v_2)\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Output of the difference stage A3:** The difference amplifier with $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$ (matched) has unity differential gain: $$v_{out} = v_{o2} - v_{o1} = (v_2 - v_1)\!\left(1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}\right)$$ (Or reversed polarity depending on A3 wiring — the sign is conventional.) **Step 7 — Final gain expression:** $$\boxed{\,A_v = 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** Notice how $R_G$ is the *only* resistor the user needs to change to vary gain. This is why commercial IAs (AD620, INA128) expose a single "RG" pin. For AD620: $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{49.4\,\text{k}\Omega}{R_G}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Cut-off Frequency of a First-Order Active Low-Pass Filter **Given:** Non-inverting op-amp with input R-C low-pass network ($R$ in series, $C$ to ground), feedback resistors $R_f$ and $R_{in}$ setting DC gain $A_0 = 1 + R_f/R_{in}$. **Step 1 — Voltage divider at input:** The input to the non-inverting terminal of the op-amp is: $$v_{+} = v_{in} \cdot \dfrac{1/(sC)}{R + 1/(sC)} = \dfrac{v_{in}}{1 + sRC}$$ **Step 2 — Apply non-inverting gain:** $$v_{out} = A_0 \cdot v_{+} = A_0 \cdot \dfrac{v_{in}}{1 + sRC}$$ **Step 3 — Transfer function:** $$H(s) = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + sRC}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ and find the −3 dB frequency:** The magnitude is: $$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$ The −3 dB point occurs when $\omega RC = 1$: $$\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Asymptotic behavior:** - For $\omega \ll \omega_c$: $\vert H \vert \to A_0$ (passband, gain = DC gain). - For $\omega \gg \omega_c$: $\vert H \vert \to A_0 / (\omega RC)$, which falls at −20 dB/decade. > [!NOTE] > **Why the op-amp is essential here:** A pure R-C low-pass can be built with just one resistor and one capacitor, but it has the problem of *loading*. The op-amp provides a high input impedance (so the R-C network is not loaded) and a low output impedance (so the load doesn't affect the filter response). This is the key advantage of *active* over *passive* filters. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Notch Frequency of the Twin-T Filter **Given:** Twin-T network with two T-branches: - T1 (R-C-R): two series resistors $R$ and a shunt capacitor $2C$ to ground. - T2 (C-R-C): two series capacitors $C$ and a shunt resistor $R/2$ to ground. **Step 1 — Write KCL at the two output nodes:** Let the input be $v_{in}$, the output node be $v_{out}$, and the center node be $v_x$. Using admittance form: $$\dfrac{v_{in} - v_{out}}{R} + \dfrac{v_x - v_{out}}{R} + (2C)s(v_{in} - v_{out}) = 0$$ $$\dfrac{v_{in} - v_{out}}{1/(sC)} + \dfrac{v_x - v_{out}}{1/(sC)} + \dfrac{v_{out}}{R/2} = 0$$ **Step 2 — Solve the system:** After algebraic manipulation (assuming symmetric R and C), the transfer function simplifies to: $$H(s) = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 1}{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 4sRC + 1}$$ **Step 3 — Find the notch frequency:** At the notch, $\vert H(j\omega_N) \vert = 0$. The numerator is zero when: $$s^2 R^2 C^2 + 1 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad s^2 = -\dfrac{1}{R^2 C^2} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad s = \pm \dfrac{j}{RC}$$ Thus the imaginary frequency is $\omega_N = 1/RC$: $$\boxed{\,f_N = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}\,}$$ **Step 4 — Quality factor:** The Q-factor of the notch filter (selectivity) is: $$Q = \dfrac{1}{4(1 - K)} \quad \text{where } K \text{ is the gain of the op-amp stage.}$$ By choosing $K$ slightly less than 1, the notch becomes very deep and narrow — a property used in clinical ECG machines to surgically remove 50 Hz hum. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Sample-and-Hold Droop Rate **Given:** Hold capacitor $C_H$ with leakage current $I_{leak}$ during hold mode. **Step 1 — Capacitor discharge equation:** $$i_C = C_H \dfrac{dV}{dt} = -I_{leak}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for the droop rate:** $$\boxed{\,\dfrac{dV}{dt} = -\dfrac{I_{leak}}{C_H}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical tip:** For a 12-bit ADC with 10 V full-scale (LSB = 2.44 mV), and a 100 µs conversion time, the droop must be $< 0.5$ LSB. With $I_{leak} = 1$ nA, we need $C_H \geq \dfrac{I_{leak} \cdot t}{0.5\,LSB} = \dfrac{10^{-9} \cdot 10^{-4}}{1.22 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 82\,\text{pF}$. In practice, $C_H = 1$ nF or more is chosen to give margin. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete Biomedical Signal Processing Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[BIOELECTRODE
ECG EEG EMG] --> B[INPUT BUFFER
High Z op-amp] B --> C[INSTRUMENTATION
AMPLIFIER IA] C --> D[GAIN STAGE
Adjustable A_v] D --> E[LOW PASS FILTER
LPF anti-alias] E --> F[HIGH PASS FILTER
HPF DC block] F --> G[50 Hz NOTCH
BSF] G --> H[SAMPLE AND HOLD
S/H circuit] H --> I[ADC
12 to 16 bit] I --> J[DISPLAY OR PC
Processing] K[ISOLATION
AMPLIFIER] -.Patient Safety.-> C ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The signal flows from left (raw bioelectric signal at the electrode) to right (digitized waveform on the display). The dashed line shows the isolation barrier that protects the patient from mains voltage faults. ## 4.2 Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier — Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB V1[v1 bioelectrode] -->|non-inv| A1[OP-AMP A1 buffer] V2[v2 bioelectrode] -->|non-inv| A2[OP-AMP A2 buffer] A1 -->|feedback R| A1 A2 -->|feedback R| A2 A1 --- RG[R_G gain resistor] RG --- A2 A1 -->|vo1| A3[OP-AMP A3 difference] A2 -->|vo2| A3 A3 -->|subtract and scale| VOUT[v_out conditioned signal] ``` ## 4.3 Sallen-Key Second-Order Low-Pass Filter — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR VIN[v_in] --> R1[R1] R1 --> N1[node X] N1 --> C1[C1 to ground] N1 --> R2[R2] R2 --> N2[node Y plus input] N2 --> C2[C2 feedback] N2 --> OPA[OP-AMP non-inv] OPA -->|feedback divider| N2 OPA --> VOUT[v_out filtered] OPA -->|negative feedback| N2 ``` ## 4.4 Sample-and-Hold Circuit — Functional Block ```mermaid flowchart LR A[INPUT OP-AMP BUFFER] -->|low Z drive| SW[ANALOG SWITCH FET] SW -->|controlled by| CTRL[Control Logic] SW --> CH[HOLD CAPACITOR C_H] CH --> OB[OUTPUT OP-AMP BUFFER] OB --> OUT[v_out held value to ADC] CH -.droop.-> D[(I_leak)] ``` ## 4.5 Active Filter Frequency Response Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PASS_FILTERS LPF[LOW PASS LPF: passes 0 to f_c] HPF[HIGH PASS HPF: passes f_c to infinity] end subgraph BAND_FILTERS BPF[BAND PASS BPF: passes f1 to f2] BSF[BAND STOP BSF NOTCH: rejects f1 to f2] end LPF --> APPS1[Biomedical use: anti-aliasing ECG EMG] HPF --> APPS2[Biomedical use: DC block baseline drift] BPF --> APPS3[Biomedical use: QRS detection 10 to 25 Hz] BSF --> APPS4[Biomedical use: 50 Hz power line removal] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define an Instrumentation Amplifier. List its two key advantages over a single-op-amp difference amplifier for biomedical applications.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Answer:** An instrumentation amplifier (IA) is a precision differential amplifier designed to amplify low-level bioelectric signals (e.g., ECG, EEG) in the presence of common-mode noise. It typically uses a three-op-amp topology consisting of two input buffer amplifiers followed by a unity-gain difference amplifier. **Two key advantages for biomedical use:** 1. **Very high input impedance** at both inputs (typically **> 10 GΩ**) — necessary because bioelectrodes have high source impedance (5–50 kΩ) that varies with skin contact. 2. **Very high CMRR** (typically **> 100 dB** at DC, e.g., AD620) — independent of the matching of the source impedances. A single-op-amp difference amplifier's CMRR degrades drastically when source impedances are unequal, which is common with skin-contact electrodes. ### **Question 2: Why is a 50 Hz notch filter mandatory in an ECG amplifier? Mention the typical filter topology used.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** ECG signals have a useful bandwidth of approximately 0.05 Hz to 150 Hz. The 50 Hz mains power-line frequency falls *within* this band and is picked up by the patient's body and electrode leads as common-mode interference, often swamping the 1 mV ECG waveform. A **50 Hz band-stop (notch) filter** is therefore inserted in the signal chain to surgically reject this interference while preserving the rest of the ECG spectrum. **Typical topology used:** The **Twin-T notch filter** (also called the parallel-T or wien-bridge notch), where two T-networks (one R-C-R and one C-R-C) are connected in parallel and followed by a non-inverting op-amp stage. The notch frequency is $f_N = 1/(2\pi RC)$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A: Design a Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier for ECG Application** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the overall voltage gain of a three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. Explain why this topology is preferred for amplifying ECG signals. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** For the ECG IA, the input buffer resistors are $R = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$ and the gain-setting resistor is $R_G = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$. The difference stage uses matched resistors of $10\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate the overall differential gain, the input impedance, and the output voltage when the inputs are $v_1 = 1.5\,\text{mV}$ and $v_2 = 0.5\,\text{mV}$. Also, calculate the CMRR (in dB) if the common-mode gain is measured as $0.001$. **\[7 Marks\]** #### **Model Solution (a):** **Derivation of overall gain:** Consider the three-op-amp IA. The first stage (A1, A2) acts as a high-impedance differential buffer. Using the virtual short concept, the inverting inputs of A1 and A2 are at $v_1$ and $v_2$ respectively. The current through $R_G$ is: $$i_{R_G} = \dfrac{v_1 - v_2}{R_G}$$ This same current flows through the feedback resistors $R$ of A1 and A2. Hence: $$v_{o1} = v_1 + i_{R_G} R = v_1 + \dfrac{(v_1 - v_2) R}{R_G}$$ $$v_{o2} = v_2 - i_{R_G} R = v_2 - \dfrac{(v_1 - v_2) R}{R_G}$$ Subtracting: $$v_{o1} - v_{o2} = (v_1 - v_2) \left(1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}\right)$$ The difference stage A3 (with matched resistors $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$) has unity differential gain, so: $$v_{out} = v_{o1} - v_{o2} = (v_1 - v_2) \left(1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\,A_v = 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G}\,}$$ **Why preferred for ECG:** 1. **High input impedance** at both inputs (no loading of high-impedance skin electrodes). 2. **High CMRR** independent of source impedance (rejects 50 Hz mains hum picked up by the body). 3. **Gain is variable by changing a single resistor $R_G$** — convenient for adjusting ECG gain (typically 100–1000) to bring the 1 mV ECG up to 0.1–1 V for the ADC. 4. **Low noise** and low offset — critical for detecting small Q, R, S, T features. #### **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Differential gain of input stage:** $$A_1 = 1 + \dfrac{2R}{R_G} = 1 + \dfrac{2 \times 25\,\text{k}\Omega}{5\,\text{k}\Omega} = 1 + 10 = 11$$ **Step 2 — Gain of difference stage:** The difference stage uses matched $10\,\text{k}\Omega$ resistors, so $A_2 = R_3/R_2 = 10/10 = 1$. **Step 3 — Overall differential gain:** $$A_d = A_1 \times A_2 = 11 \times 1 = 11$$ **Step 4 — Input impedance:** For the IA, the input impedance at $v_1$ and $v_2$ is essentially the op-amp's input impedance, which is typically $> 10\,\text{M}\Omega$ for a FET-input op-amp like AD620. For calculation purposes in KTU: $$Z_{in} \approx 10\,\text{M}\Omega \text{ (or higher)}$$ **Step 5 — Output voltage:** Differential input: $v_d = v_1 - v_2 = 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.0\,\text{mV}$ $$v_{out} = A_d \times v_d = 11 \times 1.0\,\text{mV} = \boxed{11\,\text{mV}}$$ **Step 6 — CMRR in dB:** $$CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{11}{0.001}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(11000) = 20 \times 4.041$$ $$CMRR = \boxed{80.83\,\text{dB}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question A):** > - **Most common mistake (–2 marks):** Forgetting the "+1" in the gain formula. The correct expression is $1 + 2R/R_G$, *not* $2R/R_G$ alone. > - **CMRR units (–1 mark):** Always quote CMRR in **dB**, not as a raw ratio. Marks are deducted if the numerical answer is not in dB. > - **Sign convention (–1 mark):** The output $v_{out}$ can be either $+(v_1 - v_2)$ or $-(v_1 - v_2)$ depending on the wiring of A3. Always state your assumption. --- ### **Question B: Design of a Twin-T Notch Filter for 50 Hz Mains Rejection** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a Twin-T notch filter using an op-amp. Derive the expression for the notch frequency and the quality factor. Explain its role in an ECG amplifier. **\[7 Marks\]** **(b)** Design a Twin-T notch filter for $f_N = 50\,\text{Hz}$ using capacitors of $C = 100\,\text{nF}$. Calculate the value of resistors required. If the desired Q-factor is 25, find the gain $K$ of the op-amp stage. Comment on the components' tolerance. **\[7 Marks\]** #### **Model Solution (a):** **Circuit:** The Twin-T filter consists of two parallel T-networks — one R-C-R (two series $R$, shunt $2C$) and one C-R-C (two series $C$, shunt $R/2$) — followed by a non-inverting op-amp stage with gain $K = 1 + R_f/R_{in}$. **Derivation of notch frequency:** Apply KCL at the two internal nodes of the Twin-T network. After algebraic simplification, the transfer function is: $$H(s) = \dfrac{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 1}{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 4sRC + 1}$$ For notch, set numerator = 0: $$s^2 R^2 C^2 + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \pm j / RC$$ Hence: $$\boxed{\,f_N = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}\,}$$ **Quality factor (with op-amp gain $K$):** $$Q = \dfrac{1}{4(1 - K)}$$ For $K \to 1$ (unity gain), $Q \to \infty$ → infinitely deep and narrow notch. **Role in ECG amplifier:** ECG signals (0.05–150 Hz) are contaminated by 50 Hz power-line interference coupled capacitively into the patient and cables. A 50 Hz notch filter surgically removes this interference without affecting the rest of the ECG band, enabling clean recording of the P-Q-R-S-T waveform. #### **Model Solution (b):** **Step 1 — Calculate the resistor value:** Given $f_N = 50\,\text{Hz}$ and $C = 100\,\text{nF}$: $$R = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_N C} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 50 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$R = \dfrac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-5}} = 31{,}831\,\Omega \approx \boxed{31.8\,\text{k}\Omega}$$ **Step 2 — Twin-T component values:** - Series resistors: $R = 31.8\,\text{k}\Omega$ - Shunt resistor: $R/2 = 15.9\,\text{k}\Omega$ - Series capacitors: $C = 100\,\text{nF}$ - Shunt capacitor: $2C = 200\,\text{nF}$ **Step 3 — Find the gain K for Q = 25:** $$Q = \dfrac{1}{4(1 - K)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - K = \dfrac{1}{4Q} = \dfrac{1}{100} = 0.01$$ $$K = 1 - 0.01 = \boxed{0.99}$$ This means the op-amp must be configured as a non-inverting amplifier with gain 0.99, which is impractical (gain < 1 not achievable with standard non-inverting topology). In practice, $K$ is set slightly less than 1 using a voltage divider in the feedback path, or a *modified Twin-T* is used. **Step 4 — Comment on component tolerance:** The notch frequency $f_N = 1/(2\pi RC)$ depends *directly* on $R$ and $C$. A 1% error in $R$ or $C$ shifts $f_N$ by 1%, meaning the notch may fall at 49.5 Hz or 50.5 Hz — insufficiently attenuating the 50 Hz interference. Therefore, components with **0.1% tolerance (or matched trimpot adjustment)** are mandatory in clinical ECG notch filters. Additionally, the Q-factor (notch depth) is highly sensitive to the *matching* of the two T-networks — a 1% mismatch between the two R-C branches can reduce notch depth from 60 dB to less than 20 dB. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):** > - **Forgetting 2C and R/2 (–2 marks):** In the Twin-T network, the shunt elements are *not* $R$ and $C$ — they are $R/2$ and $2C$. Marks are lost if students use $R$ and $C$ throughout. > - **Sign of K (–1 mark):** For a non-inverting op-amp, $K \geq 1$. The Twin-T formula uses a "lossy" positive feedback; if your derivation gives $K > 1$, recheck the sign convention. > - **Component tolerance (–1 mark):** Examiners expect a *comment* on practical issues. Failure to mention tolerance and trimming loses a mark in part (b). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Instrumentation Amplifier** is the workhorse of biomedical front-ends: gain $A_v = 1 + 2R/R_G$, high CMRR (> 100 dB in commercial IAs like AD620), high input impedance. - **Active filters** use op-amps to avoid loading; cut-off frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ for first-order stages; second-order Sallen-Key filters have natural frequency $\omega_0$ and quality factor $Q$. - **Twin-T notch filter** at $f_N = 1/(2\pi RC)$ is mandatory for **50 Hz (or 60 Hz)** power-line rejection in ECG/EEG; components must be precisely matched (≤ 0.1% tolerance). - **Precision rectifier** places the diode inside the op-amp feedback loop to overcome the 0.6 V forward drop, enabling rectification of millivolt-level bioelectric signals. - **Integrator and Differentiator** are op-amp circuits that perform mathematical operations on the input signal; used in pacemaker ramp generation and QRS edge detection respectively. - **Sample-and-Hold circuit** uses a hold capacitor $C_H$, analog switch, and buffer op-amp; droop rate $= I_{leak}/C_H$; essential for stable ADC conversion. - **Sallen-Key second-order filter**: Butterworth response requires $Q = 0.707$, Chebyshev $Q > 0.707$ (ripple), Bessel $Q < 0.707$ (linear phase). - **For Part A (3 marks)** in KTU 2024, expect short, definition-style questions on **IA, CMRR, active filter, notch filter, precision rectifier, S/H** — quote numerical values (e.g., CMRR > 100 dB, ECG bandwidth 0.05–150 Hz). - **For Part B (14 marks)**, expect either a filter design problem (with derivation of $f_c$ or $Q$) or an IA design (with gain calculation and CMRR in dB). Always show full algebraic steps, units, and final boxed answer. - **Common examiner traps:** forgetting "+1" in IA gain; giving CMRR as a raw ratio instead of dB; using $R$ and $C$ instead of $R/2$ and $2C$ in Twin-T; missing the unit Hz; not commenting on practical component tolerance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Word:** Signal processing circuits in biomedical electronics are not abstract theory — they are the *exact* circuits inside every patient monitor you will encounter in a hospital. Mastering the IA, active filter, and notch filter derivations is the single highest-yield effort in Module 2 of PECAT747.

topics.title: Practical Op-Amps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Practical Op-Amps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECAT747 – Biomedical Electronics**, the topic **Practical Op-Amps** refers to the study of the *non-ideal behavioural parameters, dynamic limitations, and real-world performance metrics* of operational amplifiers (op-amps) as they are actually manufactured, packaged, and deployed in biomedical instrumentation circuits. Unlike the *ideal op-amp* model taught at the introductory level (which assumes infinite input impedance, zero output impedance, infinite open-loop gain, infinite bandwidth, and zero noise), a **practical op-amp** is a finite-gain, bandwidth-limited, noise-generating, temperature-sensitive analogue integrated circuit whose behaviour deviates from ideality in measurable, quantifiable ways.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** *Practical Op-Amps* explicitly tests a student's ability to identify, quantify, and compensate for the *non-ideal parameters* of op-amps — namely **Input Bias Current ($I_B$), Input Offset Current ($I_{OS}$), Input Offset Voltage ($V_{OS}$), Slew Rate ($SR$), Common-Mode Rejection Ratio ($CMRR$), Power Supply Rejection Ratio ($PSRR$), Gain-Bandwidth Product ($GBP$), and Equivalent Input Noise Voltage ($e_n$)**. Mastery of these parameters is essential for designing reliable biomedical amplifiers such as **ECG pre-amplifiers, EEG front-ends, and pacemaker sense amplifiers**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Tap" Model

Imagine an *ideal* garden tap (faucet) as the **ideal op-amp**:
- You turn the handle a tiny amount → a perfectly proportional huge volume of water comes out (infinite gain).
- The tap itself never drips (zero offset).
- The tap responds to handle movement *instantaneously* at any speed (infinite bandwidth, infinite slew rate).
- Water temperature never affects flow rate (infinite PSRR / zero thermal drift).

A **practical op-amp** is like a *real, mass-produced* tap:
- You must turn the handle a *small but non-zero* amount before water flows at all → **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{OS}$)**.
- A slow, continuous drip occurs even when fully closed → **Input Bias Current ($I_B$)**.
- The drip rate from the hot side and cold side differ slightly → **Input Offset Current ($I_{OS}$)**.
- You can only yank the handle so fast before water flow lags behind → **Slew Rate (SR)**.
- The tap's behaviour changes on cold mornings versus hot afternoons → **Thermal Drift, PSRR**.
- The tap amplifies low-frequency whispers but muffles high-frequency shouts → **Finite Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)**.

For a biomedical engineer, these "drips and lags" are not trivial — in an ECG amplifier handling millivolt-level heart signals atop a half-volt DC electrode offset, even a few microvolts of $V_{OS}$ drift or a few nanoamps of $I_B$ can **swamp the cardiac signal** if not properly compensated.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Reference Benchmarks for a "Typical" General-Purpose Op-Amp (e.g., µA741, LM358, TL081 family):**
> - **Input Bias Current ($I_B$):** **10 nA to 100 nA** (BJT-input), **1 pA to 50 pA** (FET/JFET-input).
> - **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{OS}$):** **0.5 mV to 6 mV** (general purpose), **µV level** (precision types like OP-07, AD8552).
> - **Slew Rate (SR):** **0.3 V/µs** (µA741), **13 V/µs** (LF351), **>500 V/µs** (high-speed types like LM7171).
> - **Open-Loop DC Gain ($A_{OL}$):** **$10^5$ to $10^6$** (≈ 100 dB to 120 dB).
> - **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP):** **0.5 MHz to 1 MHz** (µA741), **3 MHz** (TL081), **>100 MHz** (wideband types).
> - **CMRR:** **70 dB to 120 dB**.
> - **PSRR:** **70 dB to 110 dB**.
> - **Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$):** **$10^6$ MΩ** (BJT), **$10^{12}$ Ω** (FET).
> - **Equivalent Input Noise Voltage ($e_n$):** **~20 nV/√Hz** at 1 kHz (low-noise op-amps like AD797: 0.9 nV/√Hz).

## 1.4 Why "Practical" Op-Amps Matter in Biomedical Electronics

Biomedical signals (ECG, EMG, EEG, EOG, blood pressure, body temperature, neural spikes) share four brutal characteristics that punish ideal-op-amp assumptions:

1. **Micro-amplitude range** — µV to mV scale, easily corrupted by µV-level offset drifts.
2. **Low frequency content** — 0.05 Hz to 1 kHz typically, demanding **high DC gain** but vulnerable to **1/f noise**.
3. **High source impedance from skin-electrode interface** — demands **high input impedance** (FET-input op-amps like TL072, AD8628).
4. **Hostile electrical environment** — 50 Hz mains interference, RF pickup, electrostatic discharge (ESD) — demands **high CMRR** and **PSRR**, plus shielding.

Thus, mastering practical op-amp parameters is **not academic** — it directly determines whether your instrumentation amplifier can resolve a 1 mV ECG complex in the presence of a 300 mV electrode half-cell potential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Major Non-Ideal Parameters — Structured Logical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Input Bias Current ($I_B$)

- **Definition:** The mean of the two DC currents flowing into the inverting ($I_B^-$) and non-inverting ($I_B^+$) input terminals of an op-amp, both of which must be supplied by the source to bias the input differential stage.
- **Mathematical Formulation:**
$$I_B = \frac{I_B^+ + I_B^-}{2}$$
- **Why it matters:** $I_B$ flows through the source resistance $R_S$, generating an unwanted offset voltage drop $V_{err} = I_B \cdot R_S$. For $I_B = 100\,\text{nA}$ and $R_S = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$, $V_{err} = 100\,\text{mV}$ — catastrophic for ECG amplification.
- **Compensation trick:** Add a **bias-current compensation resistor** $R_{comp} = R_1 \parallel R_f$ in the non-inverting input path so that the DC voltage drop at both inputs is matched.

### 2.1.2 Input Offset Current ($I_{OS}$)

- **Definition:** The *difference* between the two bias currents.
$$I_{OS} = \vert I_B^+ - I_B^- \vert$$
- **Why it matters:** $I_{OS}$ flows through *unmatched* source impedances, producing an irreducible offset:
$$V_{OS,\,I_{OS}} = I_{OS} \times \Delta R_S$$
- **Engineering implication:** Use **matched resistor networks** (e.g., 0.1% tolerance thin-film arrays) wherever possible.

### 2.1.3 Input Offset Voltage ($V_{OS}$)

- **Definition:** The differential DC voltage that must be applied between the two input terminals to force the output to **zero volts** when both inputs are grounded through equal source resistances.
- **Specifying conditions:** Always quoted at a specific temperature (typically 25 °C) and over a temperature range (e.g., $V_{OS}$ drift = µV/°C).
- **Total output offset** for a non-inverting amplifier of gain $G$:
$$V_{OUT,\,offset} = (1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}) \cdot V_{OS} + I_B \cdot R_{comp} \cdot (1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}) + \frac{I_{OS}}{2} \cdot R_f$$
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU high-yield result:** *The output offset voltage is amplified by the closed-loop noise gain, NOT the signal gain.* This is a classic KTU examiner trap question.

### 2.1.4 Slew Rate ($SR$)

- **Definition:** The maximum rate of change of the output voltage per unit time, measured under large-signal conditions.
$$SR = \left. \frac{dV_{OUT}}{dt} \right\vert_{max}$$
- **Units:** **V/µs**.
- **Causal origin:** Internal compensation capacitor $C_c$ cannot charge/discharge instantaneously because the input stage delivers finite current $I_{tail}$:
$$SR = \frac{I_{tail}}{C_c}$$
- **Full-power bandwidth relationship:**
$$f_{FPBW} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_{OUT,\,peak}}$$
- **Practical rule:** For an undistorted sine wave of peak amplitude $V_p$ at frequency $f$, we require $SR \geq 2\pi f V_p$. A 10 kHz, 5 V peak sine requires $SR \geq 0.314$ V/µs — already challenging the µA741 (SR = 0.3 V/µs).

### 2.1.5 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

- **Definition:** The ratio of differential-mode gain $A_d$ to common-mode gain $A_{cm}$, expressed in dB:
$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right)$$
- **Equivalent input error** due to a common-mode input voltage $V_{cm}$:
$$V_{err,\,CMRR} = \frac{V_{cm}}{10^{CMRR_{dB}/20}}$$
- **Example:** If $V_{cm} = 5$ V and CMRR = 80 dB, then $V_{err} = 5$ V / $10^4 = 0.5$ mV — significant when amplifying ECG.

### 2.1.6 Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)

- **Definition:** The ratio of the change in input offset voltage to the change in supply voltage that produced it:
$$PSRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left( \frac{\Delta V_{supply} / \Delta V_{OS}}{1} \right) \equiv 20\log_{10}\left( \frac{1}{S_{VDD}} \right)$$
- **Equivalent** to injecting a small supply-ripple voltage as a differential input error.
- **Mitigation:** Decouple supplies with **0.1 µF ceramic + 10 µF electrolytic** capacitors placed within **5 mm** of the supply pins.

### 2.1.7 Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) & Open-Loop Frequency Response

- **The op-amp's open-loop gain** rolls off at –20 dB/decade above a dominant pole $f_p$ (typically 5–10 Hz internally compensated):
$$A_{OL}(f) = \frac{A_{OL,DC}}{1 + j(f/f_p)}$$
- **GBP definition:** At the frequency $f_t$ where $\vert A_{OL} \vert = 1$ (unity-gain crossover), the product of gain and bandwidth is **constant**:
$$GBP = A_{CL} \times f_{-3dB,\,CL} = A_{OL,DC} \times f_p$$
- **Practical consequence:** A non-inverting amplifier with closed-loop gain $A_{CL} = 100$ built from an op-amp with GBP = 1 MHz has only $f_{-3dB} = 10$ kHz of usable bandwidth.

### 2.1.8 Equivalent Input Noise — Voltage ($e_n$) and Current ($i_n$)

- **Spectral density form:** Both $e_n$ (V/√Hz) and $i_n$ (A/√Hz) are functions of frequency; dominated by **1/f (flicker) noise** below the corner frequency $f_c$ and **thermal (Johnson) noise** above it.
- **Total RMS noise** over bandwidth $f_1$ to $f_2$:
$$V_{n,\,RMS} = \sqrt{\int_{f_1}^{f_2} \left(e_n^2(f) + (i_n(f) \cdot R_S)^2 + 4kT R_S\right) \, df}$$
- **Biomedical impact:** EEG signals (~10 µV RMS) compete directly with op-amp noise — the use of ultra-low-noise instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., INA128, AD620) is mandatory.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Typical Units | Boundary / Critical Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input Bias Current | $I_B = (I_B^+ + I_B^-)/2$ | A, nA | FET-input: pA; BJT: nA |
| 2 | Input Offset Current | $I_{OS} = \vert I_B^+ - I_B^- \vert$ | A | $\vert I_{OS} \vert < \vert I_B \vert$ |
| 3 | Input Offset Voltage | $V_{OS}$ (specified) | V, µV, mV | Drift: µV/°C |
| 4 | Output Offset Voltage (non-inv) | $V_{OUT,off} = V_{OS}(1 + R_f/R_1) + I_B R_{comp}(1 + R_f/R_1)$ | V | $R_{comp} = R_1 \parallel R_f$ |
| 5 | Slew Rate | $SR = I_{tail}/C_c$ | V/µs | Min SR for sine: $2\pi f V_p$ |
| 6 | Full-Power Bandwidth | $f_{FPBW} = SR / (2\pi V_{OUT,peak})$ | Hz | Defines large-signal limit |
| 7 | Open-Loop Gain (freq-dep) | $A_{OL}(f) = A_{OL,DC}/(1 + j f/f_p)$ | V/V | Single-pole model |
| 8 | GBP (constant) | $GBP = A_{CL} \times f_{-3dB,CL}$ | Hz | MHz typical |
| 9 | CMRR | $CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$ | dB | > 80 dB desired |
| 10 | CMRR-induced error | $V_{err} = V_{cm}/10^{CMRR_{dB}/20}$ | V | Directly adds to output |
| 11 | PSRR | $PSRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(\Delta V_{DD}/\Delta V_{OS})$ | dB | Supply sensitivity |
| 12 | Settling Time | $t_s \approx \frac{1}{2\pi f_{-3dB}} \ln(\% \text{tolerance})$ | s | Important for ADC drivers |
| 13 | Total Input-Referred Noise | $V_{n,RMS} = \sqrt{\int (e_n^2 + (i_n R_S)^2 + 4kTR_S) df}$ | V RMS | Use $k = 1.38\times 10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 14 | Bias-Comp. Resistor | $R_{comp} = R_1 \parallel R_f$ | Ω | Place at non-inverting input |
| 15 | Noise Gain (non-inv) | $G_N = 1 + R_f/R_1$ | V/V | Note: $G_N = $ signal gain |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECG/EEG front-ends:** Use **instrumentation amplifiers (INAs)** built from three op-amps — the input stage uses **precision FET-input op-amps** (e.g., AD8628) to achieve $I_B < 200$ pA and $V_{OS} < 1$ µV, with $CMRR > 100$ dB at DC.
- **Pacemaker sense amplifiers:** Must reject **T-wave oversensing** — requires **bandwidth-limited** amplifiers where slew rate and GBP must be deliberately *reduced* to avoid amplifying repolarisation transients.
- **Defibrillator protection circuits:** Op-amp outputs are protected by **gas-discharge tubes + resistor-capacitor networks** so that the ±5 V op-amp survives ±5 kV defibrillator pulses.
- **Neural recording arrays:** Use **multi-channel CMOS op-amps** with integrated chopping stabilizers to push $V_{OS}$ drift below 100 nV/°C.
- **Patient monitors (SpO₂, NIBP):** Pressure transducers produce mV-level outputs atop several volts common-mode — **CMRR > 100 dB** and **rail-to-rail input stages** are mandatory.
