```mermaid
        BRAIN["INNER SPHERE Brain Conductivity 0.33 S/m Radius 8 cm"]
        SKULL["MIDDLE SPHERE Skull Conductivity 0.0042 S/m Radius 8.5 cm"]
        SCALP["OUTER SPHERE Scalp Conductivity 0.33 S/m Radius 9.2 cm"]
        BRAIN --- SKULL
        SKULL --- SCALP
    end
    DIP["CORTICAL CURRENT DIPOLE"] --> BRAIN
    EL["SCALP ELECTRODE"] -.Records.-> SCALP
```

> **Physical Insight:** The skull's poor conductivity (~80× more resistive than brain/scalp) acts as a **low-pass spatial filter**, smearing and attenuating EEG signals — explaining why high-resolution EEG requires source localization algorithms (e.g., LORETA, sLORETA).

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Clinical EEG Interpretation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RAW["RAW EEG SIGNAL"] --> ART["ARTIFACT REMOVAL EOG EMG ECG"]
    ART --> FILT["FILTERING 0.5 to 70 Hz Notch 50 Hz"]
    FILT --> SPEC["SPECTRAL ANALYSIS FFT Wavelet"]
    SPEC --> FEAT["FEATURE EXTRACTION Band powers Hjorth ERP"]
    FEAT --> DEC["DECISION Epilepsy Sleep stage BCI command"]
```

> **Engineering Translation:** This pipeline transforms a raw physiological time-series into clinically actionable information.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define EEG. Mention the five frequency bands of EEG with their frequency ranges.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Electroencephalogram (EEG)** is the spontaneous, rhythmic bioelectric activity of the brain recorded from the scalp using surface electrodes, representing the summed post-synaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons.

| Band | Frequency Range |
|---|---|
| Delta (δ) | 0.5 – 4 Hz |
| Theta (θ) | 4 – 8 Hz |
| Alpha (α) | 8 – 13 Hz |
| Beta (β) | 13 – 30 Hz |
| Gamma (γ) | > 30 Hz |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why are pyramidal neurons responsible for the EEG signal rather than other cortical cell types?**

**Model Answer:**

Pyramidal neurons are responsible because:
1. They are arranged in **palisade (parallel) fashion**, perpendicular to the cortical surface.
2. They possess long, parallel **apical dendrites**.
3. Their **post-synaptic potentials** (not action potentials) are summable across many cells.
4. The synchronous activation of **~10⁴–10⁵ neurons** produces a measurable extracellular dipole, summed constructively due to parallel orientation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Design + Theory

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional stages of a **clinical EEG acquisition system**. Discuss the role of the **instrumentation amplifier** and the **notch filter**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **10–20 international electrode placement system**. Discuss the conventions for electrode naming and the differences between **bipolar and referential montages**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (a):

A clinical EEG acquisition system consists of the following functional stages:

1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)**: Transduce ionic currents at the scalp to electronic signals. Skin preparation with abrasive gel ensures impedance **< 5 kΩ**.

2. **Input Protection Circuit**: Uses **optical isolation** (typically 5 kV rating) to protect the subject from mains leakage currents per IEC 60601-1.

3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)**: Three-op-amp topology with
   - Differential gain ≈ **100**
   - **CMRR > 80 dB** at 50 Hz
   - Input impedance > **100 MΩ**
   - Critical for rejecting common-mode 50 Hz interference picked up by the body.

4. **Bandpass Filter**: High-pass at **0.5 Hz** (removes DC skin-electrode offset) and low-pass at **70 Hz** (anti-alias).

5. **Notch Filter**: A narrow band-reject filter at **50 Hz** (India) / **60 Hz** (USA) with Q ≈ 30 to remove powerline interference without affecting nearby EEG bands (alpha, low beta).

6. **Main Amplifier**: Additional gain up to 10,000 to bring 10–100 µV signals into the ADC range (0–5 V).

7. **ADC**: **16–24 bit** resolution, sampling rate **≥ 256 Hz**.

8. **DSP/Display**: Spectral analysis, artifact rejection, storage.

**Role of IA:** Rejects common-mode noise (especially 50 Hz mains pickup) due to high CMRR; provides initial low-noise amplification; offers high input impedance to avoid loading the electrode-skin interface.

**Role of Notch Filter:** Selectively removes the **50 Hz powerline interference** without distorting the surrounding EEG frequency content (notch Q-factor tuning ensures <3 dB attenuation at 49 Hz and 51 Hz).

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (b):

The **10–20 system** (Jasper, 1958) is the standardized method for placing scalp electrodes:

- **Reference points:** Nasion (Nz), Inion (Iz), Pre-auricular points (A1, A2).
- **Distances:** Measured as **10% and 20%** fractions of the Nasion-to-Inion and pre-auricular-to-pre-auricular lines.

**Naming Convention:**
- **Letter** = Lobe (Fp, F, T, C, P, O)
- **Number** = Position: **Odd = Left hemisphere**, **Even = Right hemisphere**, **z = Midline**.
- Example: **C3** = Central electrode, Left hemisphere; **Pz** = Parietal midline.

**Total:** 21 electrodes in standard clinical use; up to 256 in research.

**Montages:**

| Montage | Formula | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Bipolar** | Vᵢ = Eᵢ − Eᵢ₊₁ | Rejects common noise, highlights local gradients |
| **Referential** | Vᵢ = Eᵢ − Reference (A1+A2) | Absolute amplitude comparison |
| **Average Reference** | Vᵢ = Eᵢ − Mean(E) | Minimizes reference bias |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**

**(a)** Discuss the **biophysical origin** of the EEG signal. Derive the expression for the **scalp potential** produced by a current dipole inside a spherical volume conductor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **five brain wave bands** in detail with their frequency, amplitude, and clinical/physiological correlates. Mention how these rhythms change during **sleep, epilepsy, and anesthesia**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (a):

The EEG originates from the **summated post-synaptic potentials** (EPSPs and IPSPs) of **cortical pyramidal neurons**, *not* from action potentials.

**Why Pyramidal Neurons?**
1. Aligned perpendicular to cortical surface.
2. Long parallel apical dendrites.
3. ~10⁴–10⁵ synchronized neurons required for a scalp-detectable signal.

**Derivation of Scalp Potential:**

Under the quasi-static assumption (since $\lambda \gg$ head), Maxwell's equations reduce to:

$$
\nabla^{2}V = 0 \quad \text{(homogeneous regions)}
$$

For a current dipole $\vec{p}$ at $\vec{r}_0$, the inhomogeneous Poisson equation is:

$$
\nabla^{2}V = -\frac{1}{\sigma}\vec{p}\cdot\nabla\delta(\vec{r}-\vec{r}_0)
$$

Using the Green's function $G = \frac{1}{4\pi r}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(\vec{r}) &= \frac{1}{\sigma}\int G\,\vec{p}\cdot\nabla\delta\,d^{3}r_0 \\
&= \frac{1}{\sigma}\vec{p}\cdot\nabla G \\
&= \frac{1}{\sigma}\cdot\vec{p}\cdot\left(-\frac{\hat{r}}{4\pi r^{2}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,V = \frac{1}{4\pi\sigma}\cdot\frac{\vec{p}\cdot\hat{r}}{r^{2}}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** $V$ is maximum when the dipole is radially oriented (perpendicular to scalp); falls off as $1/r^{2}$; depends on tissue conductivity $\sigma$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (b):

**Five Brain Wave Bands:**

| Band | Frequency (Hz) | Amplitude (µV) | State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 | 100 – 200 | Deep slow-wave sleep, infancy |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 | 20 – 100 | Drowsiness, meditation, memory encoding |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 | 20 – 60 | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 | 2 – 20 | Active thinking, alertness, anxiety |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 | < 2 | Higher cognition, consciousness, perception |

**Changes During Sleep:** Waking (Beta) → Stage 1 (Theta) → Stages 2–3 (Sleep spindles + K-complexes) → Stage 4 (Delta dominant) → REM (low-voltage mixed Beta-like).

**Changes During Epilepsy:** 
- **Generalized seizures:** High-amplitude (>1 mV) spike-and-wave discharges (3 Hz).
- **Focal seizures:** Localized rhythmic spikes at seizure focus.

**Changes During Anesthesia:** Progressive slowing — Beta (awake) → Alpha (sedation) → Theta (light anesthesia) → Delta (deep anesthesia) → Burst suppression (very deep).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on "Brain Signals" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing EEG with ECG/EMG origins** — EEG comes from **post-synaptic potentials**, not action potentials. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Writing "Alpha waves occur during sleep"** — Alpha waves occur in **relaxed wakefulness with eyes closed**, NOT during sleep. Delta waves dominate deep sleep. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting the 50 Hz notch filter value** — India uses **50 Hz**, USA uses **60 Hz**. Writing 60 Hz in an Indian exam is a deduction. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Wrong electrode naming convention** — Even numbers = **Right hemisphere**, Odd = **Left**, z = midline. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping units in formulas** — Always write amplitude in **µV** (not mV), frequency in **Hz**, and time in **ms**. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Not mentioning CMRR specification** — The instrumentation amplifier must have **CMRR > 80 dB** at 50 Hz. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Study of Brain Signals"**

- **EEG** = Summed post-synaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons; amplitude **10–100 µV**; frequency **0.5–100 Hz**.
- **Bands:** Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30) Hz.
- **Mnemonic:** **"Doctors Treat All Brain Ghosts"** → D-T-A-B-G.
- **Alpha** = Relaxed, eyes closed, **occipital dominant** (mu rhythm = 8–12 Hz over motor cortex).
- **Delta** = Deep sleep, **highest amplitude** (~100–200 µV).
- **Gamma** = Conscious perception, binding problem, lowest amplitude.
- **10–20 System:** **21 electrodes** standardized by Jasper; **Odd = Left, Even = Right, z = Midline**.
- **Skull Conductivity:** **80× lower** than brain → major signal attenuation.
- **Skull-Brain-Scalp three-sphere model** with conductivities 0.0042 / 0.33 / 0.33 S/m.
- **Dipole Potential:** $V = \frac{1}{4\pi\sigma}\cdot\frac{\vec{p}\cdot\hat{r}}{r^{2}}$.
- **Instrumentation Amplifier:** CMRR **> 80 dB**, input Z **> 100 MΩ**.
- **Filters:** High-pass **0.5 Hz**, Low-pass **70 Hz**, Notch **50 Hz** (India).
- **Sampling Rate:** **≥ 200 Hz** (Nyquist), KTU uses **256 Hz**.
- **Electrode Impedance Requirement:** **< 5 kΩ** per electrode.
- **Montages:** Bipolar, Referential (linked-ears), Average reference.
- **Applications:** Epilepsy (spike-wave), Sleep staging, BCI (P300, motor imagery), Anesthesia depth (BIS).
- **Clinical EEG amplitude range:** 10–100 µV (vs ECG 1 mV, EMG 50 µV–5 mV).
- **Invasive ECoG:** ~80× larger amplitude (1–10 mV) due to bypassing skull attenuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Respiratory and Other Measurements - Spirometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Respiratory and Other Measurements - Spirometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Spirometer** is a precision biomedical instrument used to quantitatively measure the volume of air inspired and expired by the lungs as a function of time. It produces a graphical recording called a **Spirogram** (volume-time trace) or **Flow-Volume Loop** (flow-volume trace) from which clinically significant pulmonary parameters such as **Tidal Volume (TV)**, **Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV)**, **Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV)**, and **Vital Capacity (VC)** are derived.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECAT747 - Module 3):**
> *A spirometer is a device that measures the volume of air exhaled or inhaled over a period of time, used to assess pulmonary function. It operates on displacement, flow-sensing, or thermal principles.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you have a **balloon connected to a jar of water that is inverted**. Every time you blow into the balloon, it displaces water out of the jar into a measuring cylinder. The amount of water that overflows tells you exactly how much air you exhaled. This is the basic principle of a **water-sealed spirometer** — the patient's breath physically displaces a known fluid, and the volume of displaced fluid equals the volume of air breathed.

For a dry/floating system, think of a **gym bellows** (like a fireplace accordion). As you push the bellows, it compresses a spring and moves a pointer along a calibrated scale. A spirometer works similarly: a bell or bellows moves up/down as you breathe, and that mechanical motion is converted into an electrical signal by a **linear variable differential transformer (LVDT)** or a **potentiometer**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Constants):**
> - **Tidal Volume (TV):** **500 mL** (normal quiet breathing volume)
> - **Respiratory Rate (RR):** **12–20 breaths/min**
> - **Vital Capacity (VC):** ~**4.8 L** in healthy adult males
> - **Total Lung Capacity (TLC):** ~**6.0 L**
> - **ATPS to BTPS conversion factor:** Uses body temperature (**37 °C**), ambient pressure, and saturated with water vapor.

## 1.3 Clinical Significance

A spirometer is the cornerstone instrument of **Pulmonary Function Testing (PFT)**. It is used to:
- Diagnose **obstructive lung diseases** (Asthma, COPD, Emphysema)
- Diagnose **restrictive lung diseases** (Pulmonary fibrosis, Sarcoidosis)
- Pre-operative assessment of surgical fitness
- Monitoring progression of respiratory disease and response to bronchodilator therapy

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Spirometers

Spirometers are broadly classified based on the **sensing principle** used to measure the breathed air:

### A. Volume-Displacement Spirometers
These measure the **cumulative volume** of air directly.

1. **Water-Sealed Spirometer (Wet Spirometer)**
   - The patient breathes into a **bell immersed in water**.
   - The bell rises and falls as air enters/leaves.
   - Movement is recorded via a **kymograph** (rotating drum with pen).
   - *Example:* Collins Stead-Wells spirometer, Benedict-Roth spirometer.

2. **Bellows-Type Spirometer (Dry Spirometer)**
   - Uses a **folding bellows** (accordion-style) instead of water.
   - A **counterweight + spring** system returns the bellows to the original position.
   - Position is sensed by a **potentiometer** or **LVDT**.
   - *Example:* Vitalograph series.

3. **Wedge Spirometer**
   - Uses a **wedge-shaped bellows** that expands/contracts linearly.
   - Higher accuracy at low flow rates.
   - Used in **dry-rolling seal spirometers**.

### B. Flow-Sensing Spirometers
These measure **flow rate** and integrate it to get volume.

1. **Fleisch Pneumotachometer**
   - Based on **Poiseuille's Law** of laminar flow.
   - Flow passes through a **bundle of parallel capillary tubes** creating a known resistance.
   - **Differential pressure** across the resistance is proportional to flow.
   - Volume = ∫ Flow dt.

2. **Lilly-Type (Screen-Type) Pneumotachometer**
   - Uses a **fine mesh screen** instead of capillary tubes.
   - Linear over a wider flow range.

3. **Turbine Flow Meter (Wright's Respirometer)**
   - Airflow rotates a **lightweight vane/turbine**.
   - Rotations are counted optically to give volume.

4. **Hot-Wire Anemometer (Thermal Mass Flow Sensor)**
   - A **heated thermistor/bead** is cooled by airflow.
   - Cooling power required to maintain temperature is proportional to flow.
   - Used in modern handheld peak flow meters.

5. **Ultrasonic Flow Sensor**
   - Measures transit-time difference of ultrasonic pulses traveling **with** and **against** the flow.
   - No moving parts, highly accurate, used in modern digital spirometers.

## 2.2 Lung Volumes and Capacities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — 4 Volumes and 4 Capacities:**
> Capacities = Sum of two or more lung volumes.

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Value (Adult Male) | Definition |
|--------|-----------|---------------------------|------------|
| TV / V_T | Tidal Volume | **0.5 L** | Volume of air inspired/expired in normal quiet breathing |
| IRV | Inspiratory Reserve Volume | **3.0 L** | Additional air that can be inspired forcefully after a normal inspiration |
| ERV | Expiratory Reserve Volume | **1.0 L** | Additional air that can be expired forcefully after a normal expiration |
| RV | Residual Volume | **1.2 L** | Air remaining in lungs after maximum expiration |
| IC | Inspiratory Capacity | TV + IRV = **3.5 L** | Max air inspired from end of normal expiration |
| FRC | Functional Residual Capacity | ERV + RV = **2.2 L** | Air in lungs at end of normal expiration |
| VC | Vital Capacity | TV + IRV + ERV = **4.8 L** | Max air exhaled after max inspiration |
| TLC | Total Lung Capacity | TV + IRV + ERV + RV = **6.0 L** | Total volume the lungs can hold |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3:**

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $V = \int Q \, dt$ | Volume = integral of flow over time | L |
| $Q = \frac{\Delta P}{R}$ | Poiseuille flow (Pneumotachometer) | L/s |
| $\Delta P = \frac{8 \eta L Q}{\pi r^{4}}$ | Pressure drop across capillary tube (Poiseuille) | Pa |
| $V_{BTPS} = V_{ATPS} \times \frac{310}{273 + T_{amb}} \times \frac{P_{amb} - P_{H_2O}^{T_{amb}}}{P_{amb} - 47}$ | ATPS → BTPS conversion (Body conditions) | L |
| $FVC$ | Forced Vital Capacity (maximal forced expiration) | L |
| $FEV_1$ | Volume expired in 1st second of forced expiration | L |
| $\frac{FEV_1}{FVC}$ | Tiffeneau Index (Normal > **0.75–0.80**) | ratio |
| $PEF$ | Peak Expiratory Flow rate | L/min |
| $MVV = TV \times RR_{max}$ | Maximum Voluntary Ventilation (35 × 160 = 5600 L/min) | L/min |
| $V_D = V_T \times \frac{Pa_{CO_2} - Pe_{CO_2}}{Pa_{CO_2}}$ | Bohr's equation for dead space | mL |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tiffeneau Index Note:** A ratio < **0.70** indicates **obstructive disease** (e.g., COPD). A normal or increased ratio with reduced VC indicates **restrictive disease**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Working Principle of a Water-Sealed Spirometer

The classical water-sealed spirometer follows the **Archimedes' Principle** of buoyancy. The setup consists of a **hollow bell (lightweight, usually plastic or metal)** inverted over a **water reservoir**.

**Step 1: Patient Expiration**
When the patient expires, the exhaled air is channeled via a **mouthpiece and one-way valve** into the inverted bell. The displaced air pushes the bell **upward** in the water seal.

**Step 2: Volume Displacement Equation**
The volume of air entering the bell equals the change in volume of the bell's internal cavity:

$$
V_{air} = A \times \Delta h
$$

where $A$ = internal cross-sectional area of the bell (m²), and $\Delta h$ = vertical displacement of the bell (m).

**Step 3: Mechanical-to-Electrical Conversion**
The bell is connected to a **pen and pulley system**. As the bell moves, the pen traces a curve on a **kymograph** (rotating drum).

**Step 4: Modern Electronic Sensing**
In modern spirometers, the bell movement is sensed by a **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)**:

$$
V_{out} = k \times (N_1 - N_2) \times \Delta x
$$

where $N_1, N_2$ are induced EMFs in two secondary coils, $k$ is a constant, and $\Delta x$ is core displacement proportional to lung volume.

## 3.2 Derivation: ATPS to BTPS Conversion

Lung measurements are recorded at **Ambient Temperature Pressure Saturated (ATPS)** but clinically reported at **Body Temperature Pressure Saturated (BTPS = 37 °C = 310 K, body pressure, saturated with water vapor at 47 mmHg)**.

Using the **Ideal Gas Law** $PV = nRT$:

**Step 1:** At constant moles of gas, $n$ is constant:

$$
\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}
$$

**Step 2:** Rearranging for $V_2$ (the BTPS volume):

$$
V_{BTPS} = V_{ATPS} \times \frac{P_1}{P_2} \times \frac{T_2}{T_1}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting saturated water vapor pressures:
- $P_1$ = Ambient pressure − water vapor pressure at ambient temperature
- $P_2$ = Ambient pressure − 47 mmHg (water vapor at 37 °C)
- $T_1$ = Ambient temperature in Kelvin = 273 + $T_{amb}$
- $T_2$ = 310 K (body temperature)

$$
V_{BTPS} = V_{ATPS} \times \frac{310}{273 + T_{amb}} \times \frac{P_{amb} - P_{H_2O}^{T_{amb}}}{P_{amb} - 47}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: FEV_1/FVC Tiffeneau Index

**Step 1:** Patient performs **forced vital capacity maneuver** (maximal inspiration → maximal forced expiration).

**Step 2:** Two volumes are recorded:
- $FVC$ = total volume expired
- $FEV_1$ = volume expired in the first 1 second

**Step 3:** The Tiffeneau ratio is:

$$
Tiffeneau = \frac{FEV_1}{FVC}
$$

**Step 4:** Clinical interpretation:
- **Normal:** Tiffeneau $\geq 0.75$
- **Obstructive disease (Asthma, COPD):** Tiffeneau $< 0.70$ (airflow limited)
- **Restrictive disease (Fibrosis):** Tiffeneau $\geq 0.75$ but both FVC and FEV_1 reduced proportionally

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Spirometer Types

| Parameter | Water-Sealed | Bellows | Fleisch Pneumotach | Ultrasonic |
|-----------|--------------|---------|---------------------|------------|
| Sensing Principle | Volume displacement | Volume displacement | Flow (ΔP) | Flow (ultrasonic) |
| Accuracy | High | Moderate | High | Very High |
| Maintenance | High (water hygiene) | Low | Low | Very Low |
| Portability | No (large) | Moderate | Yes | Yes (handheld) |
| Resistance | Low | Low | Very Low | Negligible |
| Frequency Response | Poor (slow bell) | Moderate | High (>50 Hz) | High |
| KTU Exam Frequency | High (classic theory) | Moderate | High (Poiseuille-based) | Low (modern) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Modern Electronic Spirometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Mouthpiece] --> B[Flow / Pressure Sensor]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    D --> E[Microprocessor with Integration Algorithm]
    E --> F[Display LCD Screen]
    E --> G[Memory Storage]
    E --> H[Printer or PC Interface]
    B1[LVDT or Differential Pressure Transducer] -.supplies.-> B
    C1[Filter and Offset Adjustment] -.stage.-> C
    E1[Volume = Integral of Flow dt] -.algorithm.-> E
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Water-Sealed Spirometer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Patient Expires Air] --> V[One-Way Valve]
    V --> BL[Inverted Bell in Water Reservoir]
    BL --> P1[Pulley and Counterweight System]
    P1 --> PE[Pen Writing on Kymograph Drum]
    PE --> TR[Continuous Spirogram Trace Volume vs Time]
    P2[Modern LVDT Attached to Bell] -.electronic.-> P1
    P3[Kymograph Rotates at Constant Speed] -.drive.-> PE
```

## 4.3 Lung Volumes and Capacities Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TLC[Total Lung Capacity 6.0 L]
    TLC --> VC[Vital Capacity 4.8 L]
    TLC --> RV[Residual Volume 1.2 L]
    VC --> IRV[Inspiratory Reserve Volume 3.0 L]
    VC --> TV[Tidal Volume 0.5 L]
    VC --> ERV[Expiratory Reserve Volume 1.0 L]
    TV --> IC[Inspiratory Capacity 3.5 L]
    IC --> IRV2[IRV 3.0 L]
    IC --> TV2[TV 0.5 L]
    ERV --> FRC[Functional Residual Capacity 2.2 L]
    FRC --> ERV2[ERV 1.0 L]
    FRC --> RV2[RV 1.2 L]
```

## 4.4 Pneumotachometer Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Inhaled or Exhaled Air] --> R[Known Flow Resistance: Capillary Tube or Mesh Screen]
    R --> DP[Differential Pressure Transducer measures deltaP]
    DP --> AMP[Amplifier and Linearizer]
    AMP --> INT[Integrator Circuit yields Volume]
    INT --> OUT[Volume Display L or mL]
    F1[Flow Q = deltaP divided by R] -.relation.-> DP
    F2[Volume V = Integral of Q dt] -.integration.-> INT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3, Q1]**

**Q1.** Define spirometer. List any **four lung volumes and capacities** measured by a spirometer.

**Model Answer:**
A spirometer is a biomedical instrument that measures the volume of air inspired and expired by the lungs as a function of time, producing a graphical trace called a spirogram.
Four parameters: **Tidal Volume (TV)**, **Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV)**, **Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV)**, and **Vital Capacity (VC)**.

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 3, Q2]**

**Q2.** What is the **Tiffeneau Index**? Write its normal value and clinical significance.

**Model Answer:**
The Tiffeneau Index is the ratio $FEV_1 / FVC$, where $FEV_1$ is the forced expiratory volume in 1 second and $FVC$ is the forced vital capacity.
Normal value: $\geq 0.75$. A value below **0.70** indicates **obstructive lung disease** such as asthma or COPD.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Expected Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a water-sealed spirometer**. Discuss its merits and demerits. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

A water-sealed spirometer consists of a **lightweight hollow bell** suspended upside down over a **water chamber** with the open end submerged. The patient breathes through a **mouthpiece connected to a two-way valve**. Exhaled air enters the bell and pushes it up; the bell descends during inspiration.

**Working:**
1. The **bell displacement** is directly proportional to the volume of air breathed in or out: $V = A \times h$ where $A$ is the bell cross-section and $h$ is vertical movement.
2. The bell is connected via a **pulley-counterweight** system to a **pen writing on a rotating kymograph drum** — producing a continuous volume-time trace (spirogram).
3. In modern variants, the bell's movement is sensed by an **LVDT** whose output voltage is proportional to lung volume.

**Merits:**
- High accuracy, gold standard for static lung volumes
- Direct volume measurement (no integration needed)
- Reliable for slow, sustained breathing maneuvers

**Demerits:**
- Bulky, not portable
- Water hygiene and level maintenance required
- Limited frequency response (bell inertia)
- Temperature drift affects accuracy (needs BTPS correction)

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **Fleisch pneumotachometer**. Derive the relationship between pressure drop and flow rate. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The Fleisch pneumotachometer is a **flow-sensing device** that uses the principle of **Poiseuille's Law** for laminar flow through a bundle of fine capillary tubes.

**Construction:**
- A **bundle of parallel capillary tubes** (typically 100–400 tubes of ~1–2 mm diameter) acts as a known linear resistance.
- A **heated screen upstream** prevents moisture condensation.
- A **differential pressure transducer** measures pressure drop $\Delta P$ across the resistance.
- Volume is obtained by electronically integrating flow over time.

**Derivation using Poiseuille's Law:**
Poiseuille's equation for laminar flow through a cylindrical tube:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L}
$$

For a bundle of $N$ parallel tubes:

$$
Q_{total} = N \times \frac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \eta L} = \frac{\Delta P}{R_{flow}}
$$

where $R_{flow} = \dfrac{8 \eta L}{N \pi r^{4}}$ is the constant flow resistance.

Hence:

$$
\Delta P = R_{flow} \times Q
$$

The differential pressure is **linearly proportional to flow rate** within the laminar regime. The flow signal is then **integrated** to obtain volume:

$$
V = \int_{0}^{t} Q(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**(a)** Define the following lung volumes and capacities with normal adult male values: **TV, IRV, ERV, RV, VC, TLC**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Definition | Value |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| **Tidal Volume (TV)** | Volume of air inspired or expired in a normal quiet breath | **0.5 L** |
| **Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV)** | Max additional air that can be inspired after a normal inspiration | **3.0 L** |
| **Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV)** | Max additional air that can be expired after a normal expiration | **1.0 L** |
| **Residual Volume (RV)** | Air remaining in lungs after maximal forced expiration | **1.2 L** |
| **Vital Capacity (VC)** | Max volume of air that can be expired after maximal inspiration = TV + IRV + ERV | **4.8 L** |
| **Total Lung Capacity (TLC)** | Total volume of lungs after maximal inspiration = TV + IRV + ERV + RV | **6.0 L** |

**(b)** What is **PEF and MVV**? A patient has TV = 0.5 L and maximum respiratory rate of 40 breaths/min. Calculate MVV. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**PEF (Peak Expiratory Flow):** The maximum flow rate achieved during a forced expiration, measured in **L/min**. It is a key indicator of large airway obstruction (e.g., asthma).

**MVV (Maximum Voluntary Ventilation):** The maximum volume of air a person can breathe in 1 minute by voluntary hyperventilation. It is a measure of respiratory muscle endurance and airway resistance.

**Mathematically:**

$$
MVV = TV \times RR_{max}
$$

**Substituting given values:**

$$
MVV = 0.5 \, L \times 40 \, breaths/min = 20 \, L/min
$$

*(Note: A healthy adult typically achieves 150–200 L/min; the calculated low value indicates severe restrictive or obstructive disease.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse volumes with capacities** — Capacities are sums of two or more volumes and **cannot be measured directly by a simple spirometer** (e.g., RV, FRC, TLC require helium dilution or body plethysmography).
> - **Always mention BTPS correction** when reporting spirometry values — failure to do so costs 2 marks.
> - **Tiffeneau Index** must be written as a ratio (FEV_1/FVC), NOT just FEV_1.
> - In pneumotachometer derivations, students often forget the **integration step** (Volume = ∫Q dt) — this is a frequent 2-mark loss.
> - **Laminar flow assumption** in Poiseuille's Law is critical — the pneumotachometer is inaccurate at high (turbulent) flow rates.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Spirometer** = device to measure lung **volume** as a function of time → produces a **spirogram**.
- ☐ Two sensing classes: **Volume-Displacement** (wet, dry, wedge) vs. **Flow-Sensing** (Fleisch, turbine, ultrasonic, hot-wire).
- ☐ **Water-sealed** uses Archimedes' principle; **Fleisch** uses Poiseuille's law; **Ultrasonic** uses transit-time difference.
- ☐ **4 Volumes:** TV (0.5 L), IRV (3.0 L), ERV (1.0 L), RV (1.2 L).
- ☐ **4 Capacities:** IC = TV+IRV, FRC = ERV+RV, VC = TV+IRV+ERV, TLC = sum of all 4 volumes.
- ☐ **Tiffeneau Index** = $FEV_1 / FVC$ → normal ≥ 0.75; < 0.70 indicates obstruction.
- ☐ **ATPS → BTPS** conversion requires temperature factor (310/273+T) and water vapor pressure correction (47 mmHg at 37 °C).
- ☐ **MVV** = TV × $RR_{max}$ (typical: 150–200 L/min healthy adult).
- ☐ Pneumotachometer: $\Delta P = R_{flow} \times Q$, and $V = \int Q \, dt$.
- ☐ Spirometer CANNOT measure RV, FRC, TLC directly — needs helium dilution or body plethysmography.
- ☐ **Restrictive disease** = reduced VC, normal/high Tiffeneau; **Obstructive disease** = reduced Tiffeneau.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Deafness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Deafness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Deafness** (clinically termed *hearing impairment* or *hearing loss*) is defined as the partial or total inability to perceive sound in one or both ears, quantified by an elevation in the auditory threshold relative to the standardized **0 dB HL (Hearing Level)** reference, which is the average threshold of a young, otologically normal adult across standard audiometric frequencies (125 Hz – 8 kHz).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECAT747 – Module 3):**
> Measurement of Deafness is the clinical and electronic process of quantifying an individual's hearing sensitivity by determining the softest sound they can detect (the *threshold*) at various frequencies, and identifying the site of lesion along the auditory pathway (outer, middle, inner ear, auditory nerve, or central pathways).

The subject of **audiometry** encompasses all electrophysiological and psychoacoustic techniques used to evaluate auditory function. The principal instruments used are:

- **Pure Tone Audiometer** – Generates calibrated pure tones (sine waves) of known frequency and intensity to obtain the behavioural hearing threshold.
- **Impedance Audiometer (Tympanometer)** – Measures middle-ear compliance and acoustic reflex thresholds.
- **BERA / ABR System** – Records electrical responses from the auditory nerve and brainstem to acoustic stimuli.
- **OAE Analyzer** – Detects sounds emitted by the outer hair cells of the cochlea.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Eye Test for the Ears":**
> Imagine you are testing the eyesight of a patient. You ask them, *"Can you read this line of letters?"* and keep reducing the font size until they cannot read anymore. The smallest line they can read is their *visual threshold*.
> **Audiometry is exactly the same — but for ears.** The audiologist plays tones (beeps) at different *pitches (frequencies)* and *loudness levels (intensities)*. The patient presses a button the moment they hear the faintest possible sound. That faintest audible level is their *hearing threshold*. Plotting these thresholds on a graph (an **audiogram**) reveals *how much* and *what type* of hearing loss exists — much like a fingerprint of the ear's health.

## 1.3 Key Standardized Reference Values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Constants Used in Audiometry (must be memorized for KTU):**
> - **Reference Sound Pressure:** $P_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}$
> - **Reference Sound Intensity:** $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$
> - **Standard Audiometric Frequencies:** 250 Hz, 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, 4 kHz, 8 kHz
> - **0 dB HL Reference:** Threshold of an otologically normal young adult
> - **Threshold of Pain:** ~120–130 dB SPL
> - **Conversational Speech:** ~40–60 dB SPL
> - **Whisper:** ~20–30 dB SPL

## 1.4 Classification of Deafness

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Favourite Classification (3 Types):**
> 1. **Conductive Hearing Loss (CHL):** Defect in the *outer* or *middle* ear (e.g., wax, otitis media, ossicle damage). Sound fails to reach the inner ear efficiently. **Air–bone gap is present.**
> 2. **Sensorineural Hearing Loss (SNHL):** Defect in the *cochlea* (inner ear) or *auditory nerve* (e.g., presbycusis, noise-induced loss). **No air–bone gap.**
> 3. **Mixed Hearing Loss:** A combination of both conductive and sensorineural components.

## 1.5 Degree of Hearing Loss (WHO & KTU Standard)

| Degree of Loss | dB HL Range (Average of 0.5, 1, 2, 4 kHz) |
|----------------|------------------------------------------|
| Normal | –10 to 15 dB HL |
| Slight | 16 – 25 dB HL |
| Mild | 26 – 40 dB HL |
| Moderate | 41 – 55 dB HL |
| Moderately Severe | 56 – 70 dB HL |
| Severe | 71 – 90 dB HL |
| Profound | > 90 dB HL |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Decibel (dB) — The Fundamental Unit of Audiometry

Human ears respond to an enormous range of sound pressures — from the faintest whisper (~20 µPa) to the roar of a jet engine (~200 Pa) — spanning **seven orders of magnitude**. Because this range is too vast for a linear scale, audiologists use the **logarithmic decibel (dB) scale**.

### 2.1.1 Sound Pressure Level (SPL)
SPL is an *absolute* physical measurement expressed in dB relative to the standard reference pressure $P_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$.

$$\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) \;\; \text{dB SPL}$$

### 2.1.2 Sound Intensity Level (SIL)
SIL is expressed in dB relative to the standard reference intensity $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$.

$$\text{SIL} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \;\; \text{dB}$$

### 2.1.3 Hearing Level (HL)
HL is a *biologically calibrated* scale. Each audiometer adjusts SPL such that **0 dB HL = average normal threshold** at every frequency. This is implemented via frequency-dependent attenuator correction (ISO 8253-1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HL is used clinically:** A 30 dB HL tone at 1 kHz and 30 dB HL tone at 4 kHz feel equally loud to a normal listener, but the underlying SPL values are *different* (because the ear is more sensitive at 1–4 kHz). HL normalizes this.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD — Memorize this table verbatim:**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}(P / P_0)$ | dB SPL | Absolute acoustic measurement |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $\text{SIL} = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_0)$ | dB | Power-based acoustic |
| 3 | Intensity–Pressure Relation | $I \propto P^2$ | W/m² | Bridge between SIL and SPL |
| 4 | Power Ratio in dB | $\text{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{out}} / P_{\text{in}})$ | dB | Amplifier/electrical dB |
| 5 | Voltage Ratio in dB | $\text{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{out}} / V_{\text{in}})$ | dB | Voltage-based electrical dB |
| 6 | Pure Tone Average (PTA) | $\text{PTA} = (HL_{500} + HL_{1k} + HL_{2k} + HL_{4k})/4$ | dB HL | Quantifying degree of loss |
| 7 | Air–Bone Gap (ABG) | $\text{ABG} = HL_{\text{air}} - HL_{\text{bone}}$ | dB | Diagnosing conductive component |
| 8 | Octave Frequency Ratio | $f_2 = 2 \cdot f_1$ | Hz | Defines one octave |
| 9 | Loudness (Phon) | $L_N = 20 \log_{10}(P / P_{1\,\text{kHz}})$ | phon | Equal-loudness contours |
| 10 | SISI Index | $\text{SISI} = (N_{\text{positive}}/20) \times 100$ | % | Cochlear vs retrocochlear diagnosis |
| 11 | Acoustic Reflex Threshold | $500$–$4000$ Hz at 70–100 dB HL | dB HL | Middle-ear muscle reflex |
| 12 | OAE SNR Criterion | $\text{SNR} = L_{\text{OAE}} - L_{\text{noise floor}}$ | dB | Pass if SNR $\geq 6$ dB |

> **Critical Note on `|` symbol usage:** In the table above, fractions use `/` and ratios use parentheses — never the pipe `|` character — to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 The Pure Tone Audiometer — Block Diagram Theory

A clinical pure-tone audiometer is composed of the following sub-systems:

1. **Oscillator:** Generates pure sine tones at standard audiometric frequencies (125 Hz – 8 kHz, in octave or half-octave steps). Typically uses a phase-shift or direct-digital-synthesis (DDS) oscillator.
2. **Attenuator (dB HL dial):** Stepped logarithmic attenuator (typically 5 dB steps) calibrated to produce 0 dB HL at threshold reference.
3. **Output Selector:** Routes the tone to one of three transducers:
   - **Air-conduction headphones (TDH-39 / ER-3A insert phones):** Stimulus reaches cochlea via the outer and middle ear (full auditory pathway).
   - **Bone-conduction vibrator (B-71 placed on mastoid):** Bypasses outer/middle ear; stimulates cochlea directly — used to differentiate conductive from sensorineural loss.
   - **Free-field loudspeaker (optional):** Used for paediatric or aided testing.
4. **Masking Noise Generator:** Produces narrow-band or white noise to the *non-test ear* to prevent *cross-hearing* (when the test tone is loud enough to be heard in the opposite ear).
5. **Patient Response Button & Indicator:** Patient signals detection; the audiologist records the threshold using the *modified Hughson–Westlake method* (10 dB down, 5 dB up).

## 2.4 Masking — The Hidden Pitfall

> [!WARNING]
> When testing one ear with intensities above ~40 dB HL, the skull vibrates and the tone may "cross over" to the non-test ear (called **cross-hearing**). This produces a falsely good threshold. **Masking** presents narrow-band noise to the non-test ear at a level that shifts its threshold by 30–40 dB above the masking noise, isolating the test ear. KTU often asks the formula or conditions for masking.

## 2.5 Impedance Audiometry (Tympanometry) Theory

The middle ear is an *acoustic compliance* system. A tympanometer measures how much sound energy the eardrum reflects as air pressure in the ear canal is varied from +200 to –400 daPa (decapascals).

- **Compliance (Y):** Equivalent volume of air in cm³ (or mL); normal peak at 0 daPa with 0.3–1.6 mL compliance.
- **Type A curve:** Normal middle ear.
- **Type B curve:** Flat (no peak) — indicates fluid (otitis media) or perforation.
- **Type C curve:** Negative-pressure peak — eustachian tube dysfunction.
- **Type As / Ad:** Stiffened or hyper-compliant — ossicular fixation/discontinuity.

## 2.6 Auditory Brainstem Evoked Response Audiometry (BERA / ABR / BAER)

BERA records electrical activity from the auditory nerve and brainstem in response to click stimuli (~0.1 ms, 10–90 dB nHL). Surface electrodes pick up five major waves:

- **Wave I** — Auditory nerve
- **Wave II** — Cochlear nucleus
- **Wave III** — Superior olivary complex
- **Wave IV** — Lateral lemniscus
- **Wave V** — Inferior colliculus (most robust, used to estimate hearing threshold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical utility:** BERA is the **gold standard for objective audiometry**, especially in newborns, infants, and malingerers. Threshold = the lowest intensity at which Wave V is just identifiable.

## 2.7 Otoacoustic Emissions (OAE)

The healthy cochlea (specifically the *outer hair cells*) actively amplifies soft sounds and emits a portion of this energy back through the eardrum as a faint sound. A sensitive microphone in the ear canal detects this. Two types:
- **TEOAE (Transient Evoked):** Click stimulus; tests 1–4 kHz.
- **DPOAE (Distortion Product):** Two pure tones $f_1, f_2$ evoke distortion product at $2f_1 - f_2$.

**Pass criterion:** Emission amplitude ≥ 6 dB above noise floor.

## 2.8 SISI Test (Short Increment Sensitivity Index)

This test discriminates **cochlear** (recruitment-positive) from **retrocochlear** (8th nerve) lesions. The audiometer presents a sustained tone 20 dB above threshold, superimposing 1 dB increments 20 times. A patient with cochlear lesion detects most of them (SISI score 60–100%); retrocochlear patients miss most (score 0–20%).

$$\text{SISI} = \frac{\text{Number of 1-dB increments perceived}}{20} \times 100 \, \%$$

## 2.9 Tone Decay Test (Carhart)

A continuous tone is presented at threshold. The patient indicates when the tone becomes inaudible; the audiologist increases the level until the tone is sustained for 60 seconds. **Cochlear lesions:** decay < 20 dB. **Retrocochlear lesions:** rapid decay (often > 30 dB).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why SPL uses "20 log" and SIL uses "10 log"

**Starting point:** Sound intensity is proportional to the *square* of sound pressure (since intensity = power/area and power ∝ V² for an acoustic wave, and V ∝ P).

$$I \propto P^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{I}{I_0} = \left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^2$$

**Step 1 — Take the base-10 logarithm of both sides:**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)^2\right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the power rule of logarithms** $\log(a^b) = b \cdot \log(a)$:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = 2 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by 10** to obtain SIL in decibels:

$$\text{SIL} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = \text{SPL}$$

**Conclusion:** The two equations are *mathematically identical*. The "20" appears with pressure (because of the square-law relationship) and the "10" appears with intensity (because of the linear relationship). This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pure Tone Average (PTA) Numerical Example

**Problem:** A patient shows the following air-conduction thresholds (in dB HL) on the right ear:

| Frequency | 500 Hz | 1 kHz | 2 kHz | 4 kHz |
|-----------|--------|-------|-------|-------|
| Threshold (dB HL) | 35 | 40 | 50 | 45 |

**Step 1 — Apply the PTA formula:**

$$\text{PTA} = \frac{\text{HL}_{500} + \text{HL}_{1k} + \text{HL}_{2k} + \text{HL}_{4k}}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$\text{PTA} = \frac{35 + 40 + 50 + 45}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the numerators:**

$$\text{PTA} = \frac{170}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the quotient:**

$$\text{PTA} = 42.5 \; \text{dB HL}$$

**Step 5 — Classify the loss** using the WHO table from §1.5:
- 42.5 dB HL falls within **41 – 55 dB HL** → **Moderate hearing loss**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Air–Bone Gap and Differential Diagnosis

**Problem:** A patient presents the following audiometric thresholds on the left ear:

| Frequency | AC (dB HL) | BC (dB HL) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| 500 Hz | 50 | 15 |
| 1 kHz | 55 | 20 |
| 2 kHz | 60 | 25 |
| 4 kHz | 55 | 20 |

**Step 1 — Calculate the Air–Bone Gap (ABG) at each frequency:**

$$\text{ABG}(f) = \text{HL}_{\text{air}}(f) - \text{HL}_{\text{bone}}(f)$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate:**

| Frequency | AC | BC | ABG |
|-----------|----|----|-----|
| 500 Hz | 50 | 15 | 35 |
| 1 kHz | 55 | 20 | 35 |
| 2 kHz | 60 | 25 | 35 |
| 4 kHz | 55 | 20 | 35 |

**Step 3 — Interpret the ABG:**
- AC threshold is *significantly worse* than BC threshold.
- A **persistent ABG > 10 dB** across frequencies indicates a **conductive component**.
- Therefore, this patient has **Conductive (or Mixed) Hearing Loss**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** If both AC and BC are equally elevated, with ABG = 0, the diagnosis is **purely sensorineural**. If BC is normal (≤ 20 dB HL) but AC is elevated, the diagnosis is **purely conductive**. If BC is also elevated AND there is a gap, the diagnosis is **mixed**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — SISI Score Calculation

**Problem:** A patient is presented with 1-dB increments 20 times superimposed on a 20 dB SL sustained tone. They correctly detected 16 of the 20 increments.

**Step 1 — Apply the SISI formula:**

$$\text{SISI Score} = \frac{\text{Number of increments perceived}}{\text{Total increments presented}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\text{SISI Score} = \frac{16}{20} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$\text{SISI Score} = 0.8 \times 100 = 80 \, \%$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:**
- Score 60–100% → **Positive SISI** → Indicates **Cochlear (end-organ) pathology** (e.g., Ménière's disease, presbycusis).
- Score 0–20% → **Negative SISI** → Indicates **Retrocochlear (8th nerve) pathology** (e.g., acoustic neuroma).

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Intensity & Pressure Conversion

**Problem:** A sound has a measured SPL of 80 dB. Calculate (a) the actual pressure, and (b) the intensity ratio.

**Part (a) — Pressure:**

$$\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = 80$$

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = 4$$

$$\frac{P}{P_0} = 10^4 = 10{,}000$$

$$P = 10{,}000 \times 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^5 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 0.2 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Part (b) — Intensity ratio:**

$$I / I_0 = 10^{\text{SPL}/10} = 10^{80/10} = 10^8$$

**Interpretation:** A sound at 80 dB SPL is **10,000 times** the reference pressure and **100,000,000 times** the reference intensity — a vivid demonstration of the dynamic range of human hearing.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Test Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD — Use this when a KTU question asks "Which test for which condition?":**

| Clinical Scenario | Test of Choice | Why |
|-------------------|----------------|-----|
| Newborn hearing screening | OAE + BERA | Objective, no behavioural response required |
| Suspected otitis media (child) | Tympanometry | Detects middle-ear fluid, non-invasive |
| Differentiate CHL vs SNHL | Pure Tone Audiometry (AC + BC) | Reveals air–bone gap |
| Suspected acoustic neuroma | BERA + Tone Decay + SISI | Detects retrocochlear pathology |
| Malingering / functional loss | BERA (objective) | Cannot be voluntarily suppressed |
| Cochlear vs retrocochlear (confirmed SNHL) | SISI + Tone Decay | Differentiates site of lesion |
| Hearing aid fitting | Pure Tone Audiometry + Speech audiometry | Determines gain, output, and compression needs |
| Industrial NIHL screening | Pure Tone Audiometry (4 kHz dip) | Classic 4 kHz notch in noise-induced loss |
| Ototoxicity monitoring (e.g., gentamicin) | DPOAE + PTA | OAE changes precede PTA changes |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Clinical Pure Tone Audiometer

```mermaid
graph LR
    OSC["OSCILLATOR\nDDS Phase Shift\n125 Hz to 8 kHz"] --> ATT["STEP ATTENUATOR\n5 dB Steps\nRange minus 10 to 120 dB HL"]
    ATT --> SW["OUTPUT SELECTOR\nSWITCH"]
    SW --> AC["AIR CONDUCTION\nTDH 39 Headphones\nor ER 3A Insert"]
    SW --> BC["BONE CONDUCTION\nB 71 Vibrator\nMastoid Placement"]
    SW --> FF["FREE FIELD\nLoudspeaker\nOptional"]
    MASK["MASKING NOISE\nGENERATOR\nNarrow Band or White"] --> CONTR["MIXING AND\nCONTROL CIRCUIT"]
    AC --> CONTR
    BC --> CONTR
    FF --> CONTR
    CONTR --> PT["PATIENT\nRESPONSE BUTTON"]
    PT --> IND["RESPONSE\nINDICATOR LAMP\nand AUDIOLOGIST METER"]
    IND --> LOG["AUDIOMETRIC\nCHART RECORDER\nor DIGITAL LOG"]
    LOG --> AUD["AUDIOGRAM\nOUTPUT"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The oscillator generates the test tone → the attenuator adjusts the level in calibrated dB HL steps → the switch routes the tone to AC, BC, or free-field transducers → masking noise is mixed in when needed → the patient signals detection → the response is recorded on an audiogram.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — BERA / ABR System

```mermaid
graph LR
    STIM["CLICK STIMULUS\nGENERATOR\n100 us 10 to 90 dB nHL"] --> EP["EARPHONE\nINSERT or HEADPHONE"]
    EP --> EAR["PATIENT EAR\nSurface Electrodes\nVertex Mastoid Forehead"]
    EAR --> AMP["DIFFERENTIAL\nAMPLIFIER\nGain 100000\nCMRR greater than 100 dB"]
    AMP --> FIL["BANDPASS FILTER\n100 Hz to 3000 Hz"]
    FIL --> AVG["SIGNAL AVERAGER\n1024 to 2048 Sweeps"]
    AVG --> DSP["DIGITAL SIGNAL\nPROCESSOR\nWave V Detection"]
    DSP --> DIS["DISPLAY AND\nRECORDING\nLatency Amplitude Plot"]
    STIM -.trigger.-> AVG
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The signal averager is essential because ABR signals are in the **microvolt range (0.1–1 µV)** and are buried in EEG noise (~50 µV). Averaging 1000–2000 time-locked sweeps improves SNR by $\sqrt{N}$ — yielding ~30–45 dB noise reduction.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Audiometric Workflow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["PATIENT\nENTERS AUDIOMETRIC\nSUITE"] --> B["HISTORY AND\nOTOSCOPY\nWax Removal Check"]
    B --> C["PURE TONE\nAUDIOMETRY\nAC plus BC plus Masking"]
    C --> D{"AUDIOGRAM\nINTERPRETATION"}
    D -- "ABG Present" --> E["CONDUCTIVE LOSS\nor MIXED LOSS"]
    D -- "No ABG" --> F["SENSORINEURAL\nLOSS"]
    E --> G["TYMPANOMETRY\nConfirm Middle Ear\nStatus"]
    F --> H{"SITE OF\nLESION?"}
    H -- "SISI and Tone Decay" --> I["Cochlear vs\nRetrocochlear"]
    H -- "Speech Audiometry" --> J["Speech Reception\nThreshold and\nDiscrimination Score"]
    I --> K["BERA for\nRetrocochlear\nConfirmation"]
    G --> L["DIAGNOSIS AND\nREHABILITATION\nHearing Aid or Surgery"]
    K --> L
    J --> L
    L --> M["OAE for\nNewborn or\nMonitoring"]
```

## 4.4 Audiogram Schematic — Interpretation Visual

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid limitation:** The audiogram is a frequency-vs-threshold graph with separate AC and BC symbols. We represent it textually here:

| Audiometric Symbol | Ear & Conduction | Notation on Chart |
|---|---|---|
| O | Right Air Conduction | Red, right side |
| X | Left Air Conduction | Blue, left side |
| < | Right Bone Conduction (Mastoid) | Red, right side |
| > | Left Bone Conduction (Mastoid) | Blue, left side |
| [ or ] | Right/Left Bone Conduction (Forehead) | Used when mastoid is occluded |

> **Normal Audiogram:** AC and BC thresholds cluster between **–10 and +20 dB HL** across all frequencies.
> **Conductive Loss Pattern:** BC within normal, AC depressed (gap > 10 dB).
> **Sensorineural Pattern (Presbycusis):** Both AC and BC depressed, sloping from low to high frequencies.
> **Noise-Induced Loss (NIHL):** Classic **4 kHz dip (notch)** with recovery at 8 kHz.
