# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define the term "Hearing Level (dB HL)" and explain why it is used clinically instead of dB SPL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Hearing Level (dB HL) is a *biologically calibrated* scale in which **0 dB HL is defined as the average threshold of an otologically normal young adult** at each audiometric frequency.

**Reason for clinical use (2 marks):** The human ear is *not equally sensitive* across frequencies; it is most sensitive around 1–4 kHz. If clinicians used dB SPL alone, the same SPL value would represent different *perceived loudness* at different frequencies. The HL scale applies **frequency-specific correction factors** (calibrated per ISO 8253-1) to every audiometer so that **0 dB HL always represents the threshold of normal hearing**, regardless of frequency. This makes audiograms directly comparable across patients and clinics.

---

### **Question A2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between conductive and sensorineural hearing loss with respect to air–bone gap.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Conductive (CHL) | Sensorineural (SNHL) |
|---|---|---|
| Site of Lesion | Outer or middle ear | Cochlea or auditory nerve |
| Air Conduction (AC) Threshold | Elevated (worse than normal) | Elevated (worse than normal) |
| Bone Conduction (BC) Threshold | **Normal** (≤ 20 dB HL) | **Elevated** (matches AC) |
| **Air–Bone Gap (ABG)** | **Present** (> 10 dB) | **Absent** (≤ 10 dB) |
| Common Causes | Wax, otitis media, otosclerosis | Presbycusis, NIHL, acoustic neuroma |
| Treatment | Mostly medical/surgical | Hearing aid / cochlear implant |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question B — Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a clinical pure-tone audiometer. Describe the Hughson–Westlake method of threshold determination. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

Refer to §4.1 (the Mermaid block diagram). The audiometer consists of:
1. **Oscillator** (DDS/phase-shift) — generates pure tones at 125 Hz – 8 kHz.
2. **Attenuator** — stepped attenuator in 5-dB steps, calibrated to deliver 0 dB HL at threshold.
3. **Output selector switch** — routes signal to AC (headphone), BC (bone vibrator), or free-field speaker.
4. **Masking noise generator** — provides narrow-band noise to the non-test ear.
5. **Patient response system** — push-button + indicator lamp.
6. **Recorder / Display** — plots audiogram.

**Working (2 marks):**
- The audiologist presents a tone at a clearly audible level (e.g., 30 dB HL) and then varies intensity.
- Responses are recorded across frequencies 250 Hz – 8 kHz.
- For BC measurement, the B-71 vibrator is placed on the mastoid process.
- Masking noise (30–40 dB above AC threshold of non-test ear) is applied when required.

**Hughson–Westlake Method (2 marks):**
- This is the **standard threshold-seeking procedure (ISO 8253-1)**.
- **Initial presentation:** tone is presented at a level clearly audible (e.g., 30 dB HL).
- **"10 dB down, 5 dB up" rule:** The audiologist *decreases* intensity in 10-dB steps until the patient *no longer responds*. Then the audiologist *increases* intensity in 5-dB steps until the patient responds.
- **Threshold = lowest level at which the patient responds in at least 2 out of 3 ascending trials** at that frequency.
- This bracketing minimises response bias and is reproducible.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**A patient has the following audiometric thresholds. Identify the type, degree, and configuration of hearing loss. State the appropriate rehabilitation strategy.**

| Frequency | 500 Hz | 1 kHz | 2 kHz | 4 kHz |
|-----------|--------|-------|-------|-------|
| AC (R) | 60 | 65 | 70 | 80 |
| BC (R) | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Air–Bone Gap calculation (2 marks):**

| Frequency | AC | BC | ABG |
|-----------|----|----|-----|
| 500 Hz | 60 | 15 | 45 |
| 1 kHz | 65 | 20 | 45 |
| 2 kHz | 70 | 25 | 45 |
| 4 kHz | 80 | 30 | 50 |

**Step 2 — Interpretation (3 marks):**
- **ABG is significant (45–50 dB)** at all frequencies → **Conductive Hearing Loss**.
- **PTA (AC)** = (60 + 65 + 70 + 80) / 4 = **68.75 dB HL** → **Moderately Severe loss**.
- **BC thresholds are normal to slightly elevated** → Inner ear is intact.
- **Configuration:** *Flat* (similar loss across all frequencies, no high-frequency slope or notch).

**Step 3 — Rehabilitation (2 marks):**
- **Medical/surgical referral first:** Possible otosclerosis, chronic otitis media, or ossicular discontinuity.
- **Surgical options:** Tympanoplasty, myringotomy with grommet, stapedectomy.
- **If surgery is not viable:** A **bone-anchored hearing aid (BAHA)** or conventional **air-conduction hearing aid with appropriate gain** (~50 dB) may be fitted.
- **Speech therapy and auditory training** if the loss is long-standing.

---

### **Question B — Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the principle, procedure, and clinical applications of BERA (Auditory Brainstem Evoked Response Audiometry). [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
- BERA measures the **electrical responses of the auditory nerve and brainstem** to brief acoustic stimuli (usually 0.1 ms clicks).
- The responses are *evoked potentials* with amplitudes of **0.1 – 1 µV**, buried in spontaneous EEG noise of ~50 µV.
- **Signal averaging** over 1000–2000 time-locked sweeps improves SNR by $\sqrt{N}$, achieving 30–45 dB noise reduction.
- Five Jewett waves (I–V) are generated from successive neural generators:
  - Wave I — Auditory nerve
  - Wave II — Cochlear nucleus
  - Wave III — Superior olivary complex
  - Wave IV — Lateral lemniscus
  - Wave V — Inferior colliculus (most robust — used to estimate hearing threshold).

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Patient preparation:** Skin cleaned with abrasive paste; electrodes placed at vertex (active), mastoid/earlobe (reference), and forehead (ground).
2. **Stimulus:** Alternating-polarity clicks at 10–90 dB nHL, presented monaurally at ~10–30 per second.
3. **Recording:** Differential amplifier with gain ~10⁵ and bandpass 100–3000 Hz.
4. **Averaging:** 1024–2048 sweeps accumulated; the result is plotted as latency (ms) vs amplitude.
5. **Threshold estimation:** The lowest stimulus intensity at which **Wave V is just identifiable** is taken as the ABR threshold (correlates within 10–20 dB of behavioural threshold).

**Clinical Applications (2 marks):**
- **Newborn hearing screening** (especially in NICU babies).
- **Objective audiometry** in malingerers, unconscious patients, or those unable to co-operate.
- **Differential diagnosis** of retrocochlear lesions (e.g., acoustic neuroma — shows delayed or absent Wave V, prolonged I–V interval > 4.5 ms).
- **Intraoperative monitoring** during cerebellopontine angle or brainstem surgery.
- **Neurological assessment** of brainstem integrity (e.g., in multiple sclerosis, coma).

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Define Tympanometry. Sketch and explain the various types of tympanograms. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Tympanometry is an *objective* acoustic immittance test that measures the **compliance (mobility) of the tympanic membrane and middle-ear system** as a function of the air pressure in the sealed external ear canal, varied from +200 to –400 daPa.

**Principle (2 marks):**
- A probe tone of **226 Hz** is delivered into the sealed ear canal while the air pressure is swept.
- The probe microphone measures the *reflected* sound energy.
- **Maximum compliance** (peak of the curve) occurs at the pressure equal to the **middle-ear pressure** (normally 0 daPa).

**Types of Tympanograms (3 marks):**

| Type | Curve Shape | Peak Pressure | Peak Compliance | Clinical Meaning |
|------|-------------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **A** | Normal peaked curve | 0 daPa (between +50 and –100) | 0.3 – 1.6 mL | **Normal** middle ear |
| **As** | Low-amplitude peaked curve | 0 daPa | < 0.3 mL | **Stiff middle ear** (otosclerosis, tympanosclerosis) |
| **Ad** | High-amplitude peaked curve | 0 daPa | > 1.6 mL | **Hypermobile / flaccid** (ossicular discontinuity, monomeric TM) |
| **B** | Flat (no peak) | Absent | Flat | **Fluid-filled middle ear** (otitis media with effusion), perforation, or wax occluding canal |
| **C** | Peaked curve, shifted left | < –100 daPa | Normal/slightly reduced | **Negative middle-ear pressure** (eustachian tube dysfunction, retracted TM) |

**Acoustic Reflex (1 mark):**
- A loud sound (70–100 dB HL) normally triggers contraction of the **stapedius muscle**, stiffening the ossicular chain.
- The reflex is recorded as a *compliance decrease* on the tympanogram.
- **Absent reflex** → middle-ear pathology, severe SNHL, or retrocochlear lesion (e.g., acoustic neuroma — *stapedial reflex decay*).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on "Measurement of Deafness" Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating dB SPL with dB HL** — They are NOT the same. dB SPL is *absolute*; dB HL is *biologically calibrated*. Examiners award zero if you interchange them.
> 2. **Forgetting the air–bone gap rule** — Always calculate ABG and state *explicitly* whether it is present (>10 dB) or absent (≤10 dB) before naming the type of loss.
> 3. **Omitting masking discussion** — When the test tone exceeds ~40 dB HL, masking is essential. Many students omit this in the audiometer description and lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong BERA wave assignment** — Wave V is from the *inferior colliculus* (not the auditory nerve — that is Wave I). Confusing these costs full marks in Part B.
> 5. **Skipping the Hughson–Westlake explanation** — "How is threshold determined?" is asked every cycle. State the *10-dB-down, 5-dB-up* rule explicitly.
> 6. **SISI percentages** — A score of 80% does NOT mean the patient is "80% deaf"; it means they detected 80% of 1-dB increments (a test of recruitment).
> 7. **PTA formula** — Always average 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 2 kHz, AND 4 kHz (four frequencies). Using only three loses the mark.
> 8. **Tympanogram types** — Jadenberg/Jerger classification (A, As, Ad, B, C) must be memorised *with their peak pressures and clinical correlations*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — "Measurement of Deafness"**

- **Three Types of Hearing Loss:** Conductive, Sensorineural, Mixed. Diagnose via **air–bone gap**.
- **Reference Pressure:** $P_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$. **Reference Intensity:** $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$.
- **SPL** = $20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$ dB. **SIL** = $10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ dB. **HL** = biologically calibrated scale.
- **Pure Tone Audiometer** block: Oscillator → Attenuator → Selector (AC/BC/FF) → Masking → Patient response → Recorder.
- **Hughson–Westlake method:** 10 dB down, 5 dB up. Threshold = lowest level with 2/3 positive responses.
- **PTA** = (HL at 500, 1k, 2k, 4k Hz) / 4.
- **Air–Bone Gap (ABG)** = AC – BC; > 10 dB → conductive component.
- **Masking:** narrow-band noise to non-test ear when test tone > 40 dB HL.
- **Tympanometry:** Probe 226 Hz, pressure sweep +200 to –400 daPa.
  - **A** = normal; **As** = stiff; **Ad** = hypermobile; **B** = flat (fluid); **C** = negative pressure.
  - **Acoustic reflex** at 70–100 dB HL; absent in retrocochlear lesions.
- **BERA / ABR:** Objective audiometry, 5 Jewett waves, **Wave V from inferior colliculus** = threshold indicator.
  - Used for: newborns, malingerers, retrocochlear diagnosis, intraoperative monitoring.
  - Latency delay of Wave V → acoustic neuroma, demyelination.
- **OAE:** Tests outer hair cell function. **TEOAE** (clicks, 1–4 kHz) and **DPOAE** ($2f_1 - f_2$).
  - **Pass criterion:** Emission ≥ 6 dB above noise floor.
  - **Absent OAE** in: SNHL > 30 dB, middle-ear pathology.
- **SISI Test:** 1-dB increments on a 20-dB SL tone; **≥ 60% positive = cochlear**, **≤ 20% = retrocochlear**.
- **Tone Decay Test:** Retrocochlear lesions show rapid decay (≥ 30 dB); cochlear lesions stable.
- **Speech Audiometry:** SRT (speech reception threshold) ≈ PTA; SDS (speech discrimination score) reduced in SNHL and central lesions.
- **WHO Hearing Loss Grades:** Normal ≤ 15, Mild 26–40, Moderate 41–55, Severe 71–90, Profound > 90 dB HL.
- **Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL):** Classic **4-kHz dip** on audiogram.
- **Presbycusis (age-related):** Sloping high-frequency SNHL.
- **Rehabilitation:** CHL → medical/surgical; SNHL → hearing aid / cochlear implant; profound bilateral SNHL unresponsive to aids → **cochlear implant + BERA mapping**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electroretinogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electroretinogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Electroretinogram (ERG)** is a non-invasive, objective electrophysiological test that measures the cumulative electrical response of the retina — specifically its neuronal and non-neuronal cells (photoreceptors, bipolar cells, Müller cells, and ganglion cells) — to a standardized light stimulus. The biopotential is captured using a corneal contact lens electrode and amplified to produce a characteristic waveform that is clinically interpreted against **ISCEV (International Society for Clinical Electrophysiology of Vision)** standard protocols.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECAT747 / Module 3):** The Electroretinogram is grouped under *Bioelectric Recording Systems / Diagnostic Biopotentials*. The student is expected to identify each waveform component, explain its cellular origin, and describe the electrode-amplifier chain used to record it.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The EKG of the Eye"

Think of the ERG as the **"EKG of the eye"** — just as an Electrocardiogram sums the electrical activity of millions of cardiac cells into a single trace via skin electrodes, the ERG sums the electrical activity of roughly **150 million photoreceptors** plus millions of interneurons into a single voltage trace via a corneal electrode. A bright flash of light is the "depolarization trigger" (analogous to a heartbeat), and the resulting retinal cell currents produce a measurable waveform on the oscilloscope. If the retina is diseased, the waveform distorts — exactly as a damaged heart muscle distorts an EKG.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The typical ERG signal amplitude ranges from **100 µV to 500 µV** with the **a-wave latency ≈ 10–12 ms** and **b-wave implicit time ≈ 30–50 ms** under standard ISCEV scotopic conditions.

### 1.3 Physical Setup (High-Level)

| Element | Specification |
|---|---|
| Stimulus | Ganzfeld (full-field) flash of calibrated luminance |
| Active Electrode | Burian-Allen corneal contact lens / DTL fiber electrode |
| Reference Electrode | Temporal / forehead skin electrode (Ag/AgCl) |
| Ground Electrode | Earlobe or forehead |
| Amplifier Gain | ×10,000 to ×100,000 |
| Bandwidth | 0.3 Hz – 300 Hz (clinical) / DC – 10 kHz (research) |
| Output Display | Oscilloscope / Computer-based acquisition system |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Cellular Origin of ERG Waveform Components

The flash ERG is a **mass potential** — a spatial-temporal sum of ionic currents flowing across the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and neural retina in response to light. It is conventionally divided into named waves:

- **a-wave (Negative deflection, ~10–12 ms):**
  - Originates from the **photoreceptor outer segments** (rod and cone hyperpolarization).
  - Represents the **closure of Na⁺ channels** in the outer segment membrane in response to light → reduced dark current.
  - Best isolated using intense flashes under dark adaptation.

- **b-wave (Positive deflection, ~30–50 ms):**
  - Originates from the **ON-bipolar cells** and the **Müller (glial) cell K⁺ siphoning current**.
  - The "b-wave trough-to-peak amplitude" is the **single most clinically measured index** of outer-retinal function.

- **c-wave (Slow positive, ~2–4 s):**
  - Generated by the **retinal pigment epithelium (RPE)** — specifically the apical membrane hyperpolarization.
  - Used in research for evaluating RPE and choroidal disease.

- **d-wave (OFF-response, photopic):**
  - Generated by **OFF-bipolar cells** and cone photoreceptors when the light stimulus turns OFF.
  - Visible in long-flash photopic ERG.

- **Photopic Negative Response (PhNR):**
  - Negative wave following the b-wave, originating from **retinal ganglion cells**.
  - Clinical gold standard for **glaucoma assessment**.

### 2.2 Classification of ERG Tests

1. **Full-field (Ganzfeld) ERG** — entire retina stimulated.
   - **Scotopic ERG** — dark-adapted ≥ 20 min; isolates **rod system** using dim blue flash.
   - **Photopic ERG** — light-adapted; isolates **cone system** using bright white flash on a rod-saturating background.
2. **Pattern ERG (PERG)** — uses contrast-reversing checkerboard; isolates **macular ganglion cells**.
3. **Multifocal ERG (mfERG)** — localized cone responses across the macular region.
4. **Flicker ERG** — high-frequency (30 Hz) cone-driven response.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Naka-Rushton Equation (photoreceptor response vs. luminance)** | $V(I) = \dfrac{V_{max} \cdot I^{n}}{I^{n} + K^{n}}$ | $V$ = response amplitude (µV), $I$ = flash luminance (cd·s/m²), $V_{max}$ = max amplitude, $K$ = half-saturation constant, $n$ = slope (≈ 1.0 for normal retina) |
| **Amplifier Input-Referred Noise** | $V_{n,in} = \dfrac{V_{n,out}}{G}$ | Where $G$ = amplifier gain; must be $< 1 \,\mu V$ for clean ERG |
| **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | $A_{d}$ = differential gain, $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain. Clinical ERG amps: CMRR $> 100$ dB |
| **Input Impedance of Amplifier** | $Z_{in} \geq 10 \cdot Z_{electrode}$ | Electrode $Z \approx 5\text{–}10 \text{ k}\Omega$ → $Z_{in} \geq 100 \text{ M}\Omega$ |
| **b-wave Amplitude** | $V_{b} = V_{b,trough} - V_{a,trough}$ | Peak-to-trough measurement; normal: 350–500 µV scotopic |
| **High-Pass Filter (1st order)** | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Clinical $f_c = 0.3$ Hz |
| **Low-Pass Filter (1st order)** | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Clinical $f_c = 300$ Hz |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | ERG target: SNR $> 40$ dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Naka-Rushton:**
> - When $I \ll K$: $V \approx (V_{max}/K^{n}) \cdot I^{n}$ → linear region (small responses).
> - When $I \gg K$: $V \approx V_{max}$ → saturation (photoreceptor maximum firing).
> - Normal retinal slope $n \approx 1.0$. A reduced slope indicates **photoreceptor dysfunction** (e.g., retinitis pigmentosa).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ERG recording systems drive innovation in **low-noise biopotential amplifiers**, **active electrode design** (DTL thread, ERG-jet lens), and **DSP-based artifact rejection**. The same amplifier topology — **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** with driven-right-leg circuit — is reused in ECG, EEG, and EMG systems, making ERG a canonical case study in **biomedical front-end design** for the KTU PECAT747 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Naka-Rushton Equation for Retinal Luminance Response

The Naka-Rushton equation models the **steady-state retinal response amplitude $V$** as a function of flash luminance $I$. We derive it starting from the Michaelis–Menten enzyme kinetics analogy (used for photoreceptor transduction cascade).

**Step 1 — Start with the Michaelis–Menten saturation relation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(I) &= V_{max}\cdot \frac{[I]}{[I] + K_m} \\
\text{where } [I] &= \text{substrate (light intensity) concentration}, \\
K_m &= \text{Michaelis constant (half-saturation luminance)}.
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the simplest rectangular hyperbola. It fits, but with imperfect curvature for the human retina.

**Step 2 — Generalize by raising both terms to the power $n$ (Hill coefficient).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(I) &= V_{max}\cdot \frac{I^{n}}{I^{n} + K^{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The Hill exponent $n$ accounts for **cooperativity** in the phototransduction cascade (e.g., multiple G-protein subunits activated per rhodopsin bleach). A higher $n$ means a steeper transition from dark-current to saturated response.

**Step 3 — Determine $V_{max}$ and $K$ empirically.**

For a normal photopic ERG: $V_{max} \approx 400 \,\mu V$, $K \approx 1.0 \log\text{ cd·s/m}^{2}$, $n \approx 1.0$.

**Step 4 — Linearize for small $I$ to extract $K$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } I \ll K &\Rightarrow V(I) \approx \frac{V_{max}}{K^{n}} \cdot I^{n} \\
\text{Take log: } \log V &\approx n \log I + \log\!\left(\frac{V_{max}}{K^{n}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

A double-log plot (log $V$ vs. log $I$) is linear in this region; its slope gives $n$ and its intercept gives $K$.

**Step 5 — Saturation check for large $I$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } I \gg K &\Rightarrow I^{n} + K^{n} \approx I^{n} \\
\Rightarrow V(I) &\approx V_{max}\cdot \frac{I^{n}}{I^{n}} = V_{max}
\end{aligned}
$$

The response plateaus at $V_{max}$, confirming photoreceptor saturation.

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example

**Problem:** A scotopic ERG is recorded with flash luminance $I = 2.0 \log\text{ cd·s/m}^{2}$. The Naka-Rushton parameters are $V_{max} = 450 \,\mu V$, $K = 1.0$, and $n = 1.0$. Calculate the b-wave amplitude.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I \text{ in linear units} &= 10^{2.0} = 100 \text{ cd·s/m}^{2} \\
V(I) &= V_{max} \cdot \frac{I^{n}}{I^{n} + K^{n}} \\
V(100) &= 450 \cdot \frac{100^{1}}{100^{1} + 1^{1}} \\
V(100) &= 450 \cdot \frac{100}{101} \\
V(100) &= 450 \cdot 0.9901 \\
V(100) &\approx 445.5 \,\mu V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Predicted b-wave amplitude ≈ **445.5 µV** (within normal scotopic range of 350–500 µV). ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Pathology on ERG Parameters

| Disease | a-wave | b-wave | PhNR | b/a Ratio | Slope $n$ (Naka-Rushton) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal** | Normal | Normal | Normal | ~2.0 | ~1.0 |
| **Retinitis Pigmentosa** | Reduced (rod loss) | Severely reduced | Normal initially | Decreased | Reduced (< 0.7) |
| **Congenital Stationary Night Blindness** | Normal | Severely reduced | Normal | < 1.0 | Normal |
| **Open-Angle Glaucoma** | Normal | Normal | Reduced | Normal | Normal |
| **Cone Dystrophy** | Normal (photopic) | Reduced (photopic) | Variable | Reduced photopic | Reduced photopic |
| **Macular Degeneration (dry)** | Normal | Normal | Variable | Normal | Normal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the b/a Ratio Matters:** It separates photoreceptor disease (both a and b reduced) from post-photoreceptor disease (a normal, b reduced). This is one of the **highest-yield clinical pearls** for KTU viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — ERG Recording System

The following Mermaid block-level functional architecture shows the **complete signal flow** from light stimulus generation to clinical display, mapping every sub-system interaction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Ganzfeld Stimulator] --> A2[Calibrated Light Flash]
    A2 --> A3[Patient Eye and Retina]
    A3 -->|Photoreceptor Response| A4[Corneal Contact Lens Electrode]
    A5[Reference AgAgCl Electrode] --> A6[Differential Preamp Stage]
    A4 --> A6
    A6 -->|Gain x1000| A7[Instrumentation Amplifier INA128]
    A7 --> A8[Bandpass Filter 0.3 Hz to 300 Hz]
    A8 --> A9[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    A9 --> A10[Main Amplifier Gain x100]
    A10 --> A11[16 bit ADC at 1 kHz Sampling]
    A11 --> A12[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    A12 --> A13[Artifact Rejection Algorithm]
    A13 --> A14[Average of 100 Sweeps]
    A14 --> A15[Display Oscilloscope and LCD]
    A15 --> A16[Clinician Interpretation]
    A17[Driven Right Leg Circuit] --> A7
    A4 --> A17
    A5 --> A17
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cell-by-Cell Origin of ERG Waves

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L[Light Flash Stimulus] --> P[Photoreceptors Rods and Cones]
    P -->|Hyperpolarization| W1[a wave - Negative]
    P --> B[ON Bipolar Cells]
    B -->|Depolarization| M[Muller Glial Cells]
    M -->|K plus Siphoning| W2[b wave - Positive]
    RPE[Retinal Pigment Epithelium] -->|Slow Response| W3[c wave - Slow Positive]
    OF[OFF Bipolar Cells at Light Offset] --> W4[d wave - OFF Response]
    GC[Retinal Ganglion Cells] --> W5[PhNR - Photopic Negative]
    W1 --> OUT[Composite ERG Waveform]
    W2 --> OUT
    W3 --> OUT
    W4 --> OUT
    W5 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams:** The first flowchart shows the **electronic hardware chain**; the second shows the **biological origin** of each ERG wave. For KTU drawing questions, you can choose either perspective, but the **hardware signal flow** is more frequently asked in ESE Module questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Electroretinogram. Mention the standard electrode placement and typical amplitude range.*

**Model Answer:**
The Electroretinogram (ERG) is a non-invasive electrophysiological recording of the electrical activity of the retina in response to a light stimulus. The active electrode is a **Burian-Allen corneal contact lens** (or DTL fiber electrode) placed on the anesthetized cornea, the reference electrode is on the **temporal skin near the eye**, and the ground is on the **forehead or earlobe**. Typical amplitude ranges from **100 µV to 500 µV** with the b-wave being the largest deflection.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the cellular origin of the a-wave and b-wave of the ERG?*

**Model Answer:**
The **a-wave** originates from the **hyperpolarization of photoreceptor (rod and cone) outer segments** due to closure of cGMP-gated Na⁺ channels. The **b-wave** originates from the **depolarization of ON-bipolar cells** combined with the **K⁺ siphoning current of Müller glial cells**. The b-wave is the clinically dominant feature of the ERG.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete ERG recording system. Label each block and state the typical gain and bandwidth at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the origin of each waveform component (a, b, c, d, and PhNR) in a standard flash ERG. Briefly state the clinical relevance of the b/a ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Signal Flow (7 Marks):**

The complete ERG recording system has the following cascade:

1. **Ganzfeld Stimulator** — produces uniform full-field flash; luminance controlled in log cd·s/m².
2. **Corneal Electrode** — Burian-Allen contact lens captures retinal mass potential; output is a differential voltage of **100–500 µV** between active and reference.
3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA128 or AD620)** — first-stage gain **×1000**, very high CMRR (≥ 100 dB), input impedance > 100 MΩ.
4. **Bandpass Filter** — 0.3 Hz high-pass (to remove DC electrode offset) and 300 Hz low-pass (to remove high-frequency noise). $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$.
5. **Notch Filter at 50 Hz** — removes power-line interference.
6. **Main Amplifier** — additional gain **×100**; total system gain **10⁵**.
7. **16-bit ADC** at 1 kHz sampling rate (Nyquist: covers up to 500 Hz comfortably).
8. **DSP / Computer** — averages **100–200 sweeps** to improve SNR; SNR improvement: $\sqrt{N}$, so 100 sweeps → 20 dB improvement.
9. **Display** — oscilloscope or LCD with b-wave amplitude and implicit time printed.

(Diagrammatic representation should match Section 4.1 above.)

**(b) Waveform Origin and b/a Ratio (7 Marks):**

- **a-wave (negative, ~10–12 ms):** Photoreceptor outer-segment hyperpolarization. Reflects the closure of Na⁺ channels in darkness-to-light transition.
- **b-wave (positive, ~30–50 ms):** ON-bipolar depolarization + Müller K⁺ current. **Most clinically measured wave.**
- **c-wave (slow positive, ~2–4 s):** RPE apical membrane response; useful for RPE diseases.
- **d-wave (OFF response, photopic):** OFF-bipolar + cone depolarization at light offset.
- **PhNR (negative after b-wave):** Retinal ganglion cells; **glaucoma biomarker.**

**Clinical relevance of b/a ratio:**
- **Normal b/a ≈ 2.0**
- **Reduced b/a (< 1.0)** with **normal a-wave** → post-photoreceptor (bipolar/Müller) disease, e.g., CSNB.
- **Both a and b reduced proportionally** → photoreceptor disease, e.g., retinitis pigmentosa.
- **PhNR reduced** with normal a, b → ganglion cell disease, e.g., glaucoma.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the Naka-Rushton equation for the retinal luminance response. State the boundary conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A photopic ERG gives $V_{max} = 500 \,\mu V$, $K = 1.0$, and $n = 0.8$ (early cone dystrophy). Calculate the b-wave amplitude for flash luminance $I = 10 \text{ cd·s/m}^{2}$ and $I = 1000 \text{ cd·s/m}^{2}$. Comment on the slope. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Naka-Rushton (7 Marks):**

Starting from Michaelis–Menten kinetics for phototransduction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(I) &= V_{max}\cdot \frac{I^{n}}{I^{n} + K^{n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Boundary condition 1 (small I):** $I \ll K \Rightarrow V(I) \approx (V_{max}/K^{n}) \cdot I^{n}$. Linear on a log-log plot; slope = $n$.
- **Boundary condition 2 (large I):** $I \gg K \Rightarrow V(I) \approx V_{max}$ (saturation).
- **Normal retina:** $n \approx 1.0$, $K \approx 1.0 \log\text{ cd·s/m}^{2}$, $V_{max} \approx 400\text{–}500 \,\mu V$.
- **Pathology:** Reduced $n$ (< 0.7) implies **photoreceptor cooperativity loss**; reduced $V_{max}$ implies **reduced photoreceptor population** (e.g., RP, cone dystrophy).

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

For $I = 10 \text{ cd·s/m}^{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(10) &= 500 \cdot \frac{10^{0.8}}{10^{0.8} + 1^{0.8}} \\
10^{0.8} &= 6.31 \\
V(10) &= 500 \cdot \frac{6.31}{6.31 + 1.0} \\
V(10) &= 500 \cdot \frac{6.31}{7.31} \\
V(10) &\approx 500 \cdot 0.863 \\
V(10) &\approx 431.6 \,\mu V
\end{aligned}
$$

For $I = 1000 \text{ cd·s/m}^{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V(1000) &= 500 \cdot \frac{1000^{0.8}}{1000^{0.8} + 1^{0.8}} \\
1000^{0.8} &= 10^{2.4} \approx 251.2 \\
V(1000) &= 500 \cdot \frac{251.2}{252.2} \\
V(1000) &\approx 500 \cdot 0.996 \\
V(1000) &\approx 498 \,\mu V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on slope:** $n = 0.8$ is **reduced from the normal 1.0**, indicating **mild loss of photoreceptor cooperativity** — consistent with early cone dystrophy. The curve saturates more gradually. The system still approaches $V_{max} \approx 500 \,\mu V$ at high luminance, but with a softer transition, suggesting partial cone dysfunction rather than total cone loss.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse the a-wave origin with ganglion cells — a-wave is **photoreceptors**, ganglion cells give the PhNR.
> 2. **Do NOT** skip the gain values in the block diagram — examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for **quantitative values** (e.g., "Gain ×1000", "CMRR > 100 dB").
> 3. **Do NOT** use the symbol "|" in formulas on the answer sheet — use "divided by" or "/". (Same rule as the KTU formula sheet above.)
> 4. **Do NOT** forget the **driven-right-leg circuit** in the safety block of the ERG amplifier — patient isolation is a high-yield safety point.
> 5. **Do NOT** mix up scotopic and photopic — scotopic = rods, photopic = cones, and **DA 20 min is mandatory** for scotopic testing.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ ERG = **summed retinal mass potential** in response to a light flash, amplitude **100–500 µV**.
- ✅ **a-wave = photoreceptors**, **b-wave = bipolar + Müller**, **c-wave = RPE**, **d-wave = OFF-bipolar**, **PhNR = ganglion cells**.
- ✅ Active electrode = **Burian-Allen / DTL on cornea**; reference = **temporal skin**; ground = **forehead / earlobe**.
- ✅ Standard amplifier = **instrumentation amp with CMRR > 100 dB, input Z > 100 MΩ**.
- ✅ Bandwidth = **0.3 Hz – 300 Hz** (clinical); notch filter for **50 Hz** line noise.
- ✅ **Naka-Rushton** equation: $V(I) = V_{max} \cdot I^{n}/(I^{n} + K^{n})$; normal $n \approx 1.0$.
- ✅ **b/a ratio ≈ 2.0** normal; < 1 suggests **post-photoreceptor disease**.
- ✅ **Scotopic ERG** = dark-adapted ≥ 20 min, **rod-driven**, dim blue flash.
- ✅ **Photopic ERG** = light-adapted, **cone-driven**, bright white on rod-saturating background.
- ✅ **PhNR** reduced in **glaucoma**; **b-wave** reduced in **CSNB**; **a + b** both reduced in **retinitis pigmentosa**.
- ✅ ISCEV protocol mandates **patient consent, pupil dilation, dark adaptation, and standard flash strength**.
- ✅ **Signal averaging of 100–200 sweeps** improves SNR by $\sqrt{N}$ (≈ 20 dB for N = 100).
- ✅ **Driven right-leg circuit** is mandatory for patient safety and 50 Hz rejection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrooculogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrooculogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrooculogram (EOG):** A bioelectric signal that records the **standing corneo-retinal potential** of the human eye by measuring the voltage difference between the positively charged cornea and the negatively charged retina using surface electrodes placed around the orbit. The EOG is a fundamental ophthalmological and neuro-physiological tool used to study eye movements, diagnose disorders, and develop human-computer interface (HCI) systems.

The human eyeball behaves as a **rotating electrical dipole** embedded in a relatively conductive orbital volume conductor. When the eye rotates within its socket, the orientation of this dipole changes relative to fixed surface electrodes, producing measurable potential differences ranging typically from **0.4 mV to 1.0 mV** in amplitude.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Battery in a Bowling Ball":** Imagine your eyeball is a bowling ball with a tiny battery inside it, where the front of the ball (cornea) is the **positive terminal (+)** and the back of the ball (retina) is the **negative terminal (−)**. This battery is always on, even when your eyes are closed — it's a *standing potential* of about **0.4 to 1.0 mV**.
>
> Now, if you stick a few voltmeter probes (electrodes) on the skin around your eye, you'll notice that as you look left, right, up, or down, the battery effectively "rotates" inside the ball. The voltmeter readings change depending on which way the positive terminal points — closer to the right electrode (eye looks right) or closer to the left electrode (eye looks left). That changing reading, plotted over time, is the **Electrooculogram**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Standard Ranges

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Corneo-retinal standing potential | 0.4 – 1.0 | mV |
| EOG signal amplitude (saccades) | 50 – 3500 | µV |
| Useful signal bandwidth | DC – 100 | Hz |
| Skin-electrode impedance (target) | < 5 | kΩ |
| Typical EOG sampling rate (digital) | 100 – 1000 | Hz |
| Normal L/D (light-peak/dark-trough) ratio | > 1.8 | — |

### 1.4 Why EOG is a Unique Bioelectric Signal

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike ECG, EEG, or EMG, the EOG source is **not neural or muscular in origin**. It arises from the **metabolic activity of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE)**, which maintains a transepithelial potential across the eye. This makes the EOG a hybrid of electrophysiology and ophthalmic physiology.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of the Corneo-Retinal Potential

The standing potential arises from a **transepithelial voltage difference** maintained by the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE). The RPE actively pumps ions, creating a potential where:

- **Cornea side** → relatively **positive** (~ +1 mV relative to retina)
- **Retina (back of eye)** → relatively **negative** (reference, 0 mV)

The entire eye thus behaves as a **fixed-magnitude dipole** that **rotates** with eye movement, with a typical dipole moment magnitude of approximately **1 µA·cm** when modeled in a homogeneous volume conductor.

### 2.2 The Eye as a Rotating Dipole — The Physics

When the eye rotates by an angle **θ** from the primary (straight-ahead) position, the dipole orientation changes, and the surface electrodes (which are fixed to the skin) detect a different projection of the dipole field.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Model the eye as a current dipole **p⃗** of fixed magnitude *p*, embedded in a sphere of radius *a* (the eyeball ≈ 12 mm radius) surrounded by a homogeneous volume conductor (the orbit tissues).
2. For a dipole at the center of a spherical conductor, the surface potential at angular position **φ** from the dipole axis is governed by a **cosine law**.
3. As the eye rotates, the angular position of the cornea relative to a fixed electrode changes, modifying the recorded voltage.
4. The recorded signal is therefore proportional to **sine of the eye rotation angle** when electrodes are placed tangentially.

### 2.3 Electrode Placement (Standard Configuration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard 5-Electrode Configuration (Recommended by International Society for Clinical Electrophysiology of Vision — ISCEV):**

| Channel | Electrode Pair | Records |
|---|---|---|
| Horizontal EOG | Left outer canthus + Right outer canthus | Horizontal saccades (left/right gaze) |
| Vertical EOG | Above eyebrow + Below lower eyelid (one eye) | Vertical saccades (up/down gaze) |
| Reference | Forehead (Fpz) or earlobe | Common reference |

### 2.4 EOG Signal Characteristics by Eye Movement Type

| Movement Type | Amplitude Range | Duration | Frequency Content |
|---|---|---|---|
| Saccade (fast jump) | 200 – 3500 µV | 30 – 80 ms | 0 – 30 Hz dominant |
| Smooth pursuit | 50 – 500 µV | 200 – 800 ms | 0 – 10 Hz |
| Blink (artifact) | 1000 – 5000 µV | 100 – 400 ms | 0 – 20 Hz |
| REM sleep | 50 – 300 µV | 500 – 1500 ms | 0 – 5 Hz |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in derivations, numerical problems, and Part B questions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{surface}}(\theta) &= \frac{p \, \cos\theta}{4 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}} & &\text{(Dipole on a sphere, primary position aligned)} \\[4pt]
V_{\text{horizontal}} &= K_{h} \cdot \sin(\theta_{h}) & &\text{Horizontal channel, }\theta_{h}\text{ = horizontal gaze angle} \\[4pt]
V_{\text{vertical}} &= K_{v} \cdot \sin(\theta_{v}) & &\text{Vertical channel, }\theta_{v}\text{ = vertical gaze angle} \\[4pt]
\text{L/D Ratio} &= \frac{V_{\text{light peak}}}{V_{\text{dark trough}}} & &\text{Clinical EOG index, normal} > 1.8 \\[4pt]
\text{SNR}_{\text{EOG}} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{eye signal}}}{V_{\text{noise rms}}}\right) & &\text{Signal-to-noise ratio, target} \geq 20 \text{ dB} \\[4pt]
V_{\text{recorded}}(t) &= K \cdot \sin\!\big(\theta(t)\big) + n(t) & &\text{Total recorded EOG with additive noise } n(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $p$ | Dipole moment magnitude (corneo-retinal) | A·m |
| $\theta$ | Eye rotation angle from primary position | degrees or rad |
| $\sigma$ | Conductivity of orbital volume conductor | S/m |
| $a$ | Equivalent eyeball radius (≈ 0.012 m) | m |
| $K_{h}, K_{v}$ | Electrode-geometry constants (calibration) | µV/deg |
| $V_{\text{light peak}}$ | Max EOG amplitude in light-adapted state | µV |
| $V_{\text{dark trough}}$ | Min EOG amplitude after dark adaptation | µV |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where EOG matters in industry and research:**
> - **Assistive Technology:** EOG-driven wheelchairs and communication boards for ALS / locked-in syndrome patients.
> - **Sleep Medicine:** Detection of REM (Rapid Eye Movement) phases in polysomnography.
> - **HCI / Gaming:** Eye-controlled cursors, virtual reality (VR) gaze tracking.
> - **Ophthalmology:** Diagnosing Best disease, retinitis pigmentosa, and vitelliform lesions via Arden ratio (L/D ratio).
> - **Neuroscience:** Studying saccadic latency, smooth pursuit, and optokinetic nystagmus.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Corneo-Retinal Potential at Surface Electrodes

**Statement:** Show that the surface potential recorded by electrodes placed on either side of the eye is proportional to the **sine of the gaze angle**, given a rotating dipole model.

**Model Setup:**
- Place the eye at the origin of a coordinate system.
- Let the eye be approximated as a sphere of radius $a$ with conductivity $\sigma_{\text{eye}}$.
- The surrounding orbital tissue acts as a homogeneous volume conductor with conductivity $\sigma$.
- The corneo-retinal dipole has fixed magnitude $p$ (in A·m), aligned along the visual axis when the eye is in primary position.
- When the eye rotates by angle $\theta$ (e.g., to the right), the dipole tilts accordingly.

**Step 1 — Potential of a Dipole in an Infinite Homogeneous Medium:**

For a current dipole **p⃗** in an infinite homogeneous medium of conductivity $\sigma$, the potential at field point **r** is:

$$
V(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mathbf{p} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{r}}}{4 \pi \sigma \, r^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Potential on the Surface of a Conducting Sphere:**

When the dipole is at the **center of a sphere** of radius $a$ (the eyeball), the boundary condition requires continuity of normal current at $r = a$. The classical result (Plonsey, *Bioelectric Phenomena*) for the surface potential at angular position $\phi$ measured from the dipole axis is:

$$
V(\phi) = \frac{p \, \cos\phi}{4 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}}
$$

Here, $\phi$ is the angle between the dipole vector and the radius vector to the field point on the sphere.

**Step 3 — Geometric Interpretation of Eye Rotation:**

Let the dipole initially point along the +x axis (eye looking straight ahead). Now the eye rotates by angle $\theta$ to the right. The dipole now points along the new direction.

Place a recording electrode at angular position $\phi_{R}$ on the right side of the eye. After eye rotation, the angle between the **new dipole direction** and the **radius to the right electrode** changes from $\phi_{R}$ to $(\phi_{R} - \theta)$.

Therefore, the potential at the right electrode becomes:

$$
V_{R}(\theta) = \frac{p \, \cos(\phi_{R} - \theta)}{4 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Differential Recording (Bipolar Configuration):**

In a bipolar horizontal EOG, the right electrode and the left electrode are placed at symmetric angular positions: $\phi_{R} = +90°$ and $\phi_{L} = -90°$ (or equivalently $\pm \pi/2$ rad) with respect to the primary gaze direction.

Compute the potential at the **right electrode** after eye rotation by $\theta$:

$$
V_{R}(\theta) = \frac{p \, \cos(90° - \theta)}{4 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}} = \frac{p \, \sin\theta}{4 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}}
$$

Compute the potential at the **left electrode** after eye rotation by $\theta$:

$$
V_{L}(\theta) = \frac{p \, \cos(-90° - \theta)}{4 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}} = \frac{p \, \cos(90° + \theta)}{4 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}}
$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(90° + \theta) = -\sin\theta$:

$$
V_{L}(\theta) = -\frac{p \, \sin\theta}{4 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Differential Output Voltage:**

The differential amplifier records:

$$
V_{\text{diff}} = V_{R} - V_{L} = \frac{p \, \sin\theta}{4 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}} - \left(-\frac{p \, \sin\theta}{4 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}}\right)
$$

$$
V_{\text{diff}} = \frac{2 p \, \sin\theta}{4 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}} = \frac{p \, \sin\theta}{2 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Form with Calibration Constant:**

Defining the **electrode-geometry constant** $K = \dfrac{p}{2 \pi \sigma \, a^{2}}$, the recorded EOG voltage is:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{\text{EOG}}(\theta) = K \cdot \sin\theta\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The EOG is **linear in $\sin\theta$**, not in $\theta$ itself. For small angles ($\theta < 20°$), $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ (in radians), and the relationship is approximately linear. For larger gaze angles, the recorded voltage saturates and follows a sine curve. This is critical for designing HCI systems — the calibration must use the inverse-sine mapping, not the angle directly.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensitivity of EOG to Electrode Misplacement

**Problem:** Show that the EOG signal decreases as the electrode moves farther from the canthus.

**Solution Strategy:** The potential from a dipole falls off as $1/r^{2}$. As the electrode distance $d$ from the eye center increases (poor placement), the recorded voltage decreases quadratically.

**Step 1 — Modify the geometry:** Place the recording electrode at distance $d$ from the eye center along the canthus line, where $d > a$.

**Step 2 — The surface potential at distance $d$** (for a dipole of moment $p$ in a homogeneous medium):

$$
V(d, \phi) = \frac{p \, \cos\phi}{4 \pi \sigma \, d^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Ratio of signals** for two electrode positions $d_{1}$ and $d_{2}$ (with $d_{2} > d_{1}$):

$$
\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}} = \left(\frac{d_{1}}{d_{2}}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example:** If $d_{1} = 0.015$ m (canthus) and $d_{2} = 0.030$ m (temple):

$$
\frac{V_{2}}{V_{1}} = \left(\frac{0.015}{0.030}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Doubling the electrode distance from the eye **quarters** the signal amplitude. This is why KTU examiners emphasize proper canthal placement.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: L/D Ratio (Arden Ratio) — Clinical EOG Index

**Step 1 — The subject dark-adapts for 15 minutes**; the EOG amplitude drops to a minimum value called the **dark trough** ($V_{\text{dt}}$).

**Step 2 — The subject is then light-adapted for 10 minutes**; the EOG amplitude rises to a maximum called the **light peak** ($V_{\text{lp}}$).

**Step 3 — The Arden ratio is defined as:**

$$
\text{Arden Ratio} = \frac{V_{\text{lp}}}{V_{\text{dt}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Clinical interpretation:**

| Arden Ratio | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $\geq 1.8$ | Normal RPE function |
| $1.5 - 1.8$ | Borderline — re-test recommended |
| $< 1.5$ | Abnormal — suggests RPE / photoreceptor disease (e.g., Best disease) |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing EOG with Other Ocular Signals

| Feature | EOG | ERG (Electroretinogram) | VEP (Visual Evoked Potential) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Corneo-retinal standing potential | Retinal neural activity (rods, cones) | Visual cortex (occipital lobe) |
| **Stimulus** | None (passive) | Light flash | Pattern reversal |
| **Amplitude** | 0.1 – 1.0 mV | 0.005 – 1.0 mV | 0.001 – 0.020 mV |
| **Bandwidth** | DC – 100 Hz | 0.1 – 300 Hz | 0.1 – 100 Hz |
| **Invasive?** | No (surface electrodes) | No (contact lens electrode) | No (scalp electrodes) |
| **Primary use** | Eye movement, RPE function | Retinal disease diagnosis | Optic nerve / cortical disorders |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — EOG Acquisition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Eye as Rotating Dipole] --> B[Surface Electrodes at Canthi]
    B --> C[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    C --> D[Bandpass Filter 0.1 to 100 Hz]
    D --> E[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    E --> F[ADC and Sample at 500 Hz]
    F --> G[Microcontroller or DSP]
    G --> H[Display or HCI Output Device]
    G --> I[Storage and PC Interface]

    classDef bio fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef analog fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef digital fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class A,B bio
    class C,D,E analog
    class F,G digital
    class H,I output
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Equivalent Circuit of EOG Electrode-Skin Interface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SKIN["Skin and Tissue Volume"]
        E1[Electrode Ag or AgCl]
        E2[Electrolyte Gel]
        S1[Skin Epidermis]
        S2[Dermis and Subcutaneous]
        DIPOLE[Corneo Retinal Dipole Source]
    end

    E1 --- C1[Half Cell Potential ~ 250 mV]
    C1 --- R1[Electrode Skin Resistance 1 to 10 kohm]
    R1 --- S1
    S1 --- C2[Stratum Corneum Capacitance]
    C2 --- S2
    S2 --- DIPOLE

    DIPOLE --> SIG[EOG Signal 0.1 to 1.0 mV]
    SIG --> IA[Differential Instrumentation Amplifier]

    classDef src fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef io fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef amp fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class E1,E2,S1,S2,DIPOLE src
    class C1,R1,C2,SIG io
    class IA amp
```

### 4.3 Electrode Placement — Top-Down Anatomical View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FACE["Standard EOG Electrode Placement on Face"]
        V1[Vertical Channel Positive: Above Right Eyebrow]
        V2[Vertical Channel Negative: Below Right Lower Lid]
        H1[Horizontal Channel Positive: Right Outer Canthus]
        H2[Horizontal Channel Negative: Left Outer Canthus]
        REF[Reference Electrode: Center of Forehead Fpz]
    end

    V1 --- V2
    H1 --- H2

    classDef eye fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef chan fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef gnd fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class V1,V2,H1,H2 eye
    class REF gnd
```

### 4.4 Signal Processing Flow — From Raw EOG to Gaze Angle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Raw EOG Signal: Mixed DC Drift and AC Components] --> HPF[High Pass Filter at 0.1 Hz to Remove DC Drift]
    HPF --> LPF[Low Pass Filter at 30 Hz to Remove High Freq Noise]
    LPF --> NF[Notch Filter at 50 Hz to Remove Mains Interference]
    NF --> TH[Threshold Detector to Identify Saccade Events]
    TH --> CAL[Apply Calibration Map: K inverse sine]
    CAL --> GA[Gaze Angle Output in Degrees]
    GA --> APP[Application: Cursor Move or Wheelchair Control]

    classDef raw fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    class R raw
    class HPF,LPF,NF,TH,CAL proc
    class GA,APP out
```
