## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Electrooculogram (EOG). Mention its typical amplitude range and the standard electrode placement locations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Electrooculogram (EOG)** is a bioelectric signal that records the standing **corneo-retinal potential** of the eye using surface electrodes placed around the orbit. The cornea is electrically positive and the retina is negative, creating a dipole that rotates with eye movement.

**Amplitude range:** typically **0.4 mV to 1.0 mV** for the standing potential; **50 µV to 3500 µV** for saccadic EOG deflections.

**Standard electrode placement:**
- **Horizontal channel:** right outer canthus and left outer canthus
- **Vertical channel:** above the eyebrow and below the lower eyelid (one eye)
- **Reference:** forehead (Fpz) or earlobe
*(1 mark each for definition, amplitude, placement)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is the Arden ratio in clinical EOG? State its clinical significance and normal value.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Arden ratio** (also called the **L/D ratio**) is the clinical index derived from EOG measurements, defined as:

$$
\text{Arden Ratio} = \frac{V_{\text{light peak}}}{V_{\text{dark trough}}}
$$

It quantifies the function of the **retinal pigment epithelium (RPE)** by comparing the maximum EOG amplitude during light adaptation to the minimum amplitude during dark adaptation.

**Normal value:** greater than **1.8**.

**Clinical significance:** A subnormal Arden ratio (< 1.5) indicates RPE dysfunction, seen in conditions such as **Best disease (vitelliform macular dystrophy)**, retinitis pigmentosa, and other photoreceptor disorders.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option A**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the EOG voltage recorded by a pair of electrodes placed at the outer canthi of the eyes, modeling the eye as a rotating current dipole in a homogeneous volume conductor. Clearly state the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the complete EOG acquisition and signal conditioning system. Mention the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The eye is modeled as a sphere of radius $a \approx 12$ mm filled with a homogeneous conducting medium.
2. The surrounding orbital tissue is also a homogeneous volume conductor with conductivity $\sigma$.
3. The corneo-retinal standing potential is modeled as a current dipole **p⃗** of fixed magnitude, located at the center of the eye.
4. Recording electrodes are placed tangentially on the skin at the outer canthi.
5. The dipole rotates with the eye as a rigid body.

**Derivation steps:**

**Step 1:** The potential due to a current dipole at a field point in a homogeneous medium is:

$$
V(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mathbf{p} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{r}}}{4 \pi \sigma r^{2}}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** For a dipole at the center of a sphere of radius $a$, the surface potential at angular position $\phi$ from the dipole axis is:

$$
V_{\text{surface}}(\phi) = \frac{p \cos\phi}{4 \pi \sigma a^{2}}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** When the eye rotates by angle $\theta$ to the right, the dipole tilts. With bipolar electrodes at the right canthus ($\phi_{R} = +90°$) and left canthus ($\phi_{L} = -90°$), the new angular separations are $(\phi_{R} - \theta)$ and $(\phi_{L} - \theta)$ respectively.

The potential at the right electrode becomes:

$$
V_{R}(\theta) = \frac{p \cos(90° - \theta)}{4 \pi \sigma a^{2}} = \frac{p \sin\theta}{4 \pi \sigma a^{2}}
$$

*(2 marks)*

The potential at the left electrode becomes:

$$
V_{L}(\theta) = \frac{p \cos(-90° - \theta)}{4 \pi \sigma a^{2}} = -\frac{p \sin\theta}{4 \pi \sigma a^{2}}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4:** The differential output recorded is:

$$
V_{\text{diff}} = V_{R} - V_{L} = \frac{2p \sin\theta}{4 \pi \sigma a^{2}}
$$

**Final expression:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{\text{EOG}}(\theta) = \frac{p \sin\theta}{2 \pi \sigma a^{2}} = K \sin\theta\,}
$$

*(2 marks — boxed result and definition of K)*

---

**(b) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**

The EOG acquisition system consists of the following blocks (1 mark each for naming and 6 marks for explaining roles):

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **1. Surface Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** | Pick up the corneo-retinal potential from the skin around the eye; convert ionic current into electronic current. |
| **2. Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** | High CMRR (> 80 dB) differential amplification to reject common-mode noise such as 50 Hz mains interference. Gain typically 1000. |
| **3. Bandpass Filter (0.1–100 Hz)** | Removes DC electrode offset and high-frequency EMG / RF noise. |
| **4. Notch Filter (50 Hz)** | Suppresses power-line interference. |
| **5. ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** | Converts the analog EOG into digital samples at 500 Hz (per Nyquist criterion, since EOG < 100 Hz). |
| **6. Microcontroller / DSP** | Performs saccade detection, calibration (inverse-sine mapping), and drives the application. |
| **7. Output Interface** | Drives a wheelchair motor, computer cursor, or communication board based on decoded gaze direction. |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option B**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the origin of the corneo-retinal standing potential. How is it related to the recording of the EOG signal? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various applications of EOG in clinical diagnostics and biomedical engineering. Include a note on the Arden ratio. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Origin of Corneo-Retinal Potential (7 marks):**

**Origin (4 marks):**

The corneo-retinal standing potential is a **transepithelial potential** maintained by the **retinal pigment epithelium (RPE)**, a single layer of pigmented cells lying between the neural retina and the choroid.

- The RPE actively transports ions (primarily **K⁺, Na⁺, and Cl⁻**) across its apical and basolateral membranes.
- This active transport creates a voltage difference with the **cornea positive** and the **retina negative** when measured externally.
- The potential is **standing** — meaning it persists even in darkness and during steady fixation.
- The mechanism is dependent on the integrity of the **RPE cell layer** and the **photoreceptor–RPE interaction**.

**Relation to EOG Recording (3 marks):**

- The eye acts as a **rotating current dipole** of fixed magnitude, embedded in the conductive orbital tissues.
- When the gaze direction changes, the dipole axis rotates, but its magnitude remains constant.
- Surface electrodes placed around the orbit detect a **time-varying voltage** that is proportional to the **sine of the gaze angle** (as derived in the standard model).
- This time-varying voltage is the EOG signal.
- The EOG therefore directly reflects **eye position and movement**, making it a useful tool for both **ophthalmologic diagnosis** and **gaze-tracking applications**.

---

**(b) Applications and Arden Ratio (7 marks):**

**Clinical Diagnostics (3 marks):**

1. **Diagnosis of Best disease (vitelliform macular dystrophy):** Characterized by a severely subnormal Arden ratio (< 1.5) with normal ERG.
2. **Retinitis pigmentosa and other photoreceptor disorders:** EOG is abnormal early, often before ERG changes.
3. **Assessment of drug toxicity:** EOG is used to monitor chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine retinal toxicity.
4. **Detection of vitelliform lesions and RPE detachments.**
5. **Sleep stage identification:** REM (Rapid Eye Movement) sleep detection in polysomnography.

**Biomedical Engineering Applications (2 marks):**

1. **Assistive technology:** EOG-controlled wheelchairs and communication devices for patients with **locked-in syndrome, ALS, or severe paralysis**.
2. **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI):** Gaze-based cursor control and virtual reality (VR) input.
3. **Cognitive and psychological research:** Tracking eye movements to study attention, reading behavior, and decision-making.
4. **Driver drowsiness detection:** Slow or absent EOG saccades during microsleeps.

**Arden Ratio Note (2 marks):**

The **Arden ratio** is defined as:

$$
\text{Arden Ratio} = \frac{V_{\text{light peak}}}{V_{\text{dark trough}}}
$$

- **Dark trough:** Minimum EOG amplitude measured after 15 minutes of dark adaptation.
- **Light peak:** Maximum EOG amplitude measured after 10 minutes of light adaptation.
- **Normal value:** ≥ 1.8.
- **Abnormal value:** < 1.5 indicates RPE dysfunction.
- It is the **gold standard clinical index** derived from EOG recordings.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing EOG with ERG or EEG:** EOG is the *standing potential* of the eye; ERG is *retinal neural response to light*; EEG is *cortical brain activity*. Mixing these definitions costs easy marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the sine relation:** Writing $V = K\theta$ instead of $V = K\sin\theta$ loses a mark in any derivation. Always state the linearization condition ($\theta < 20°$) if you approximate.
> 3. **Skipping assumptions:** KTU examiners explicitly allocate **1–2 marks** for listing model assumptions (homogeneous volume conductor, spherical eye, fixed dipole magnitude). Omitting them is a common trap.
> 4. **Electrode placement errors:** Drawing electrodes on the eyeball itself, or forgetting the forehead reference, indicates weak practical knowledge.
> 5. **Arden ratio miscalculation:** The ratio is a *dimensionless* quantity; some students erroneously assign units. Always quote it as a pure number ≥ 1.8.
> 6. **Confusing dipole sign:** The cornea is **positive**, the retina is **negative**. Reversing this polarity leads to inverted signals and a wrong differential output sign in derivations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Electrooculogram (EOG):**

- **EOG** = recording of the **corneo-retinal standing potential** of the eye using surface electrodes.
- **Source:** Transepithelial potential of the **retinal pigment epithelium (RPE)** — not neural, not muscular.
- **Cornea = Positive (+)** ; **Retina = Negative (−)**.
- **Typical amplitude:** 0.4 – 1.0 mV (standing); 50 – 3500 µV (saccades).
- **Bandwidth:** DC to 100 Hz (clinical EOG typically 0.1 – 30 Hz after filtering).
- **Standard electrode placement:** outer canthi (horizontal), above brow + below lid (vertical), forehead (reference).
- **Governing equation:** $V_{\text{EOG}}(\theta) = K \sin\theta$, where $K$ is the electrode-geometry calibration constant.
- **Linearization:** For small gaze angles ($\theta < 20°$ or $< 0.35$ rad), $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ in radians.
- **Arden (L/D) ratio** = $V_{\text{light peak}} / V_{\text{dark trough}}$ ; normal **≥ 1.8** ; abnormal **< 1.5**.
- **Arden ratio < 1.5** → suggests RPE dysfunction (Best disease, retinitis pigmentosa, drug toxicity).
- **EOG falls off as $1/r^{2}$** with electrode distance — proper canthal placement is critical.
- **Saccades:** Fast, 30–80 ms, amplitude 200–3500 µV; main frequency content 0–30 Hz.
- **Blink artifact:** 1000–5000 µV, must be filtered or rejected.
- **Differential instrumentation amplifier** is mandatory (high CMRR > 80 dB) to suppress 50 Hz interference.
- **Sampling rate:** minimum 200 Hz; recommended 500 Hz (per Nyquist for 100 Hz EOG content).
- **Applications:** ophthalmic diagnosis, REM sleep detection, EOG-controlled wheelchairs, gaze-based HCI, driver drowsiness monitoring.
- **EOG ≠ ERG ≠ VEP** — know the differences (source, stimulus, amplitude, use).
- **Dipole model** assumes: spherical eye, homogeneous volume conductor, fixed dipole magnitude, central dipole position.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Recording Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Basic Recording Systems ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Basic Recording System (BRS)**: A biomedical recording system is an integrated electronic instrument chain designed to acquire, condition, amplify, process, and display low-amplitude, low-frequency bioelectric (or biopotential) signals generated by the human body — such as the heart (ECG), brain (EEG), and muscles (EMG) — into a clinically interpretable form for diagnosis, monitoring, and research. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECAT747)**, a basic recording system follows a canonical **five-stage signal chain**: $$ \text{Bioelectric Source} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Electrode} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Preamplifier} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Signal Conditioning} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Display / Recorder} $$ ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stethoscope for Electricity" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy**: Imagine trying to hear a whisper in a noisy stadium. To capture it, you would (1) cup your ears (the **electrode** picks up the tiny signal from the skin surface), (2) move closer to the person (**preamplifier** boosts the weak signal right at the source), (3) clean up the noise using a directional microphone (**signal conditioning** — filters out 50 Hz mains hum, motion artefacts, baseline drift), and finally (4) feed it into a speaker and tape recorder (**display** on a screen or **strip-chart printout**). A biomedical recording system does exactly this — except instead of sound waves, it is capturing the electrical whispers of your heart, brain, and muscles. ### 1.3 Standard Biopotential Signal Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Physiological Signal Ranges (Must Memorize for KTU)**: > > | Biopotential | Source Organ | Amplitude Range | Frequency Range | > |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| > | **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** | Heart | **0.5 – 5 mV** | **0.05 – 150 Hz** | > | **EEG (Electroencephalogram)** | Brain | **1 – 100 µV** | **0.5 – 100 Hz** | > | **EMG (Electromyogram)** | Skeletal Muscle | **50 µV – 5 mV** | **20 – 5000 Hz** | > | **EOG (Electro-oculogram)** | Eye | **10 µV – 5 mV** | **DC – 50 Hz** | > | **PCG (Phonocardiogram)** | Heart Sounds (acoustic) | **< 1 Pa** | **20 – 2000 Hz** | These metrics are non-negotiable — they dictate **electrode choice**, **amplifier gain**, **filter cut-off frequencies**, and **ADC sampling rate** in any KTU design problem. ### 1.4 Source Impedance — The Hidden Constraint > [!NOTE] > Bioelectric sources are *not* ideal voltage sources. The tissue–electrode interface behaves like a **half-cell potential (≈ ±300 mV DC offset)** in series with a **source impedance $Z_s$ of 1 kΩ to 100 kΩ**, which is why instrumentation amplifiers with **input impedance $> 10^9 \, \Omega$** are mandatory. --- ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 The Five Functional Blocks — Theory of Operation #### **Block 1: Electrode (Transducer Stage)** The electrode is the **electrolyte–metal interface** that converts ionic current (inside the body) into electronic current (inside the wire). * **Surface Electrodes** (Ag/AgCl disposable) → used for ECG, EEG surface mapping. * **Needle Electrodes** (concentric bipolar) → invasive, used for clinical EMG. * **Microelectrodes** (glass micropipette, tip diameter < 1 µm) → used for single-cell recording. The electrode–skin interface is modelled as the **Warburg impedance** in parallel with the **Helmholtz double-layer capacitance** $C_{dl}$ (typically **0.01 – 1 µF/cm²**). #### **Block 2: Preamplifier (Input Stage)** Almost universally implemented as a **three-op-amp Instrumentation Amplifier (IA)** because it provides: * **High Differential Gain** $A_d$ * **High Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** — critical because the body picks up **50 Hz mains interference** equally at both inputs. * **High Input Impedance** $Z_{in} > 10^9 \, \Omega$ to prevent loading of the high-impedance bio-source. #### **Block 3: Signal Conditioning** Includes: * **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** — eliminates DC offset and baseline wander (cut-off typically **0.05 Hz** for ECG, **0.5 Hz** for EEG). * **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** — limits bandwidth and prevents aliasing (cut-off **150 Hz** for ECG, **100 Hz** for EEG clinical, **40 Hz** for EEG diagnostic). * **Notch (Band-Stop) Filter** — removes **50 Hz power-line interference** (Q-factor ≈ 8–10). #### **Block 4: Main Amplifier / Driver** Provides the bulk of voltage gain (typically **1000× to 10,000×** for EEG, **1000×** for ECG). Often incorporates **isolation** (transformer or optocoupler) for patient safety — mandated by **IEC 60601-1**. #### **Block 5: Display / Recorder** * **Analog**: Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO), Strip-chart thermal printer. * **Digital**: LCD screen + on-board **ADC** (sampling rate per Nyquist: at least **2 × max signal frequency**, so ≥ **300 samples/s** for ECG, ≥ **200 samples/s** for EEG). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations — they account for ~70% of numerical marks in PECAT747.** $$ A_d \;=\; 1 \;+\; \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}} \quad \text{(Differential gain of 3-op-amp IA)} $$ $$ \text{CMRR}_{dB} \;=\; 20 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) $$ $$ f_c \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi R C} \quad \text{(First-order RC filter cut-off)} $$ $$ f_{sampling} \;\geq\; 2 \, f_{max} \quad \text{(Nyquist Criterion)} $$ $$ \text{Isolation Breakdown Voltage} \;\geq\; 1500 \, \text{V}_{RMS} \quad \text{(per IEC 60601-1)} $$ ### 2.3 Compact Formula Reference Table | Parameter | Formula | Typical KTU Value | Unit | |:---|:---|:---:|:---:| | Differential Gain $A_d$ | $1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$ | $1 - 1000$ | V/V | | CMRR | $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | $> 80$ | dB | | Noise Figure NF | $10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S/N_{in}}{S/N_{out}}\right)$ | $< 4$ | dB | | Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ | $2 Z_{diff} \, (1 + A_1 A_2)$ | $> 10^{9}$ | Ω | | HPF Cut-off (ECG) | $\frac{1}{2\pi R_{HPF} C_{HPF}}$ | $0.05$ | Hz | | LPF Cut-off (ECG) | $\frac{1}{2\pi R_{LPF} C_{LPF}}$ | $150$ | Hz | | Notch centre | $\frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | $50$ | Hz | | Sampling rate $f_s$ | $\geq 2 \, f_{max}$ | $\geq 300$ | Hz | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These systems are the **backbone of every clinical device** you see in an ICU: patient monitors, Holter recorders, polysomnography (sleep-lab) units, brain-computer interfaces (BCI), and even consumer wearables like the Apple Watch ECG lead-I recorder all use variations of this exact five-stage chain. --- ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation: Differential Gain of the 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **most-asked derivation in KTU board exams** for this module. Memorize it step-by-step. **Circuit Topology**: Two input buffer op-amps $A_1, A_2$ feed a unity-gain difference amplifier $A_3$. A single gain-setting resistor $R_{gain}$ sits between the inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$. Both buffers share feedback resistors $R_1$. **Step 1 — Apply the virtual-short principle at the inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$:** The voltages at the inverting inputs of $A_1$ and $A_2$ are forced equal to $V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively: $$ V_{(-),A_1} \;=\; V_1 $$ $$ V_{(-),A_2} \;=\; V_2 $$ **Step 2 — The current flowing through the gain resistor $R_{gain}$:** The same current $I$ must flow through $R_{gain}$ (no other path), and the same current flows through both $R_1$ feedback resistors. Therefore: $$ I \;=\; \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_{gain}} $$ **Step 3 — Output of the input stage (voltage across both $R_1$ resistors plus the drop across $R_{gain}$):** $$ V_{o1} - V_{o2} \;=\; I \,(R_1 + R_{gain} + R_1) \;=\; \left(\frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_{gain}}\right)(2R_1 + R_{gain}) $$ **Step 4 — Simplify by algebraic factoring:** $$ V_{o1} - V_{o2} \;=\; (V_1 - V_2) \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Output of unity-gain difference amplifier $A_3$:** The third stage subtracts and rejects common-mode: $$ V_{out} \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)(V_1 - V_2) $$ **Step 6 — Final closed-form differential gain:** $$ \boxed{\,A_d \;=\; \frac{V_{out}}{V_1 - V_2} \;=\; 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key engineering insight**: Only $R_{gain}$ needs to change to vary the gain — $R_1$ sets the maximum gain. This is why instrumentation amplifiers are commercially packaged as **single ICs** (e.g., INA128, AD620) with one external resistor $R_{gain}$. ### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Board Style **Problem**: Design the gain resistor of an ECG preamplifier using an AD620 instrumentation amplifier. Specifications: $R_1 = 39.2 \, \text{k}\Omega$ (built-in), desired differential gain $A_d = 100$. **Step 1 — Start with the gain equation:** $$ A_d \;=\; 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the known values:** $$ 100 \;=\; 1 + \frac{2 \times 39{,}200}{R_{gain}} $$ **Step 3 — Isolate the term containing $R_{gain}$:** $$ 99 \;=\; \frac{78{,}400}{R_{gain}} $$ **Step 4 — Cross-multiply and solve:** $$ R_{gain} \;=\; \frac{78{,}400}{99} \;\approx\; 791.9 \, \Omega $$ **Step 5 — Choose the nearest standard E12 value:** $$ \boxed{\,R_{gain} \;=\; 820 \, \Omega \text{ (standard E12)}\,} $$ **Step 6 — Verify by recalculating the actual gain:** $$ A_{d,actual} \;=\; 1 + \frac{2 \times 39{,}200}{820} \;\approx\; 96.6 \;\approx\; 100 \;\;(\pm 3.5\% \text{ tolerance — acceptable}) $$ ### 3.3 ECG Lead Equations (Einthoven's Triangle) For standard bipolar limb leads I, II, III measured from electrodes at **Right Arm (RA)**, **Left Arm (LA)**, and **Left Leg (LL)**: $$ V_I \;=\; V_{LA} - V_{RA} $$ $$ V_{II} \;=\; V_{LL} - V_{RA} $$ $$ V_{III} \;=\; V_{LL} - V_{LA} $$ **Einthoven's Law** (KVL around the closed triangle): $$ \boxed{\,V_I + V_{III} \;=\; V_{II}\,} $$ This law is the **clinical gold-standard integrity check** — if it fails, there is a loose electrode. --- ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Generic Bioelectric Recording System ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[BIOELECTRIC
SOURCE
Heart Brain Muscle] --> A2[ELECTRODE
Ag AgCl] A2 --> A3[PREAMP
Instrumentation
INA128] A3 --> A4[FILTER STAGE
HPF LPF Notch] A4 --> A5[MAIN AMP
Gain 1000x
with Isolation] A5 --> A6[ADC AND DSP
Sampling 500 Hz] A6 --> A7[DISPLAY
LCD CRO Printer] A7 --> A8[CLINICIAN
DIAGNOSIS] ``` ### 4.2 ECG-Specific Recording Chain ```mermaid flowchart TB src1[Cardiac Muscle
Depolarization] --> elec1[Surface Electrodes
10 placed on limbs and chest] elec1 --> leadSel[Lead Selector
Network Switches] leadSel --> preEcg[Instrumentation
Amplifier AD620
Gain 1000] preEcg --> hpEcg[HPF 0.05 Hz
Removes baseline drift] hpEcg --> lpEcg[LPF 150 Hz
Anti alias filter] lpEcg --> notchEcg[Notch 50 Hz
Power line reject] notchEcg --> isoEcg[Optocoupler
Isolation 5 kV] isoEcg --> adcEcg[12 bit ADC
500 samples per sec] adcEcg --> cpuEcg[Microcontroller
DSP and Display] cpuEcg --> lcdEcg[LCD Display
Real time waveform] cpuEcg --> printEcg[Thermal Printer
Strip chart] ``` ### 4.3 EEG Recording Architecture — Multi-Channel ```mermaid flowchart LR eegSrc[Brain Cortex
Scalp potentials] --> eegElec[Ag AgCl Disc
21 electrodes 10-20 system] eegElec --> eegMux[Analog Multiplexer
32 to 1 channel] eegMux --> eegPre[Low Noise Preamplifier
INA128 Gain 10000] eegPre --> eegFilt[Bandpass Filter
0.5 to 100 Hz] eegFilt --> eegIso[Patient Isolation
Transformer 5 kV] eegIso --> eegADC[24 bit Sigma Delta ADC
Sampling 256 Hz] eegADC --> eegProc[Digital Signal Processor
FFT and Feature Extract] eegProc --> eegMon[Neurologist Monitor
LCD PC Database] ``` ### 4.4 Decision Matrix — Which Filter Where? ```mermaid flowchart TB q1{Biopotential
Type?} q1 -->|ECG| ecgPath[HPF 0.05 Hz
LPF 150 Hz
Notch 50 Hz] q1 -->|EEG| eegPath[HPF 0.5 Hz
LPF 40 to 100 Hz
Notch 50 Hz] q1 -->|EMG| emgPath[HPF 20 Hz
LPF 5000 Hz
No notch] q1 -->|EOG| eogPath[HPF 0.1 Hz
LPF 50 Hz
Notch optional] ecgPath --> goal1[Clean P QRS T
waveform] eegPath --> goal2[Clean Delta Theta
Alpha Beta waves] emgPath --> goal3[Clean motor
unit action potentials] eogPath --> goal4[Clean eye
movement trace] ``` ### 4.5 Patient Safety — Isolation Architecture (IEC 60601-1) ```mermaid flowchart TB patient[Patient Side
Type CF or BF] --> isoBarrier[ISOLATION BARRIER
Optocoupler or Transformer
Breakdown 5 kV RMS] isoBarrier --> mainsSide[Equipment Side
Connected to Mains 230 V] patient -.->.-> ground1[Earth Ground
Independent] mainsSide -.->.-> ground2[Earth Ground
Mains referenced] isoBarrier --> leakageCtrl[Leakage Current
Limit 10 microampere CF
Limit 100 microampere BF] leakageCtrl --> safetyCheck[Safety Monitor
Per IEC 60601 1] ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** *List any three characteristic differences between ECG, EEG, and EMG biopotential signals. State one clinical application of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point × 3):** 1. **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** — amplitude **0.5 – 5 mV**, frequency **0.05 – 150 Hz**, source: depolarization of cardiac muscle. *Application*: diagnosis of myocardial infarction and arrhythmia. 2. **EEG (Electroencephalogram)** — amplitude **1 – 100 µV**, frequency **0.5 – 100 Hz** (Delta, Theta, Alpha, Beta bands), source: cortical neuron activity. *Application*: epilepsy diagnosis and sleep-stage analysis. 3. **EMG (Electromyogram)** — amplitude **50 µV – 5 mV**, frequency **20 – 5000 Hz**, source: skeletal muscle action potentials. *Application*: neuromuscular disorder detection (e.g., myasthenia gravis). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Why is an instrumentation amplifier (not a simple op-amp difference amplifier) used as the preamplifier in biomedical recording systems?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** * **High input impedance** ($> 10^9 \, \Omega$) — prevents loading of the high-impedance bioelectric source ($Z_s \approx 1$–$100 \, \text{k}\Omega$) **(1 mark)**. * **Very high CMRR** (typically $> 80 \, \text{dB}$) — rejects **50 Hz common-mode mains interference** picked up equally by both body electrodes **(1 mark)**. * **Variable gain via a single external resistor** $R_{gain}$ — provides flexible, calibrated gain (1× to 1000×) for signals ranging from EEG (µV) to ECG (mV) **(1 mark)**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **basic building blocks of a biomedical recording system**. Describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the **differential gain of a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier**. Given $R_1 = 39.2 \, \text{k}\Omega$, find $R_{gain}$ for a gain of 100. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):** $$ \text{Bio-Source} \;\to\; \text{Electrode} \;\to\; \text{Preamp} \;\to\; \text{Signal Conditioning} \;\to\; \text{Main Amp} \;\to\; \text{Display} $$ 1. **Bioelectric Source** (1 mark): Generates ionic potentials — e.g., cardiac muscle, neurons, skeletal muscle. Source impedance 1–100 kΩ, amplitude µV–mV. 2. **Electrode / Transducer** (1 mark): Converts ionic current into electronic current via the electrolyte–metal interface. Types: surface (Ag/AgCl), needle, microelectrode. 3. **Preamplifier** (1 mark): 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier providing high $Z_{in}$, high CMRR, and the first stage of voltage gain. 4. **Signal Conditioning** (2 marks): Includes HPF (removes DC drift), LPF (anti-aliasing), and **50 Hz notch filter** (removes mains hum). 5. **Main Amplifier & Isolation** (1 mark): Provides bulk gain (×1000 typical) and **patient isolation** (IEC 60601-1, breakdown > 4 kV) for safety against mains shock. 6. **Display / Recorder** (1 mark): CRO, LCD, or thermal strip-chart printer. For digital systems, an ADC samples at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist). --- **(b) Derivation & Numerical (7 marks):** *Derivation (4 marks):* Refer to **Section 3.1, Steps 1 to 6** of these notes. The key stages: $$ V_{o1} - V_{o2} \;=\; (V_1 - V_2)\left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right) $$ $$ V_{out} \;=\; A_d (V_1 - V_2) \;=\; \left(1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}\right)(V_1 - V_2) $$ *Numerical (3 marks):* $$ A_d \;=\; 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}} \;\Rightarrow\; 100 \;=\; 1 + \frac{2 \times 39{,}200}{R_{gain}} $$ $$ R_{gain} \;=\; \frac{78{,}400}{99} \;\approx\; 791.9 \, \Omega $$ $$ \boxed{\,R_{gain} \;\approx\; 820 \, \Omega \text{ (nearest E12 standard value)}\,} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **ECG recording system** with a block diagram. State the standard amplitude and frequency range of an ECG signal. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With necessary equations, explain the concept of **Einthoven's Triangle** and derive **Einthoven's Law**. Mention its clinical significance. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) ECG Recording System (7 marks):** *Block diagram (3 marks)* — Refer to **Section 4.2** of these notes (Mermaid ECG chain). *Amplitude/Frequency (2 marks)*: ECG amplitude is **0.5 – 5 mV**; frequency is **0.05 – 150 Hz** (clinical bandwidth). *Functional blocks (2 marks)*: * **Lead selector** — switches between 12 standard leads (I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, V1–V6). * **Instrumentation amplifier (AD620/INA128)** — high CMRR > 80 dB, gain ≈ 1000. * **Filters** — HPF (0.05 Hz), LPF (150 Hz), Notch (50 Hz). * **Isolation** — optocoupler/transformer (5 kV breakdown). * **Display** — LCD/thermal printer; ADC at $\geq 300$ samples/s. --- **(b) Einthoven's Triangle (7 marks):** *Setup (2 marks)*: Three electrodes (RA, LA, LL) form an equilateral triangle around the heart. The cardiac vector $\vec{M}$ projects onto the three sides of the triangle, giving three bipolar leads. *Equations (2 marks):* $$ V_I \;=\; V_{LA} - V_{RA} $$ $$ V_{II} \;=\; V_{LL} - V_{RA} $$ $$ V_{III} \;=\; V_{LL} - V_{LA} $$ *Derivation of Einthoven's Law (2 marks)*: $$ V_{II} \;=\; (V_{LL} - V_{RA}) \;=\; (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) + (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) \;=\; V_{III} + V_I $$ $$ \boxed{\,V_I + V_{III} \;=\; V_{II}\,} $$ *Clinical significance (1 mark)*: Acts as a **real-time integrity check** for electrode contact. If the equation fails during recording, one electrode is loose or has high contact impedance. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the polarity convention** in Einthoven's law ($V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$, NOT $V_{II} + V_{III} = V_I$). > 2. **Using `|` (vertical bar) in markdown tables** for absolute value — breaks the table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. > 3. **Quoting CMRR in raw ratio** (e.g., 10,000:1) instead of in **dB** ($20 \log_{10} 10{,}000 = 80 \, \text{dB}$). KTU examiners want dB. > 4. **Confusing sampling rate** with Nyquist *frequency* — sampling rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, so ECG needs $f_s \geq 300$ samples/s. > 5. **Skipping the isolation step** in block diagrams — for a clinical design problem, omitting patient isolation is an instant **−2 marks** deduction. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for PECAT747 – Basic Recording Systems:** * **Five-stage signal chain**: Source → Electrode → Preamp → Conditioning → Display. (Memorize the order — diagram is asked almost every semester.) * **Three biopotential amplitude ranges** to memorize: ECG (mV), EEG (µV), EMG (mV–µV span). Order of magnitude ≠ 1, this is a favourite trick question. * **Instrumentation amplifier** is the universal preamp — 3-op-amp topology, gain $A_d = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$, $R_{gain}$ is the **only** variable resistor. * **CMRR in dB** must always be expressed logarithmically — raw ratio is **not** accepted in board exams. * **Filters**: HPF 0.05 Hz (ECG baseline), LPF 150 Hz (ECG clinical), Notch 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (US) — remember the **country-specific mains frequency**. * **Einthoven's Triangle**: $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ — a vector projection of the cardiac dipole. * **Patient safety**: **IEC 60601-1** mandates isolation > 4 kV, leakage current < 10 µA (Type CF, e.g., ECG direct cardiac) and < 100 µA (Type BF, e.g., EMG/EEG surface). * **Nyquist sampling**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — so ECG $f_s \geq 300 \, \text{Hz}$, EEG $f_s \geq 200 \, \text{Hz}$, EMG $f_s \geq 10 \, \text{kHz}$. * **Source impedance** of tissue: **1 kΩ – 100 kΩ**, hence input impedance of amplifier must be **$10^8$–$10^{12} \, \Omega$** to avoid loading. * **Half-cell potential** at electrode interface: **±300 mV DC** — this is the reason for HPF at 0.05 Hz and the high-pass coupling capacitors at the input. * **Electrode types** in increasing invasiveness: Surface → Needle → Microelectrode. Invasiveness ↔ spatial resolution ↔ patient risk. * **Display devices**: Modern systems use **digital LCD + ADC**, but the classic **thermal strip-chart printer** is still asked in 3-mark definitions. * **Standard ECG leads**: 12 — 3 bipolar (I, II, III), 3 augmented (aVR, aVL, aVF), 6 precordial (V1–V6). * **EEG bands (in increasing frequency)**: Delta (0.5–4 Hz), Theta (4–8 Hz), Alpha (8–13 Hz), Beta (13–30 Hz), Gamma (> 30 Hz). * **EMG frequency range** (20 Hz – 5 kHz) is the **widest** of all biopotentials — this drives the **highest sampling rate** requirement.

topics.title: Biomedical Recorders - ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Biomedical Recorders - ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrocardiograph (ECG / EKG):** A biomedical recording instrument that captures the **electrical activity of the heart** over a period of time, using skin-surface electrodes placed on the limbs and chest. The output is a near-real-time voltage-versus-time waveform called the **Electrocardiogram**, with amplitudes typically ranging from **0.5 mV to 5 mV** and a frequency content of **0.05 Hz to 150 Hz**.

The ECG machine is essentially a high-gain, low-noise, differential bioelectric amplifier followed by a stylus-and-paper or digital display recorder. It is the single most widely performed diagnostic test in clinical medicine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of the heart as a large, biological battery (≈ −90 mV transmembrane potential) that fires thousands of times per day. Each heartbeat is a coordinated "discharge" of millions of cardiac cells. The ECG is simply a voltmeter that listens to this battery *from outside the body* through sticky metal electrodes, and *draws the voltage wobble on a moving strip of paper* — much like a seismograph records earthquakes.

Every peak, dip, and flat stretch on the tracing corresponds to a specific physical event inside the heart:
- A wave of **depolarization** (cells switching "ON")
- Followed later by **repolarization** (cells switching "OFF")

The doctor reads this "electrical story" to check whether the heart's rhythm, conduction speed, and muscle mass are healthy.

## 1.3 Standard Physiological & Electrical Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| ECG Signal Amplitude | **0.5 mV – 5 mV** |
| ECG Frequency Band | **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz** (dominant 0.5–40 Hz) |
| Standard Paper Speed | **25 mm/s** |
| Vertical Calibration | **10 mm = 1 mV** |
| Heart Rate (adult resting) | **60 – 100 bpm** |
| Skin–Electrode Impedance | < **5 kΩ** (modern pre-gelled Ag/AgCl) |
| Common-Mode Voltage (50/60 Hz mains) | up to several volts |
| Required CMRR | **> 80 dB** (diagnostic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 25 mm/s and 10 mm/mV?** These are *de facto universal calibration standards* adopted by the **AHA (American Heart Association)** and **IEC 60601-2-25** so that an ECG printed in Tokyo can be read in New York without conversion. KTU examiners *love* to test these values.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of the ECG Signal — The Cardiac Dipole

The heart is a three-dimensional bioelectric source. When a wave of depolarization sweeps through the myocardium, the boundary between depolarized (negatively charged inside) and resting tissue acts as a moving **electric dipole**, with vector $\vec{p}(t)$.

The body acts as a **volume conductor** (a 3D resistor filled with saline). By **Gauss's / Poisson's law**, the potential measured between two skin electrodes (Einthoven's theory) is the projection of the cardiac dipole vector onto the lead axis:

$$V_{\text{lead}} \;=\; \vec{M}(t) \cdot \hat{a}_{\text{lead}} \;=\; \vert \vec{M}(t) \vert \cos\theta$$

where:
- $\vec{M}(t)$ is the time-varying heart vector (magnitude × direction)
- $\hat{a}_{\text{lead}}$ is the unit vector along the chosen lead
- $\theta$ is the angle between the heart vector and the lead axis

## 2.2 The ECG Waveform — Anatomy of One Cardiac Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> A single, normal P-QRS-T complex represents **ONE complete cardiac cycle** lasting roughly **0.8 s at 75 bpm**. Each deflection is named in the historical order they were labeled by Einthoven (P was added *before* Q because A, B, C were already used for other waves).

| Wave / Segment | Origin | Typical Duration | Amplitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P wave** | Atrial depolarization | **≤ 0.11 s** | ≤ **2.5 mm** |
| **PR interval** | AV nodal conduction delay | **0.12 – 0.20 s** | isoelectric |
| **Q wave** | Initial septal depolarization | < 0.04 s | < 25 % of R |
| **R wave** | Main ventricular depolarization | – | dominant positive |
| **S wave** | Late ventricular depolarization | – | negative dip after R |
| **QRS complex** | Ventricular depolarization | **0.06 – 0.10 s** | 5–25 mm |
| **ST segment** | Ventricular plateau (all cells depolarized) | **0.05 – 0.15 s** | isoelectric |
| **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | **0.10 – 0.25 s** | < 5 mm |
| **QT interval** | Total ventricular systole | **0.36 – 0.44 s** | – |
| **U wave** | Late repolarization of Purkinje / papillary | small | often invisible |

## 2.3 The 12-Lead ECG System

The standard clinical ECG uses **12 leads** — 3 limb, 3 augmented limb, and 6 precordial (chest).

### Einthoven's Bipolar Limb Leads

Einthoven (1903) postulated that the three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) form an **equilateral triangle** centered on the heart, with the heart vector as the center. The bipolar leads are:

$$I \;=\; V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$

$$II \;=\; V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$

$$III \;=\; V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

**Einthoven's Law** (a direct consequence of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the triangle):

$$I + III \;=\; II \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad V_{LL} - V_{RA} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})$$

### Goldberger's Augmented Leads

$$aV_R \;=\; V_{RA} - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{LA} + V_{LL})$$

$$aV_L \;=\; V_{LA} - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{RA} + V_{LL})$$

$$aV_F \;=\; V_{LL} - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{RA} + V_{LA})$$

### Wilson's Central Terminal (Reference for unipolar chest leads)

$$V_{CT} \;=\; \frac{V_{RA} + V_{LA} + V_{LL}}{3}$$

The six precordial leads $V_1$ through $V_6$ are simply $V_{\text{chest electrode}} - V_{CT}$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Engineering Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{\text{lead}} = \vec{M}(t)\cdot\hat{a}$ | Projection of heart vector onto lead axis | mV |
| 2 | $I + III = II$ | Einthoven's law — KVL on body triangle | mV |
| 3 | $V_{CT} = \frac{V_{RA}+V_{LA}+V_{LL}}{3}$ | Wilson's central terminal | mV |
| 4 | $\text{CMRR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-mode rejection (target > 80 dB) | dB |
| 5 | $\text{Heart Rate} = \frac{1500}{\text{no. of small boxes between R–R}}$ | At paper speed 25 mm/s, 1 small box = 0.04 s | bpm |
| 6 | $\text{HR} = \frac{60}{\text{R–R interval (s)}}$ | Generic R–R formula | bpm |
| 7 | $\text{QTC} = \frac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | Bazett's corrected QT interval | s |
| 8 | $\text{Gain}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | Amplifier gain in dB | dB |
| 9 | $f_c^{\text{HP}} = 0.05\text{ Hz}$ | High-pass cutoff to remove DC baseline drift | Hz |
| 10 | $f_c^{\text{LP}} = 150\text{ Hz}$ | Low-pass cutoff to remove EMG / RF noise | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** Modern ECG design (e.g., ADI AD8232, TI ADS129x) embeds these exact filter cutoffs in firmware. The 0.05 Hz HP filter is what causes the baseline to "wobble" when the patient breathes — this *is* respiration! KTU questions frequently ask you to identify which filter is responsible for which artifact.

## 2.5 Block Diagram of a Modern ECG Machine

1. **Patient cable + Electrodes** (Ag/AgCl) → translates ionic skin current to electron flow
2. **Lead selector network** (Wilson network) → routes the chosen lead pair
3. **Instrumentation amplifier (INA)** → differential gain × 1000, CMRR > 100 dB
4. **Isolation amplifier** → 5000 V isolation, patient safety
5. **Band-pass filter** → 0.05 Hz – 150 Hz
6. **Notch filter (50/60 Hz)** → rejects mains interference
7. **Main amplifier** → additional × 10 to × 100
8. **Display / Thermal array recorder** → prints at 25 mm/s
9. **Microcontroller / DSP** → for digital storage and automated interpretation (e.g., Glasgow algorithm)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Einthoven's Law from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law

> **Goal:** Prove that $I + III = II$ using only the definition of the three bipolar leads and KVL.

**Step 1 — Write the lead definitions explicitly.**

$$I \;\equiv\; V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$

$$II \;\equiv\; V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$

$$III \;\equiv\; V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

**Step 2 — Add Lead I and Lead III.**

$$I + III \;=\; (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the inner potentials** ($+V_{LA}$ with $-V_{LA}$).

$$I + III \;=\; -V_{RA} + V_{LL}$$

**Step 4 — Reorder the right-hand side** (commutativity of subtraction).

$$I + III \;=\; V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the definition of Lead II** from Step 1.

$$V_{LL} - V_{RA} \;\equiv\; II \quad \therefore \quad \boxed{\,I + III \;=\; II\,}$$

This is purely a *consequence of the definition of the leads* — it does **not** depend on where the electrodes are placed or the size of the body. It is a *topological* identity. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Heart Rate from R–R Interval

> **Goal:** Find heart rate (bpm) given the number of small boxes (1 box = 1 mm) between two consecutive R-peaks on a standard 25 mm/s tracing.

**Step 1 — Time per small box.**

Paper speed $v = 25 \text{ mm/s}$, so 1 small box (1 mm) corresponds to:

$$t_{box} \;=\; \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{25 \text{ mm/s}} \;=\; 0.04 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Time per large box (5 small boxes).**

$$t_{large} \;=\; 5 \times 0.04 \text{ s} \;=\; 0.20 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — R–R interval in seconds.**

If there are $N$ small boxes between two consecutive R-waves:

$$T_{RR} \;=\; N \times 0.04 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert period to beats per minute.**

$$\text{HR} \;=\; \frac{60 \text{ s/min}}{T_{RR}} \;=\; \frac{60}{0.04 N} \;=\; \frac{1500}{N} \text{ bpm}$$

$$\boxed{\,\text{HR}_{\text{bpm}} \;=\; \frac{1500}{N_{\text{small boxes}}}\,}$$

**Numerical example:** $N = 20$ small boxes between R peaks → HR = $1500/20 = 75$ bpm.

## 3.3 Derivation: Cardiac Dipole Potential at a Body-Surface Electrode

> **Goal:** Express the potential at a skin electrode due to a moving dipole $\vec{p}$ inside a homogeneous volume conductor of conductivity $\sigma$.

**Step 1 — Place the dipole $\vec{p} = q\,\vec{d}$ at the origin** and a field point at position $\vec{r}$ from the dipole centre.

**Step 2 — The electric potential of a point charge $q$ in a homogeneous medium** is:

$$\phi(r) \;=\; \frac{q}{4\pi\sigma\,r}$$

**Step 3 — For a dipole** (superposition of $+q$ at $+\vec{d}/2$ and $-q$ at $-\vec{d}/2$):

$$\phi(\vec{r}) \;=\; \frac{q}{4\pi\sigma}\left[\frac{1}{\vert\vec{r}-\vec{d}/2\vert} - \frac{1}{\vert\vec{r}+\vec{d}/2\vert}\right]$$

**Step 4 — For the realistic ECG geometry** $\vert\vec{d}\vert \ll \vert\vec{r}\vert$, perform a Taylor expansion to first order:

$$\frac{1}{\vert\vec{r}\pm\vec{d}/2\vert} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{r}\left[1 \mp \frac{\vec{r}\cdot\vec{d}}{2r^{2}}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Subtract the two series and keep only the dipole term:**

$$\phi(\vec{r}) \;\approx\; \frac{q}{4\pi\sigma\,r^{2}}\,\frac{\vec{r}\cdot\vec{d}}{r} \;=\; \frac{\vec{p}\cdot\hat{r}}{4\pi\sigma\,r^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — For a measurement between two surface electrodes A and B** at distances $r_A$ and $r_B$:

$$V_{AB} \;=\; \phi(r_A) - \phi(r_B) \;=\; \frac{\vec{p}\cdot\hat{r}_A}{4\pi\sigma\,r_A^{2}} - \frac{\vec{p}\cdot\hat{r}_B}{4\pi\sigma\,r_B^{2}}$$

This is the *fundamental* equation that makes ECG possible. The detected voltage is the *projection* of $\vec{p}$ onto the lead direction. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation: CMRR Requirement from Realistic Interference

A 50 Hz mains interference of $V_{cm} = 2 \text{ V}$ (typical) is capacitively coupled to the patient. The ECG signal is $V_d = 1 \text{ mV}$ peak. To keep the interference below **1 % of the signal** (i.e., ≤ 10 µV):

$$\frac{A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}}{A_{d} \cdot V_{d}} \;\leq\; 0.01$$

$$\text{CMRR}_{\min} \;\geq\; \frac{V_{cm}}{0.01\,V_{d}} \;=\; \frac{2}{0.01 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 2 \times 10^{5}$$

Converting to dB:

$$\text{CMRR}_{\min} \;=\; 20\log_{10}(2 \times 10^{5}) \;\approx\; 106 \text{ dB}$$

This is *why* ECG instrumentation amplifiers specify **> 110 dB CMRR** — it is not marketing, it is physics.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Speed-Conversion Table (Frequently Asked)

| Clinical Parameter | Time at 25 mm/s | Time at 50 mm/s | Small Boxes (25 mm/s) | Large Boxes (25 mm/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| P wave | 0.08 s | 0.08 s | 2 | < 0.5 |
| PR interval | 0.16 s | 0.16 s | 4 | 0.8 |
| QRS | 0.08 s | 0.08 s | 2 | 0.4 |
| QT | 0.40 s | 0.40 s | 10 | 2 |
| ST segment | 0.12 s | 0.12 s | 3 | 0.6 |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level ECG Machine — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient Body and Skin] --> B[Electrodes AgAgCl x10]
    B --> C[Lead Selector Network Wilson]
    C --> D[Instrumentation Amplifier INA Gain 1000]
    D --> E[Isolation Amplifier 5kV]
    E --> F[High Pass Filter 0.05 Hz]
    F --> G[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    G --> H[Low Pass Filter 150 Hz]
    H --> I[Main Amplifier Gain 10]
    I --> J[ADC and Microcontroller DSP]
    J --> K[Display LCD]
    J --> L[Thermal Array Recorder 25 mm per s]
    J --> M[Memory Storage SD Card]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The signal flows **left to right**, from ionic currents in the body to a printed/digital trace. The INA provides the bulk of the CMRR; the isolation amplifier is a *safety* stage (galvanic separation between patient and mains-powered circuitry, mandatory per IEC 60601-1).

## 4.2 Lead Placement — 12-Lead ECG

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LIMB [Bipolar Limb Leads - Einthoven]
        RA[RA Electrode - Right Arm]
        LA[LA Electrode - Left Arm]
        LL[LL Electrode - Left Leg]
        RL[RL Electrode - Right Leg - Ground Reference]
    end
    subgraph AUGM [Augmented Limb Leads - Goldberger]
        AVR[aVR Augmented Vector Right]
        AVL[aVL Augmented Vector Left]
        AVF[aVF Augmented Vector Foot]
    end
    subgraph CHEST [Precordial Chest Leads - Wilson]
        V1[V1 - 4th ICS Right Sternal]
        V2[V2 - 4th ICS Left Sternal]
        V3[V3 - Midway V2 and V4]
        V4[V4 - 5th ICS Midclavicular]
        V5[V5 - Anterior Axillary Same Level V4]
        V6[V6 - Midaxillary Same Level V4]
    end
    RA --> I[Lead I = LA minus RA]
    LA --> I
    LL --> II[Lead II = LL minus RA]
    RA --> II
    LL --> III[Lead III = LL minus LA]
    LA --> III
```

## 4.3 Normal ECG Waveform — Temporal Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Baseline Isoelectric] --> B[P Wave Atrial Depolarization]
    B --> C[PR Segment AV Node Delay]
    C --> D[Q Wave Initial Septal]
    D --> E[R Wave Main Ventricular Upstroke]
    E --> F[S Wave Downstroke]
    F --> G[ST Segment Ventricular Plateau]
    G --> H[T Wave Ventricular Repolarization]
    H --> I[U Wave Purkinje Repolarization Optional]
    I --> J[Baseline Returns]
    J --> A
```

## 4.4 Einthoven's Triangle and Heart Vector Projection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRI [Einthoven Triangle]
        RA_node[RA Vertex Top Right]
        LA_node[LA Vertex Top Left]
        LL_node[LL Vertex Bottom]
    end
    HV[Heart Vector M of t] -. projection onto Lead I axis .-> I_axis[Lead I Axis LA to RA]
    HV -. projection onto Lead II axis .-> II_axis[Lead II Axis LL to RA]
    HV -. projection onto Lead III axis .-> III_axis[Lead III Axis LL to LA]
    RA_node --- LA_node
    LA_node --- LL_node
    RA_node --- LL_node
    HV --- RA_node
    HV --- LA_node
    HV --- LL_node
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU):** Whenever you draw Einthoven's triangle in the answer sheet, *always mark the polarity* (+ at LA for Lead I, + at LL for Lead II and III, − at RA) — examiners deduct marks if the polarity is missing.

## 4.5 Front-End Schematic (Simplified INA)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LA_in[LA Input] --> R1[R1 Gain Resistor]
    RA_in[RA Input] --> R2[R2 Gain Resistor]
    R1 --> A1[Opamp A1]
    R2 --> A2[Opamp A2]
    A1 --> RG[Gain Resistor Rg External]
    A2 --> RG
    A1 --> A3[Difference Amplifier A3]
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> OUT[Differential Output Vout]
    OUT --> NEXT[To Isolation Stage]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 2-Mark Short Questions (3 marks each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**Define the PR interval. State its clinical significance and the typical range of its duration in a healthy adult.**

**Model Answer:**

The **PR interval** is measured from the *beginning of the P wave* to the *beginning of the QRS complex*. It represents the **total time taken by the atrial depolarization wave to travel from the SA node through the atria, AV node, bundle of His, bundle branches, and Purkinje fibres up to the start of ventricular depolarization**.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Normal duration | **0.12 – 0.20 s** (3 – 5 small boxes at 25 mm/s) |
| Short PR (< 0.12 s) | Suggests **pre-excitation syndromes** (e.g., WPW) |
| Long PR (> 0.20 s) | Indicates **First-Degree AV Block** |

**Clinical significance:** It is the *direct measure of AV nodal conduction*. Prolongation = delayed AV conduction; abbreviation = bypass tract.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Why is a high CMRR (> 80 dB) essential in an ECG preamplifier? Justify with numbers.**

**Model Answer:**

A 50 Hz mains line picks up a common-mode voltage $V_{cm} \approx 1\text{–}2 \text{ V}$ on the patient through capacitive coupling. The actual cardiac signal $V_d \approx 1 \text{ mV}$ is differential. If the differential gain is $A_d = 1000$ and CMRR is only 60 dB (ratio 1000), the *interference* appearing at the output is:

$$V_{out}^{\text{interference}} \;=\; A_d \cdot V_{cm} / \text{CMRR}_{\text{ratio}} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 2}{1000} \;=\; 2 \text{ V}$$

This is **2000× larger** than the cardiac signal (1 mV × 1000 = 1 V) — the trace is unreadable.

With CMRR = 100 dB (ratio 10⁵), the interference is:

$$V_{out}^{\text{interference}} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 2}{10^{5}} \;=\; 0.02 \text{ V} \;=\; 2\% \text{ of signal}$$

This is acceptable. Hence the design rule: **CMRR ≥ 100 dB for diagnostic ECG.**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a modern single-channel ECG machine. Label all stages and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The block diagram is given in Section 4.1. Each stage is:

1. **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** — Convert ionic skin current to electronic current via a half-cell potential (~+0.22 V). Pre-gelled to maintain low (< 5 kΩ) skin–electrode impedance.

2. **Lead Selector Network (Wilson's Network + Goldberger's Network)** — Routes the desired pair (or augmented vector) to the amplifier input; provides the central terminal $V_{CT}$.

3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** — Three-op-amp topology providing differential gain ×1000, CMRR > 100 dB, very high input impedance (> 10 MΩ) to avoid loading the skin.

4. **Isolation Amplifier (Optical / Transformer)** — Provides 5 kV galvanic isolation between patient and the mains-powered recording circuitry. Mandatory patient-safety stage (IEC 60601-1).

5. **High-Pass Filter (0.05 Hz)** — Removes DC electrode offset and slow baseline drift due to respiration. Time constant = 3.2 s.

6. **Notch Filter (50 Hz / 60 Hz)** — Active twin-T or digital IIR notch with Q ≈ 30; removes mains hum.

7. **Low-Pass Filter (150 Hz)** — Removes EMG (muscle) noise and high-frequency RF.

8. **Main Amplifier** — Provides an additional ×10 gain and drives the display/recorder.

9. **Display / Thermal Array Recorder** — Prints on thermosensitive paper at 25 mm/s with 10 mm/mV vertical calibration.

10. **Microcontroller / DSP** — Digitises the signal (≥ 500 samples/s, 12-bit ADC), runs automated interpretation (e.g., Glasgow algorithm), stores to memory, and provides USB/Ethernet output.

#### (b) Explain Einthoven's triangle and derive the relationship $I + III = II$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Einthoven (1903) modelled the human torso as a homogeneous, uniform, triangular sheet of conductive tissue with the heart at its centre. The three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) form the vertices of an **equilateral triangle** known as the **Einthoven Triangle** (refer Section 4.4). The three sides of this triangle correspond to the three bipolar leads:

- Lead I axis → from RA to LA
- Lead II axis → from RA to LL
- Lead III axis → from LA to LL

The instantaneous **heart vector** $\vec{M}(t)$ is the resultant of all the depolarization dipoles in the myocardium at time $t$. The voltage recorded on any lead is the *projection* of $\vec{M}(t)$ onto that lead's axis.

By the **definition** of the leads:

$$I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}, \qquad II = V_{LL} - V_{RA}, \qquad III = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$

Adding I and III:

$$I + III = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA}) = V_{LL} - V_{RA} = II$$

Hence $\boxed{I + III = II}$. This is a *topological identity* — it is a consequence of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the closed loop RA → LA → LL → RA and is *independent* of electrode position or body shape.

**Significance:** This equation is routinely used as a *self-check* on every ECG printout. If $I + III \neq II$ within ± 0.5 mV, there is a lead-fault, a loose electrode, or a calibration error.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

#### (a) List and describe the various leads in a standard 12-lead ECG system. State the polarity of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The standard clinical ECG uses **12 leads** — 10 electrodes, 12 views of the heart.

**Group 1 — Three Bipolar Limb Leads (Einthoven, 1903):**

| Lead | Equation | Positive Electrode | Negative Electrode |
|---|---|---|---|
| I | $V_{LA} - V_{RA}$ | LA | RA |
| II | $V_{LL} - V_{RA}$ | LL | RA |
| III | $V_{LL} - V_{LA}$ | LL | LA |

**Group 2 — Three Augmented Unipolar Limb Leads (Goldberger, 1942):**
These use a *modified* central terminal of the *other two* limbs.

| Lead | Equation | Explores Region |
|---|---|---|
| aVR | $V_{RA} - \frac{1}{2}(V_{LA}+V_{LL})$ | Right ventricular / atria |
| aVL | $V_{LA} - \frac{1}{2}(V_{RA}+V_{LL})$ | Left lateral wall |
| aVF | $V_{LL} - \frac{1}{2}(V_{RA}+V_{LA})$ | Inferior wall |

**Group 3 — Six Unipolar Precordial / Chest Leads (Wilson, 1944):**
The exploring electrode is placed on the chest; the reference is **Wilson's Central Terminal** $V_{CT} = \frac{1}{3}(V_{RA}+V_{LA}+V_{LL})$.

| Lead | Position |
|---|---|
| V1 | 4th ICS, right sternal border |
| V2 | 4th ICS, left sternal border |
| V3 | Midway between V2 and V4 |
| V4 | 5th ICS, mid-clavicular line |
| V5 | Anterior axillary line, same level as V4 |
| V6 | Mid-axillary line, same level as V4 |

The right-leg (RL) electrode is *not* a lead — it is the **ground / driven-right-leg** reference used to *actively cancel* common-mode mains interference.

#### (b) An ECG recorded at 25 mm/s shows 22 small boxes between two consecutive R-waves. Calculate the heart rate. An R-wave has a height of 18 mm on the tracing. What voltage does this correspond to? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Heart rate from R–R interval.**

Number of small boxes $N = 22$.

At 25 mm/s, 1 small box = 0.04 s.

$$\text{R–R interval} \;=\; 22 \times 0.04 \text{ s} \;=\; 0.88 \text{ s}$$

$$\text{Heart Rate} \;=\; \frac{60}{\text{R–R}} \;=\; \frac{60}{0.88} \;\approx\; 68.18 \text{ bpm} \;\approx\; 68 \text{ bpm}$$

(Equivalent short-cut: $\text{HR} = 1500/N = 1500/22 = 68.18$ bpm.)

**Step 2 — Voltage corresponding to R-wave height of 18 mm.**

Standard calibration: 10 mm = 1 mV, so 1 mm = 0.1 mV.

$$V_{R} \;=\; 18 \text{ mm} \times 0.1 \text{ mV/mm} \;=\; 1.8 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 — Clinical interpretation.**

A heart rate of 68 bpm is **normal sinus rhythm (60 – 100 bpm)**. An R-wave of 1.8 mV is within the normal adult range (0.5 – 2.5 mV) — *no left or right ventricular hypertrophy* indicated.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting calibration units** — many students write "R = 18 mV" instead of 1.8 mV. Always show the conversion: 10 mm = 1 mV ⇒ 1 mm = 0.1 mV.
> 2. **Confusing PR interval with PR segment** — PR interval = P start to QRS start (includes the P wave). PR segment = P end to QRS start (isoelectric).
> 3. **Inverting Einthoven's law** — the correct form is I + III = II, not I + II = III. Memorise the loop orientation.
> 4. **Mixing up 25 mm/s and 50 mm/s** — at 50 mm/s, 1 small box = 0.02 s, so the "1500/N" formula becomes "3000/N".
> 5. **Forgetting the 4th electrode (RL)** — it is *not* a lead, it is the *ground*. Examiners specifically test this.
> 6. **Drawing Einthoven's triangle without polarity markings** — full marks require (+) and (−) on the vertices.
> 7. **Calling the ECG a "current" recorder** — it is a *voltage* recorder. The heart is modelled as a current dipole but the skin electrodes measure *potential difference*.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **ECG = voltage-vs-time record of the heart's electrical activity**, amplitude 0.5–5 mV, bandwidth 0.05–150 Hz.
- **Calibration standards:** 25 mm/s paper speed, 10 mm = 1 mV vertical.
- **Cardiac dipole model:** the heart is a moving electric dipole; skin electrodes record the *projection* of its vector onto the lead axis.
- **Einthoven's Triangle:** equilateral, RA–LA–LL vertices. The 3 bipolar leads are the 3 sides.
- **Einthoven's Law:** $I + III = II$ (always, by KVL — used as a self-check).
- **Wilson's Central Terminal:** $V_{CT} = (V_{RA}+V_{LA}+V_{LL})/3$ — reference for chest leads.
- **Goldberger's augmented leads:** aVR, aVL, aVF — modified-central-terminal unipolar limb leads.
- **12 leads total:** I, II, III, aVR, aVL, aVF, V1–V6. 10 electrodes, 1 ground (RL).
- **Standard waveform:** P → PR → QRS → ST → T → (U) → baseline.
- **Normal durations:** P ≤ 0.11 s, PR 0.12–0.20 s, QRS 0.06–0.10 s, QT 0.36–0.44 s.
- **Heart rate formulas:** HR = 60/RR(s) = 1500/N(small boxes at 25 mm/s) = 300/N(at 50 mm/s).
- **Instrumentation amplifier** is the heart of the front-end: gain ~1000, CMRR > 100 dB.
- **Isolation amplifier** (5 kV) is mandatory for patient safety (IEC 60601-1).
- **0.05 Hz HP filter** removes baseline drift; **150 Hz LP filter** removes EMG; **50 Hz notch** removes mains hum.
- **CMRR derivation:** for 2 V common-mode and 1 mV differential with 1 % tolerable interference → CMRR ≥ 106 dB.
- **Electrode type:** pre-gelled Ag/AgCl, half-cell potential ≈ +0.22 V.
- **KTU favourite 2-mark question:** *Why CMRR > 80 dB?* — derive the interference amplitude numerically.
- **KTU favourite 7-mark question:** *Explain Einthoven's triangle and derive I + III = II.*
- **KTU favourite 14-mark question:** *Block diagram of ECG machine with all stage functions.*
- **Key safety standard:** IEC 60601-1 (general), IEC 60601-2-25 (ECG-specific).
- **Modern single-chip ECG front-ends:** AD8232 (Analog Devices), ADS1292 (Texas Instruments), MAX30003 (Maxim) — integrate INA, filters, and ADC.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Patient Monitoring System (PMS):** A *Patient Monitoring System* is an integrated electronic instrumentation assembly that continuously or intermittently acquires, processes, displays, records, and transmits one or more *physiological parameters* of a patient, with the primary objective of providing real-time diagnostic information to clinical staff and triggering alerts when parameters deviate from pre-set physiological limits.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECAT747 – Biomedical Electronics, Module 4)**, a Patient Monitoring System is treated as a **multi-parameter bio-telemetry system** that converts non-electrical physiological phenomena into electrical signals, conditions them, digitizes them, and presents them to the clinician through a Human-Machine Interface (HMI).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Car Dashboard**
> Imagine driving a modern car. The dashboard continuously displays speed, fuel level, engine temperature, oil pressure, and tire pressure. You do not actively "measure" these — the sensors do it for you, the electronics process it, and the dashboard displays it. If fuel is low, a *beep* alerts you.

A **Patient Monitoring System is the medical equivalent of a car dashboard**:
- **Sensors** (ECG electrodes, SpO₂ probe, temperature probe) = Tire pressure / fuel sensors.
- **Signal Conditioner** = The car's onboard computer processing raw sensor data.
- **Display** (Bedside monitor / Central station) = Dashboard gauges.
- **Alarm Unit** = The fuel-low warning beep.

The system never replaces the doctor — it simply provides a **continuous, accurate, and tireless "second pair of eyes."**

### 1.3 Standard Physiological Metrics & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physiological Parameters Monitored (Bold = Critical, KTU-high-yield)**
>
> - **ECG (Electrocardiogram):** Heart electrical activity, range 0.05 – **5 mV**, frequency 0.05 – **150 Hz**.
> - **EEG (Electroencephalogram):** Brain activity, range 5 – **300 µV**, frequency 0.1 – **100 Hz**.
> - **EMG (Electromyogram):** Muscle activity, range 50 µV – **5 mV**, frequency 20 – **5000 Hz**.
> - **Respiration Rate:** 12 – **40 breaths/min** (adult), via impedance pneumography or chest belt.
> - **Body Temperature:** 32 °C – **42 °C**, typical probe accuracy ± **0.1 °C**.
> - **Blood Pressure (NIBP):** Systolic 80 – **200 mmHg**, Diastolic 40 – **120 mmHg**.
> - **SpO₂ (Arterial Oxygen Saturation):** 70 % – **100 %**, normal > **95 %**.
> - **Heart Rate (HR):** 30 – **250 bpm**.
> - **Capnography (EtCO₂):** 0 – **80 mmHg**, end-tidal CO₂.

### 1.4 Why Monitoring? — Clinical & Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Patient Monitoring System is Indispensable in Modern ICUs/CCUs/OTs:**
> 1. **Early Warning** — Detects deterioration *before* it becomes critical (e.g., arrhythmia detection 5–10 minutes before visible symptoms).
> 2. **Closed-Loop Therapy** — Modern ventilators, infusion pumps, and pacemakers *consume* monitoring data as control input.
> 3. **Documentation & Legal Record** — Continuous waveform + numeric storage serves as medico-legal evidence.
> 4. **Workload Reduction** — Nurse-to-patient ratio in ICUs can be 1:6; the monitor fills the perceptual gap.
> 5. **Telemedicine Enablement** — Provides the digitized physiological stream required for remote consultation.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Classification (Step-by-Step Logic)

A Patient Monitoring System can be classified along **five orthogonal axes**. KTU 2024 expects students to identify and justify the classification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis 1 — Mode of Measurement**
> - **Invasive Monitoring:** Sensor penetrates the body (e.g., intra-arterial BP catheter, central venous pressure line, intracranial pressure bolt). High accuracy, high infection risk, used in OT/ICU.
> - **Non-Invasive Monitoring:** Sensor only contacts the skin surface (e.g., surface ECG electrodes, SpO₂ finger probe, NIBP cuff). Safer, lower accuracy, ubiquitous on general wards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis 2 — Time Domain**
> - **Continuous:** ECG, SpO₂, respiration, EEG, capnography — sampled/displayed second-by-second.
> - **Intermittent:** NIBP, temperature (oral/tympanic) — measured every N minutes (typically 5/15/30/60 min).

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis 3 — Mobility / Architecture**
> - **Bedside Monitor:** Standalone unit at the patient's bed.
> - **Central Station:** Aggregates multiple bedside monitors at a nurse's desk.
> - **Ambulatory / Holter / Wearable:** Portable, battery-operated, records for 24 h – 7 days.
> - **Telemetry:** Wireless transmission from a mobile patient to a central receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis 4 — Parameter Count**
> - **Single-Parameter Monitor** (e.g., pulse oximeter).
> - **Multi-Parameter Monitor** (ECG + NIBP + SpO₂ + Temp + Resp = the standard "5-parameter" ICU monitor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis 5 — Care Setting**
> - **ICU / CCU / NICU / OT / ER / General Ward / Home Care.**

### 2.2 Functional Building Blocks — The "Why" Behind Each Stage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generic Block Chain (Universal to every Patient Monitor):**
> `Patient → Transducer/Sensor → Preamplifier → Signal Conditioner (Filter + Amplifier) → Isolation (Patient Safety) → A/D Converter → Microprocessor/DSP → Display + Alarm + Storage + Network`

- **Transducer / Sensor:** Converts physiological variable (pressure, temperature, displacement) into a measurable electrical signal. *Why?* Because the human body speaks in chemical/mechanical/thermal language; electronics speak in voltage/current.
- **Preamplifier:** First-stage low-noise amplification. *Why?* Biomedical signals are tiny (µV to mV); they would be drowned in noise otherwise.
- **Signal Conditioner:** Band-pass filtering, gain adjustment, baseline restoration. *Why?* Removes DC offset, mains interference (50/60 Hz), and motion artifact.
- **Isolation Amplifier (Mandatory per IEC 60601-1):** Galvanically separates patient-side electronics from mains-powered circuitry. *Why?* A leakage current > **10 µA** through the heart (microshock) can induce ventricular fibrillation.
- **A/D Converter:** Quantizes the analog signal for digital processing.
- **Microprocessor / DSP:** Runs the algorithms (QRS detection, SpO₂ ratio-of-ratios, arrhythmia classification, trend analysis).
- **Display (LCD/TFT) + Alarm (audible + visual) + Storage (RAM/SSD) + Network (HL7, Ethernet, Wi-Fi).**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Cheat Sheet for Patient Monitoring Systems**

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Standard | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s = 250$–$500$ Hz for ECG ($f_{max} = 150$ Hz) | Sets minimum ADC sampling rate |
| 2 | ADC Quantization | $N_{levels} = 2^n$ | $n$ = bit-width (8/12/16/24) | Determines resolution |
| 3 | ADC Step Size (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | $V_{FS}$ = full-scale input voltage | Smallest detectable voltage change |
| 4 | Resolution (Resolution) | $\%\text{FS} = \dfrac{1}{2^n - 1} \times 100$ | For 12-bit ADC ≈ **0.0244 %** | Defines measurement accuracy |
| 5 | SNR of ADC | $SNR = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB | $n$ = bits | 12-bit ≈ **74 dB**, 16-bit ≈ **98 dB** |
| 6 | Dynamic Range | $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{V_{max}}{V_{min}} \right)$ | ECG: $5\,\text{mV} / 5\,\mu\text{V} = 60$ dB | Needed to capture weakest & strongest signal |
| 7 | CMRR | $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{c}} \right)$ | ≥ **80 dB** in ECG amp | Rejects mains common-mode noise |
| 8 | SpO₂ (Beer–Lambert) | $R = \dfrac{AC_{660}}{DC_{660}} \div \dfrac{AC_{940}}{DC_{940}}$ | Lookup table to SpO₂ % | Pulse oximetry ratio |
| 9 | NIBP Oscillometric | $MAP \approx \text{peak oscillation amplitude}$ | Systolic ≈ 0.55 × MAP, Diastolic ≈ 0.85 × MAP (empirical) | Cuff-based BP estimation |
| 10 | NIBP Cuff Size | Bladder width ≥ **40 %** of limb circumference | AAMI / ISO 81060-2 | Prevents under/over-reading |
| 11 | Patient Leakage Current | $I_{leak} \leq 10\,\mu\text{A}$ (CF) | IEC 60601-1 (CF = cardiac floating) | Patient-safety electrical limit |
| 12 | Heart Rate from ECG | $HR = \dfrac{60}{RR_{interval}\,(\text{s})}$ | $RR$ = R-R peak distance | bpm computation |
| 13 | Respiration from Impedance | $Z = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$ | $\rho$ = thorax resistivity, varies with breathing | Impedance pneumography |
| 14 | Defibrillation-Proof Recovery | Recovery ≤ **5 s** post 5 kV | IEC 60601-2-49 | PMS must survive defibrillation |
| 15 | Alarm Latency | $t_{alarm} \leq 5\,\text{s}$ for life-threatening | IEC 60601-1-8 | Time from event to alarm sound |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall — Use `\vert` not `|`:** When writing the CMRR formula in your answer sheet, the absolute-value/slash style `|Ad/Ac|` is fine in handwriting, but in the printed key we use `$\vert A_d / A_c \vert$` to prevent markdown table breaking.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Operating Room:** Anesthesia workstation integrates ECG, SpO₂, EtCO₂, NIBP, multi-gas, and NMT (neuromuscular transmission) into a single monitor — *all channels time-synchronized* so an arrhythmia can be correlated with a desaturation event in the trend graph.
> - **Neonatal ICU (NICU):** Monitors add heart rate via chest-impedance, SpO₂ with **neonatal probe** (faster response, lower power IR-LED), and incubator temperature.
> - **Home Care / Wearable:** The Apple Watch / Fitbit ECG feature is a *single-lead* ambulatory patient monitor — a clinically validated KTU concept scaled to consumer form factor.
> - **Closed-Loop Anesthesia:** The monitor's BIS (Bispectral Index) output directly drives the propofol infusion pump — *the patient monitor becomes the sensor of a control system*.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Minimum Sampling Rate for ECG

The ECG signal has frequency components up to **150 Hz** (pacemaker spikes and notches). We must sample it at *at least* twice this to avoid aliasing.

**Step 1: State Nyquist Criterion**

The Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem requires:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

**Step 2: Identify $f_{max}$ for ECG**

For clinical-grade ECG with pacemaker pulse detection, the highest meaningful frequency is:

$$f_{max} = 150 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$f_s \geq 2 \times 150 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_s \geq 300 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Engineering Safety Factor**

In practice, a safety factor of 1.5 – 2× is applied to accommodate anti-aliasing filter roll-off and to allow numerical derivative / QRS-detection algorithms to work accurately. KTU monitors therefore use:

$$f_s^{clinical} = 500 \text{ Hz (typical)}$$

**Step 5: Verify the Choice**

For 500 Hz sampling, the maximum representable frequency is 250 Hz — comfortably above the 150 Hz ECG content. Aliasing cannot occur. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation II — ADC Resolution for ECG (Quantization Error)

We want the ADC's least-significant-bit (LSB) to be at least **5× smaller** than the smallest ECG feature of interest (≈ 10 µV — the smallest P-wave detail).

**Step 1: Determine Full-Scale Voltage**

A standard ECG front-end is designed for ±5 mV (total 10 mV range):

$$V_{FS} = 10 \text{ mV} = 0.010 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Write the LSB Equation**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$$

**Step 3: Set the LSB Requirement**

We require $\Delta V \leq 10\,\mu\text{V}/5 = 2\,\mu\text{V}$:

$$\frac{0.010}{2^n - 1} \leq 2 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $n$**

$$2^n - 1 \geq \frac{0.010}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 5000$$

$$2^n \geq 5001$$

Take $\log_2$ of both sides:

$$n \geq \log_2(5001) \approx 12.29$$

**Step 5: Round Up and State Result**

Since $n$ must be an integer:

$$n = 13 \text{ bits (theoretical minimum)}$$

In practice, a **12-bit ADC** is universally used (cost + speed trade-off) with a PGA (programmable-gain amplifier) providing dynamic range expansion. A **16-bit ADC** is used in research-grade Holter systems.

**Step 6: Cross-Check SNR**

For $n = 12$:

$$SNR = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 74 \text{ dB}$$

A 74 dB SNR is more than sufficient to resolve a 5 µV signal in a 5 mV full-scale — confirming the design.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Heart Rate Computation from the R-R Interval

Modern monitors detect the QRS complex via the **Pan–Tompkins algorithm** (or derivative-based equivalent), then measure the **R-R interval** in seconds.

**Step 1: Acquire R-Peak Timestamps**

Let the microprocessor mark R-peak occurrences at $t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots, t_N$ (in seconds).

**Step 2: Compute the Instantaneous R-R Interval**

$$RR_i = t_{i+1} - t_i \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Beats per Minute**

A 1-minute window contains $60 / RR_i$ beats:

$$HR_i = \frac{60}{RR_i} \quad \text{(bpm)}$$

**Step 4: Apply a Moving Average Filter (Noise Smoothing)**

To stabilize the displayed HR against ectopic beats:

$$HR_{displayed} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=k-N+1}^{k} \frac{60}{RR_i}$$

A common choice is $N = 8$ (i.e., 8-beat average ≈ 5 s of data at 72 bpm).

**Step 5: Numerical Example**

Suppose successive R-R intervals are 0.85, 0.83, 0.84, 0.86, 0.84, 0.85, 0.83, 0.84 s (a normal sinus rhythm at rest).

Convert each to HR:

$$HR = \{70.6,\, 72.3,\, 71.4,\, 69.8,\, 71.4,\, 70.6,\, 72.3,\, 71.4\} \text{ bpm}$$

Average:

$$HR_{avg} = \frac{70.6 + 72.3 + 71.4 + 69.8 + 71.4 + 70.6 + 72.3 + 71.4}{8} = \frac{569.8}{8} = 71.2 \text{ bpm}$$

The monitor will display **"HR 71 bpm"** with a sinus-rhythm indicator (green).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-Offs in PMS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.1 — Engineering Trade-Off Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU)**

| Design Choice | Option A | Option B | Trade-Off |
|---------------|----------|----------|-----------|
| Sampling Rate | 250 Hz (sufficient for QRS) | 500 Hz (captures pacer spike) | Storage ↑ 2× vs. diagnostic accuracy ↑ |
| ADC Resolution | 12-bit (4.9 µV LSB) | 16-bit (0.15 µV LSB) | Cost ↑ 3× vs. resolution ↑ 16× |
| Lead Configuration | 3-lead (I, II, III) | 12-lead (full diagnostic) | Patient mobility ↑ 5-lead vs. diagnosis quality |
| Alarm Strategy | Single threshold (L/H) | Smart alarm (multi-parameter trend, baseline drift) | False-alarm rate ↓ from **~85 %** to **~15 %** |
| Connectivity | Wired (RS-232) | Wireless (Wi-Fi / BLE) | Mobility ↑ vs. cybersecurity & EMI risk ↑ |
| Power | Mains-powered | Battery + UPS (≥ 2 h backup) | ICU/OT mandates battery backup |
| Isolation | Transformer isolation | Optocoupler / iCoupler | Modern PMS uses digital isolators (ADuM-series) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Multi-Parameter Patient Monitor

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below traces the signal flow from the patient's body to the central station. It is the **single most important diagram** the KTU examiner expects a Module-4 student to reproduce from memory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    patient["PATIENT BODY"]
    subgraph SENSOR_BANK ["SENSOR AND TRANSDUCER BANK"]
        ecg["ECG ELECTRODES 5 LEAD"]
        spo2["SPO2 FINGER PROBE"]
        nbp["NIBP CUFF"]
        temp["TEMP PROBE"]
        resp["RESP IMPEDANCE"]
    end
    subgraph COND ["SIGNAL CONDITIONING UNIT"]
        pa["PREAMP LOW NOISE"]
        iso["ISOLATION AMP IEC 60601"]
        filt["BPF 0.05 TO 150 HZ"]
        gain["GAIN 1000 TO 5000"]
    end
    subgraph ADC ["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERSION"]
        mux["ANALOG MULTIPLEXER"]
        ad["12 OR 16 BIT ADC"]
    end
    subgraph CPU ["DSP AND MICROCONTROLLER"]
        algo["QRS AND ARRHYTHMIA DETECTION"]
        spo2c["SPO2 RATIO OF RATIOS"]
        trend["TREND MEMORY 24 TO 72 H"]
    end
    subgraph HMI ["DISPLAY ALARM STORAGE NETWORK"]
        lcd["TFT LCD 12 INCH"]
        alarm["AUDIBLE AND VISUAL ALARM IEC 60601 1 8"]
        store["SSD STORAGE"]
        net["HL7 ETHERNET WIFI"]
    end
    subgraph CENTRAL ["CENTRAL NURSING STATION"]
        server["CENTRAL SERVER"]
        multi["MULTI BED VIEW 8 TO 16"]
        hl7recv["HL7 ADT RECEIVER"]
    end
    patient --> SENSOR_BANK
    SENSOR_BANK --> COND
    COND --> ADC
    ADC --> CPU
    CPU --> HMI
    HMI --> net
    net --> CENTRAL
```

### 4.2 Data-Flow Diagram — Alarm Decision Pipeline

This diagram shows how a single parameter (e.g., Heart Rate) flows from raw ECG to an alarm event.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawECG["RAW ECG SIGNAL 250 HZ"]
    preprocess["PREPROCESS BANDPASS 5 TO 30 HZ"]
    deriv["DERIVATIVE AND SQUARING"]
    mwint["MOVING WINDOW INTEGRATION 150 MS"]
    qrsdet["QRS DETECTION PAN TOMPKINS"]
    rrcomp["R R INTERVAL COMPUTATION"]
    hrcomp["HR BPM CALCULATION"]
    thresh["COMPARE TO ALARM LIMITS 50 TO 120 BPM"]
    dly["DEBOUNCE TIMER 5 S"]
    trig["TRIGGER ALARM SOUND AND RED FLASH"]
    log["LOG EVENT TIMESTAMP TO SSD AND HL7"]
    rawECG --> preprocess --> deriv --> mwint --> qrsdet --> rrcomp --> hrcomp --> thresh --> dly --> trig --> log
```

### 4.3 Architecture of a Central Monitoring Station (CMS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEDSIDE ["BEDSIDE MONITORS 1 TO 16"]
        b1["BED 1"]
        b2["BED 2"]
        bN["BED N"]
    end
    switch["NETWORK SWITCH ETHERNET 1 GBPS"]
    cserver["CENTRAL SERVER REDUNDANT"]
    db["PATIENT DATABASE HL7 FHIR"]
    cdisp["CENTRAL DISPLAY 32 INCH 4K"]
    cdisp2["WARD DISPLAY LARGE SCREEN"]
    mobile["DOCTOR TABLET AND SMARTPHONE APP"]
    hl7out["HL7 OUT TO HOSPITAL INFORMATION SYSTEM HIS"]
    BEDSIDE --> switch --> cserver --> db
    db --> cdisp
    db --> cdisp2
    db --> mobile
    cserver --> hl7out
```

### 4.4 Patient-Side Electrical Safety Subsystem (IEC 60601-1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mains["MAINS 230 V AC"]
    mediso["MEDICAL GRADE ISOLATION TRANSFORMER"]
    dc["DC SUPPLY 5 V 12 V 24 V"]
    pa["PATIENT APPLIED PART CF OR BF TYPE"]
    isoamp["ISOLATION BARRIER 5 KV REINFORCED"]
    sigcond["SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
    adcmcu["ADC AND MCU"]
    leak["LEAKAGE CURRENT MONITOR LESS THAN 10 MICROAMP CF"]
    mains --> mediso --> dc --> pa
    pa --> isoamp --> sigcond --> adcmcu
    adcmcu --> leak
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Type CF (Cardiac Floating)** applied parts (e.g., direct cardiac catheter, intracardiac ECG lead) are permitted a maximum leakage current of **10 µA** under normal and single-fault conditions. Type BF (Body Floating) permits **100 µA**. Type B (Body) permits **500 µA**. KTU 2024 expects students to know this hierarchy.
