## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: X-ray Generators

| Parameter | Single-Phase Full-Wave | Three-Phase (6-Pulse) | Three-Phase (12-Pulse) | High-Frequency |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| Output Ripple | ~100 % | ~13 % | ~4 % | < 1 % |
| Transformer Size | Large | Medium | Medium | Very small (1/10 size) |
| Beam Quality | Poor | Good | Very good | Excellent |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium-High | High | Moderate (falling) |
| Exposure Time | Longer | Shorter | Shorter | Shortest |
| KTU Frequency Asked | Common | Common | Sometimes | Trending |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Modern X-ray Machine (High-Frequency Generator)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete signal/power flow inside a modern, high-frequency X-ray unit. Every block represents a real engineering subsystem, and the arrows indicate the direction of energy/signal propagation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Mains["AC MAINS 50 60 Hz"] --> Rect["INPUT RECTIFIER BRIDGE"]
    Rect --> Filter["DC FILTER CAPACITOR BANK"]
    Filter --> Inv["HIGH FREQUENCY INVERTER IGBT MOSFET 1 TO 100 kHz"]
    Inv --> HVT["HIGH FREQUENCY HIGH VOLTAGE TRANSFORMER 30 TO 150 kV"]
    HVT --> HVR["HIGH VOLTAGE RECTIFIER CASCADE DIODE STACK"]
    HVR --> Tube["X RAY TUBE VACUUM ENVELOPE"]
    Tube --> Beam["COLLIMATED X RAY BEAM"]
    Beam --> Patient["PATIENT ANATOMICAL REGION"]
    Patient --> Detector["DIGITAL FLAT PANEL DETECTOR TFT a Se"]
    Detector --> ADC["ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER 14 BIT 16 BIT"]
    ADC --> Processor["IMAGE PROCESSING WORKSTATION"]
    Processor --> Display["DIAGNOSTIC DISPLAY 2K TO 4K"]
    Processor --> PACS["PACS ARCHIVE NETWORK STORAGE"]
    Console["OPERATOR CONSOLE kVp mA s"] --> Inv
    Console --> Processor
```

### Block-by-Block Explanation

1. **AC Mains:** Standard **230 V, 50 Hz** (or **120 V, 60 Hz**) supply.
2. **Input Rectifier Bridge:** Full-wave diode bridge converting AC to pulsating DC.
3. **DC Filter Capacitor Bank:** Smooths the DC and stores energy.
4. **High-Frequency Inverter:** IGBTs/MOSFETs chop DC into **1–100 kHz** AC. Higher frequency → smaller, lighter transformer.
5. **High-Frequency H.V. Transformer:** Steps up voltage to **30–150 kV**.
6. **H.V. Rectifier Stack:** Cascaded diode column converts HF AC to near-constant DC.
7. **X-ray Tube:** Vacuum tube where electrons are accelerated and strike the tungsten anode to produce X-rays.
8. **Collimated Beam:** Lead collimator restricts beam to the region of interest.
9. **Patient:** The beam is differentially attenuated by anatomical structures.
10. **Digital Flat-Panel Detector:** Captures the residual X-ray intensity distribution.
11. **ADC:** Converts analog detector signal to digital pixel values.
12. **Image Processor:** Applies flat-field correction, noise reduction, edge enhancement.
13. **Diagnostic Display:** High-resolution **2K–4K** monochrome monitor for radiologist review.
14. **PACS:** Picture Archiving and Communication System — long-term digital storage and remote access.
15. **Operator Console:** Allows radiographer to set **kVp, mA, exposure time (s)**, and select anatomical protocols.

---

## 4.2 X-ray Tube Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Cathode["CATHODE FILAMENT TUNGSTEN 2500 K"] --> FocCup["FOCUSING CUP NICKEL"]
    FocCup --> ElecBeam["FOCUSED ELECTRON BEAM"]
    ElecBeam --> Anode["ROTATING ANODE TUNGSTEN 74 RHENIUM 26"]
    Anode --> XrayOut["BREMSSTRAHLUNG AND CHARACTERISTIC X RAYS"]
    Glass["PYREX GLASS ENVELOPE VACUUM 10 TO THE POWER MINUS 6 TORR"] -.encloses.-> Cathode
    Glass -.encloses.-> Anode
    HVIns["HIGH VOLTAGE INSULATING BOOTS"] -.supports.-> Glass
    Cooling["OIL BATH COOLING SYSTEM"] -.surrounds.-> Glass
```

### Key Design Notes

- **Line-focus principle:** The anode face is angled (~**7–20°**) so the apparent focal spot (seen from the patient) is much smaller than the actual heat-bearing target area — this yields **sharp images** without overheating the anode.
- **Rotating anode speed:** Typically **3000–10000 RPM**, spreading heat over a large circular track.
- **Vacuum requirement:** $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ torr — prevents electron collisions with gas molecules and prevents tungsten oxidation.

---

## 4.3 Digital Radiography (DR) Workflow — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Acq["STEP 1 X RAY EXPOSURE"] --> Det["STEP 2 DETECTOR ABSORPTION"]
    Det --> Conv["STEP 3 PHOTON TO CHARGE OR LIGHT CONVERSION"]
    Conv --> Read["STEP 4 TFT READOUT LINE BY LINE"]
    Read --> Dig["STEP 5 ADC DIGITIZATION 14 BIT PIXEL MATRIX"]
    Dig --> Proc["STEP 6 PRE PROCESSING GAIN OFFSET CORRECTION"]
    Proc --> Post["STEP 7 POST PROCESSING EDGE ENHANCEMENT NOISE REDUCTION"]
    Post --> Disp["STEP 8 DISPLAY AND DIAGNOSIS"]
    Disp --> Arc["STEP 9 PACS ARCHIVING DICOM FORMAT"]
```

### DICOM Format Note

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine)** is the universal standard for medical image storage and transmission. Every DR image is stored as a DICOM file containing both the **pixel matrix** and **patient metadata** (name, age, exposure parameters, hospital ID, etc.).

---

## 4.4 Comparison Flow: Conventional Film vs. Computed Radiography vs. Digital Radiography

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Conv["CONVENTIONAL FILM SCREEN 1980S"] --> F1["CHEMICAL PROCESSING DARK ROOM"]
    F1 --> F2["PHYSICAL FILM STORAGE"]
    CR["COMPUTED RADIOGRAPHY 1990S"] --> C1["PSP PLATE READER LASER SCAN"]
    C1 --> C2["DIGITAL IMAGE DICOM FILE"]
    DR["DIGITAL RADIOGRAPHY 2000S ONWARDS"] --> D1["INSTANT TFT READOUT"]
    D1 --> D2["REAL TIME IMAGE WITHIN 5 SECONDS"]
    D2 --> D3["CLOUD PACS ACCESSIBLE FROM ANY TERMINAL"]
```

| Aspect | Film-Screen | CR (PSP) | DR (TFT) |
|--------|------------|----------|----------|
| Image Availability | ~5–10 min | ~30–60 s | **< 5 s** |
| Spatial Resolution | Very high (~**10 lp/mm**) | Moderate (~**5 lp/mm**) | High (~**3.5–5 lp/mm**) |
| Detector Reusability | No (single use) | Yes (hundreds of uses) | Yes (thousands of uses) |
| Patient Dose | Higher | Lower (~**30–50 %** less) | Lowest (~**50–70 %** less) |
| Cost | Low initial | Moderate | High initial, low lifetime |
| KTU 2024 Relevance | Historical | Transitional | **Current focus** |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q:** Define **Bremsstrahlung radiation**. Mention the factors on which its intensity depends.

**Model Answer (3 Marks distribution):**

1. **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Bremsstrahlung (German for "braking radiation") is the **continuous-spectrum X-ray emission** produced when a high-speed electron is decelerated by the strong Coulomb field of a target nucleus. The kinetic energy lost by the electron is converted into one or more X-ray photons.

2. **Factors affecting intensity (1.5 Marks):**
   - Atomic number **Z** of the target (intensity $\propto Z$).
   - Square of the tube voltage **V** (intensity $\propto V^2$).
   - Tube current **I** (intensity $\propto I$).
   - Exposure time **t**.

   Combined:

   $$
   I_{Brem} \propto Z \cdot V^{2} \cdot I \cdot t
   $$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**Q:** List **three advantages of high-frequency X-ray generators** over conventional single-phase generators.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Near-constant output voltage** with ripple **< 1 %**, producing a higher-quality, more penetrating X-ray beam (1 Mark).
2. **Smaller and lighter transformer** (about 1/10 the size) because higher operating frequency allows fewer turns in the windings (1 Mark).
3. **Shorter exposure times** and **reproducible exposures**, reducing patient motion artefacts and enabling precise dose control (1 Mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write "more efficient" without explaining *how*. You must state a specific quantitative benefit (e.g., ripple < 1 %, transformer 1/10 size, or reduced patient dose) to score the full 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Question (14 Marks, Module 4 Internal Choice)

> **Module 4 Choice Pattern:** KTU ESE provides an internal choice between two questions, each carrying **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) — 7 marks** and **(b) — 7 marks**. Choose ONE of the two questions below.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q:** With the help of a neat labelled block diagram, explain the **complete working of a modern high-frequency X-ray machine**. Compare the three types of X-ray generators in terms of output ripple and beam quality.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the block diagram of a high-frequency X-ray machine and the function of each block.**

**Model Answer:**

The modern high-frequency X-ray machine consists of the following interconnected blocks (7 Marks distribution):

1. **AC Mains Supply (0.5 Mark):** Provides standard 230 V, 50 Hz single-phase AC input.
2. **Input Rectifier & DC Filter (0.5 Mark):** A diode bridge converts AC to DC, smoothed by a capacitor bank.
3. **High-Frequency Inverter (1 Mark):** IGBTs/MOSFETs chop the DC at 1–100 kHz, producing high-frequency AC.
4. **High-Voltage H.F. Transformer (1 Mark):** Steps up the voltage to 30–150 kVp at the elevated frequency.
5. **High-Voltage Rectifier (0.5 Mark):** A cascade diode stack rectifies the HF AC to near-constant DC.
6. **X-ray Tube (1 Mark):** A vacuum diode where thermionically emitted electrons from a tungsten filament are accelerated toward a tungsten rotating anode, producing X-rays via Bremsstrahlung and characteristic emission.
7. **Collimator & Filter (0.5 Mark):** Restrict the beam geometry and remove low-energy (useless) photons.
8. **Digital Detector (FPD) and Image Chain (1 Mark):** Captures the residual X-ray intensity map, converts it to digital form via ADC, and processes it for display.
9. **Operator Console & PACS (0.5 Mark):** Allows parameter entry and provides long-term digital archival.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Compare the three types of X-ray generators.**

| Generator Type | Output Ripple | Beam Quality | Transformer Size | Cost |
|----------------|---------------|--------------|------------------|------|
| Single-Phase Full-Wave | ~100 % | Poor | Very large | Lowest |
| Three-Phase (6/12 Pulse) | ~4–13 % | Good–Very good | Medium-large | High |
| High-Frequency | < 1 % | Excellent | Very small (~1/10) | Moderate |

- **Explanation of Ripple (2 Marks):** Ripple is the residual AC component on the DC high voltage. A 100 % ripple means the voltage swings from zero to peak each half-cycle. This produces a wide range of photon energies, lowering the average beam quality.
- **Explanation of Beam Quality (2 Marks):** "Beam quality" refers to the **penetrating power and uniformity** of the X-ray spectrum. Near-constant potential (HF generators) gives mono-energetic-like beams that are more efficient per mA and produce sharper images with lower patient dose.
- **Clinical & Engineering Implication (1 Mark):** Modern radiology departments exclusively install HF generators for mammography, fluoroscopy, and CT because of dose efficiency, compactness, and reproducibility.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Explain the **principle of Digital Radiography (DR)**. With suitable diagrams, describe the **direct and indirect DR systems**, and discuss the key **performance parameters** of a digital detector (DQE, MTF, NPS).

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the principle of DR and the two acquisition methods.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Principle of DR (2 Marks):** Digital Radiography replaces the film-screen combination with a **solid-state flat-panel detector (FPD)** that directly converts the spatial X-ray intensity distribution into a **digital pixel matrix** (typically 2048 × 2048 to 3072 × 3072 pixels with 14–16 bit grayscale). The resulting image is stored in DICOM format and can be post-processed, transmitted, and archived electronically.

2. **Direct DR (2.5 Marks):**
   - Uses a **photoconductor layer**, typically **amorphous Selenium (a-Se)**, deposited onto a **TFT array**.
   - X-ray photons interact with a-Se via the **photoelectric effect**, generating **electron-hole pairs** directly.
   - The charges are collected by the TFT pixel capacitors and read out electronically.
   - **Advantage:** Higher **DQE** at diagnostic energies because there is no intermediate light-conversion stage.
   - **Disadvantage:** a-Se has lower absorption than CsI for energies above 80 keV.

3. **Indirect DR (2.5 Marks):**
   - Uses a **scintillator** (CsI:Tl or Gd₂O₂S:Tb) coupled to an **a-Si photodiode + TFT array**.
   - X-rays → **visible light photons** in the scintillator → **electrical charge** in the photodiodes.
   - **Advantage:** Mature technology, higher absorption at high kVp.
   - **Disadvantage:** Light spread between scintillator and photodiode slightly reduces spatial resolution.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Discuss the key performance parameters of a digital detector.**

1. **DQE — Detective Quantum Efficiency (2.5 Marks):**
   Defined as the ratio of the squared SNR at the detector output to the squared SNR at its input:

   $$
   DQE(f) = \frac{SNR_{out}^{2}(f)}{SNR_{in}^{2}(f)}
   $$

   DQE varies with spatial frequency $f$ and is the **single most important figure of merit** for a digital detector. Modern FPDs achieve DQE(0) of **60–80 %**, vastly superior to film-screen (~30 %).

2. **MTF — Modulation Transfer Function (2.5 Marks):**
   Describes how well the detector preserves spatial detail:

   $$
   MTF(f) = \frac{\vert C(f) \vert}{\vert C(0) \vert}
   $$

   where $C(f)$ is the Fourier transform of the line-spread function. A perfect detector has MTF = 1 at all frequencies. Real FPDs have MTF dropping to 0.1–0.2 at the Nyquist frequency.

3. **NPS — Noise Power Spectrum (1.5 Marks):**
   Characterizes the **frequency distribution of noise**:

   $$
   NPS(f) = \lim_{A \to \infty} \frac{\langle \vert \Delta I(x,y) \vert^{2} \rangle}{A}
   $$

   - Flat NPS → white noise.
   - Falling NPS → low-frequency-dominant noise (typical for FPDs).
   - NPS combined with MTF fully characterizes detector performance and feeds into DQE.

4. **Synthesis (0.5 Mark):** Higher DQE, higher MTF, and lower NPS collectively yield **lower patient dose with higher diagnostic image quality** — the central engineering goal of modern DR systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** Many students confuse the acronyms DQE, MTF, and NPS. Make sure you write the **full form first** (Detective Quantum Efficiency, Modulation Transfer Function, Noise Power Spectrum) and then the equation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Do not write generic "high resolution, low noise" as your DR answer. KTU examiners expect **explicit equations** for DQE and MTF and a **clear differentiation** between direct and indirect FPDs. Marks are deducted for vague answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4 (X-ray Machines & DR):**

- [x] X-rays are produced by **Bremsstrahlung (continuous)** and **Characteristic (line)** emission in an X-ray tube.
- [x] **Duane-Hunt Limit:** $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$ — must be derivable for full marks.
- [x] **Kramers' Efficiency:** $\eta = K \cdot Z \cdot V$ — only ~**0.7 %** of input power becomes X-rays at 100 kVp with W target.
- [x] X-ray tube components: **Cathode (filament + focusing cup), Anode (W or W-Re), Vacuum envelope (~10⁻⁶ torr), High-voltage insulation, Cooling system**.
- [x] **Line-focus principle** — angled anode face gives small apparent focal spot for image sharpness while spreading heat over a larger area.
- [x] **High-frequency generator** is the **modern standard** — ripple < 1 %, transformer ~1/10 size.
- [x] **X-ray generator evolution:** Single-phase → Three-phase (6/12-pulse) → High-frequency.
- [x] **Digital Radiography (DR)** replaces film with **flat-panel detectors (FPDs)** based on **TFT arrays**.
- [x] **Direct DR** uses **amorphous Selenium (a-Se)** photoconductor; **Indirect DR** uses a **scintillator (CsI:Tl)** + a-Si photodiodes.
- [x] **Computed Radiography (CR)** uses **photostimulable phosphor (PSP) plates** — transitional technology.
- [x] **Beer-Lambert Law:** $I = I_{0} e^{-\mu x}$ — **HVL** = $\dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$.
- [x] **Heat Units (HU):** $HU = V_{kV} \times I_{mA} \times t_{s}$ for single-phase full-wave.
- [x] **DQE, MTF, NPS** are the three key detector performance metrics — write the full forms and equations.
- [x] **DICOM** is the universal medical image storage standard.
- [x] Modern DR reduces patient dose by **50–70 %** compared to film-screen radiography.
- [x] **Safety constants to memorize:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, W melting point = **3422 °C**, W atomic number = **74**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** When answering any X-ray question in the ESE, always begin with a **neat block diagram** (worth 2–3 marks by itself), label every block, and follow up with the **relevant equations**. The examiner's reward pattern in KTU 2024 strongly favours *diagram + formula + numerical example* trios over lengthy theoretical essays.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: X-ray Computer Tomography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** X-ray Computer Tomography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Computed Tomography (CT)** — formally **X-ray Computed Tomography (X-CT or CT scan)** — is a non-invasive diagnostic imaging modality that uses computer-processed combinations of multiple X-ray projections taken from different angular orientations to produce **cross-sectional (tomographic) images** of specific regions of a scanned object, allowing the user to see inside the object without cutting it open.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Etymology (Board-Favourite One-Liner):**
> The word *Tomography* is derived from the Greek words **"tomos"** (meaning *slice* or *section*) and **"graphein"** (meaning *to write*). CT literally means "writing the slice."
>
> **Nobel Prize Connection:** CT was invented independently by **Sir Godfrey Newbold Hounsfield** (engineer at EMI Laboratories, UK) and **Allan MacLeod Cormack** (South African-American physicist). They shared the **Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1979**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Operating Principle:**
> CT uses the principle that different tissues in the body attenuate (absorb) X-rays by different amounts. By measuring these attenuations from many angles, a computer mathematically reconstructs a 2D cross-sectional map of the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x,y)$ inside the body.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Bread Loaf" Analogy (Examiner's Favourite Intuition Question)**
> Imagine a freshly baked loaf of bread with raisins embedded inside. You cannot see the raisins through the crust.
>
> - **Conventional X-ray** is like pressing the whole loaf flat against a window — the raisins' shadows overlap and you only see a confusing 2D projection.
> - **CT Scan** is like slicing the loaf into thin pieces with a knife. Each slice shows the raisins clearly with their exact positions.
> - Mathematically, CT reconstructs the "slice" *without actually slicing the patient*, by taking many "shadow" projections and mathematically inverting them (Radon Transform / Inverse Radon Transform).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> The following constants are used throughout CT physics and are frequently tested:
>
> - **Linear Attenuation Coefficient of Water:** $\mu_{\text{water}} \approx 0.195 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ at **80 keV effective energy** (or sometimes $0.206 \text{ cm}^{-1}$ at 73 keV).
> - **Standard CT Number Scale (Hounsfield Units):** $-1000$ HU for **air**, $0$ HU for **water**, $\approx +1000$ HU for **dense cortical bone**.
> - **Modern Clinical CT Slice Thickness:** $0.5 \text{ mm} – 10 \text{ mm}$.
> - **Tube Voltage Range:** $80 \text{ kVp} – 140 \text{ kVp}$.
> - **Tube Current Range:** $20 \text{ mA} – 800 \text{ mA}$ (modern modulated scanners).

### 1.4 Block Schematic of a CT System

A standard CT scanner consists of four major subsystems:

1. **Gantry Assembly** – Contains the rotating X-ray tube and detector array.
2. **X-ray Generation Subsystem** – High-voltage generator + X-ray tube (typically tungsten anode, rotating anode).
3. **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** – Detector array, analog front-end, A/D converters.
4. **Image Reconstruction Computer** – Performs filtered back-projection (or iterative reconstruction) to produce the image matrix (typically $512 \times 512$ pixels).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Basis: The Beer-Lambert Law

The X-ray beam is **monochromatic** (assumed) and travels along a straight line through tissue. Its intensity $I$ after passing through tissue of thickness $x$ with linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$ is given by the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$
I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}
$$

Where:

- $I_0$ = incident X-ray intensity
- $I$ = transmitted X-ray intensity
- $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹), depends on tissue density, atomic number, and photon energy
- $x$ = path length through tissue (cm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Heterogeneous Tissue Reality:**
> For a CT ray passing through many different tissues (e.g., bone → muscle → fat → lung → muscle), the total attenuation is the **line integral** of $\mu$ along the path:
> $$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\int_L \mu(x,y)\,ds}$$
> Taking the natural log gives the **projection** (or *radon transform*):
> $$p = \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \int_L \mu(x,y)\,ds$$
> CT reconstruction is the mathematical inverse of this operation.

---

### 2.2 Hounsfield Unit (CT Number)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Most Repeated Question in KTU Boards):**
> The **CT Number** (also called the **Hounsfield Unit, HU**) of a tissue is a normalized quantitative measure of radiodensity calculated as:
> $$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$
> where $\mu_{\text{tissue}}$ and $\mu_{\text{water}}$ are the linear attenuation coefficients of the tissue and water, respectively, measured under the same X-ray beam conditions.

This normalization makes CT numbers **independent of the X-ray beam energy**, allowing reproducible comparisons between scanners.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> **Note on formatting:** All absolute values are rendered with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering / Diagnostic Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Beer-Lambert Law** | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | $I, I_0$ in photons/s or dose rate | Base equation for all X-ray transmission imaging |
| 2 | **Projection (Radon Transform)** | $p = \ln(I_0 / I) = \int_L \mu \, ds$ | $p$ is dimensionless | Input data to reconstruction algorithm |
| 3 | **CT Number / HU** | $HU = 1000 \times \dfrac{\mu - \mu_w}{\mu_w}$ | Air: $-1000$, Water: $0$, Bone: $+1000$ | Tissue characterization; windowing; thresholding |
| 4 | **Mass Attenuation Coefficient** | $\mu_m = \mu / \rho$ | cm²/g | Independent of physical density |
| 5 | **Pixel Size (in-plane resolution)** | $\Delta x = \dfrac{\text{FOV}}{N}$ | e.g., $0.5 \text{ mm}$ for FOV $25 \text{ cm}$, $N=512$ | Determines spatial resolution |
| 6 | **Slice Sensitivity Profile (SSP)** | Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of slice profile | $0.5$–$10$ mm | Determines z-axis resolution |
| 7 | **Rose Criterion (Detectability)** | $C \sqrt{N} \geq k$ (k ≈ 3–5) | Dimensionless | Minimum contrast/noise for visible lesion |
| 8 | **Dose (CTDI)** | $\text{CTDI} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot T} \int_{-7T}^{+7T} D(z)\,dz$ | mGy | Radiation dose descriptor |
| 9 | **SNR (image)** | $\text{SNR} \propto \dfrac{\bar{N}}{\sigma_N}$ where $\bar{N}$ = mean photon counts | — | Image noise quality |
| 10 | **Pitch (Helical CT)** | $\text{Pitch} = \dfrac{\text{Table Travel per Rotation}}{\text{Total Beam Collimation}}$ | $0.5$–$2.0$ | Controls speed vs. dose vs. coverage |
| 11 | **Nyquist Frequency** | $f_{\text{Ny}} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot \Delta x}$ | cycles/mm | Maximum resolvable spatial frequency |

### 2.4 Why CT Number Matters in Engineering/Medicine

- **Quantitative Tissue Characterization:** Differentiates fat ($\approx -100$ HU), water ($0$), CSF ($\approx +15$), grey matter ($+35$ to $+45$), white matter ($+20$ to $+30$), blood clot ($+60$ to $+100$), and bone ($+700$ to $+3000$).
- **Image Windowing:** Allows clinicians to "window" the display (window width/level) to optimize contrast for specific tissues.
- **Therapy Planning:** Used in radiation oncology to compute electron density maps.
- **Perfusion & Angiography (CTA):** Dynamic CT measures HU vs. time to derive blood flow.

---

### 2.5 Generations of CT Scanners (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generation Classification** is a **guaranteed question** in Module 4. Memorize the following table.

| Generation | Scanning Motion | Beam Geometry | Detectors | Acquisition Time (Per Slice) | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st (1971)** | Translate–Rotate | Pencil beam | 1 | $4$–$5$ min | Hounsfield's original EMI scanner; two collimated pencil beams + two detectors |
| **2nd (1975)** | Translate–Rotate | Narrow fan beam | $5$–$30$ | $20$ s | Faster than 1st; still uses translation |
| **3rd (1976)** | Rotate–Rotate | Wide fan beam | $600$–$1000$ | $1$–$5$ s | Tube and detector array rotate together; **most common modern principle** |
| **4th (1978)** | Rotate (tube) + Stationary ring | Fan beam | $1200$–$4800$ fixed ring | $1$–$5$ s | Only tube rotates; detectors are stationary |
| **5th (1983)** | Electron beam scanning | Fan/parallel | Dual arrays | $50$ ms | Cardiac CT; no mechanical motion; electron beam steered onto tungsten anode ring |
| **6th (1989)** | **Helical / Spiral** | Fan beam | $3$rd/4$th gen type + slip ring | Continuous | Patient table moves while gantry rotates |
| **7th (1998+)** | **Multi-detector / Multi-slice (MDCT)** | Cone beam | $4, 16, 64, 128, 256, 320$ rows | $<1$ s whole body | Volumetric acquisition; isotropic voxels |

---

### 2.6 Image Reconstruction Algorithms

> **Why are reconstruction algorithms needed?**
> The detectors collect *projections* (line integrals). To obtain the actual image $\mu(x,y)$, we need an **inverse Radon transform**.

#### A. Simple Back-Projection (Conceptual Only — Has a Flaw)

For each projection angle $\theta$, "smear" the projection back across the image plane. Summing many smeared projections produces an image. **Problem:** Produces a blurred image with a characteristic $1/r$ halo (star artifact).

#### B. Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) — *Clinical Standard for 40+ years*

Apply a **high-pass filter** (e.g., Ram-Lak, Shepp-Logan, Hamming, Hann, Butterworth) to each projection *before* back-projecting. The filter removes the $1/r$ blur. Implemented via the **Central Slice Theorem (Fourier Slice Theorem)**.

#### C. Iterative Reconstruction (IR) — *Modern State of the Art*

Start with an initial guess image; simulate forward projections; compare with measured projections; update image; iterate. Methods: **ART (Algebraic Reconstruction Technique), SIRT, SART, MLEM, OSEM, and commercial variants like ASIR, SAFIRE, ADMIRE, iDose⁴, AIDR 3D**.

**Advantages of IR:** Lower dose, lower noise, better low-contrast detectability. **Disadvantage:** Computationally expensive (now feasible with GPU acceleration).

---

### 2.7 Image Quality Parameters

| Parameter | Definition | Typical Value | Influencing Factors |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spatial Resolution** | Smallest resolvable distance between two objects | $0.4$–$1.0$ lp/mm | Focal spot size, detector size, pixel size, reconstruction kernel |
| **Contrast Resolution** | Ability to distinguish small differences in attenuation | $3$–$5$ HU | Dose, noise, slice thickness, reconstruction algorithm |
| **Noise (σ)** | Standard deviation of HU in uniform ROI | $2$–$10$ HU | mAs, kVp, slice thickness, reconstruction algorithm |
| **Artifacts** | Any feature in image not present in object | Streak, beam hardening, motion, partial volume, ring, metal | Many — see Section 4 |
| **Linearity** | CT number vs. μ | Linear by definition | Calibration, beam hardening correction |

---

### 2.8 Helical / Spiral CT and Pitch

In helical CT, the patient table moves continuously while the gantry rotates. The **pitch** is defined as:

$$
\text{Pitch} = \frac{\text{Table Travel per 360° Rotation}}{\text{Total Beam Collimation (N × T)}}
$$

- **Pitch = 1.0** → contiguous slices
- **Pitch < 1.0** → overlapping (better z-resolution, higher dose)
- **Pitch > 1.0** → gaps (faster, lower dose, possible aliasing)

### 2.9 Multi-Detector CT (MDCT)

Uses **2D detector arrays** (e.g., $64 \times 0.625$ mm or $128 \times 0.6$ mm). Cone-beam geometry requires **3D reconstruction** algorithms (3D FBP, 3D iterative). Enables:

- Isotropic sub-millimeter voxels
- Whole-body scan in < 10 seconds
- Cardiac CT in single heartbeat
- CT angiography (CTA) and CT perfusion

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Beer-Lambert to CT Number

**Starting Point:** A monochromatic X-ray beam of intensity $I_0$ passes through a small slab of tissue of thickness $dx$ with linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x)$.

The fractional decrease in intensity is proportional to the path length and the local attenuation:

$$
dI = -\mu(x) \cdot I(x) \, dx
$$

**Step 1:** Separate variables to integrate.

$$
\frac{dI}{I(x)} = -\mu(x) \, dx
$$

**Step 2:** Integrate from $x=0$ to $x = L$, where $I(0) = I_0$ and $I(L) = I$.

$$
\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\int_{0}^{L} \mu(x) \, dx
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the left integral.

$$
\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -\int_{0}^{L} \mu(x) \, dx
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange.

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \int_{0}^{L} \mu(x) \, dx
$$

**Step 5 (Inversion):** The detector measures $I$ and we know $I_0$, so we can compute the **projection**:

$$
p \equiv \int_{0}^{L} \mu(x) \, dx = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)
$$

**Step 6 (CT Number Definition):** Normalize relative to water.

$$
HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: For a particular voxel, the measured projection through water (calibration scan) gives $\ln(I_0/I)_{\text{water}} = 1.56$, and through the tissue of interest gives $\ln(I_0/I)_{\text{tissue}} = 1.95$ over the same effective path length $L = 10$ cm. Find the CT number.

> **Step 7:** Solve for $\mu_{\text{water}}$.
> $$1.56 = \mu_{\text{water}} \times 10 \implies \mu_{\text{water}} = 0.156 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

> **Step 8:** Solve for $\mu_{\text{tissue}}$.
> $$1.95 = \mu_{\text{tissue}} \times 10 \implies \mu_{\text{tissue}} = 0.195 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

> **Step 9:** Substitute into CT number formula.
> $$HU = 1000 \times \frac{0.195 - 0.156}{0.156} = 1000 \times \frac{0.039}{0.156} \approx 250 \text{ HU}$$

> **Interpretation:** A CT number of $250$ HU corresponds approximately to **dense cancellous bone** or **highly vascularized tumor tissue** (compare: blood clot $\approx +60$ to $+100$, cortical bone $+700$ to $+3000$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Pitch on Effective Slice Thickness (Helical CT)

For a helical CT with single detector row of collimation $T$, slice sensitivity profile (SSP) is broadened by table motion during rotation. The effective slice sensitivity profile along z is:

$$
\text{SSP}_{\text{eff}}(z) \approx T \cdot \sqrt{1 + \frac{p^2}{(N \cdot T)^2} \cdot z_{\text{rot}}^2}
$$

For practical purposes, the effective slice thickness is:

$$
T_{\text{eff}} \approx \frac{T \cdot \text{Pitch}}{\sqrt{1 + (\pi \cdot p)^2 / K}}
$$

where $p$ is pitch and $K$ depends on interpolation algorithm (180° or 360° linear interpolation, or more advanced  $z$-filtering).

**Simplified empirical rule (commonly used in clinical practice):**

$$
T_{\text{eff}} \approx \text{Pitch} \times T
$$

This is a useful first-order approximation: at **pitch = 1.0**, effective slice thickness equals nominal collimation; at **pitch = 1.5**, slices are 50% wider.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Noise in CT Images (Quantum Limited Case)

The dominant noise source in CT is **quantum (Poisson) noise** from finite photon statistics. If $N$ photons are detected per detector element per projection, the standard deviation of $\mu$ estimates is:

$$
\sigma_{\mu} = \frac{\mu}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

Because CT number is linear in $\mu$, the noise in HU is:

$$
\sigma_{HU} = \frac{1000 \cdot \mu_{\text{tissue}}}{\mu_{\text{water}} \cdot \sqrt{N}}
$$

**Key Engineering Implication:**
$$\sigma_{HU} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\text{mAs}}}$$

So **quadrupling** the mAs halves the image noise. This is the central trade-off in CT dose optimization.

---

### 3.4 Reconstruction: Central Slice Theorem (Fourier Slice Theorem) — Conceptual Derivation

**Statement:** The 1D Fourier transform of a parallel-beam projection $p_\theta(s)$ acquired at angle $\theta$ equals a 1D slice through the 2D Fourier transform of the object $f(x,y)$ along the same angle.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$
\mathcal{F}_1\{p_\theta(s)\} = F(\omega \cos\theta, \omega \sin\theta)
$$

**Where:**

- $\mathcal{F}_1$ denotes 1D Fourier transform along $s$ (detector position)
- $F(u,v)$ is the 2D Fourier transform of the object
- $\omega$ is the spatial frequency along the detector

**Proof Sketch:**

The projection at angle $\theta$ is:

$$
p_\theta(s) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} f(s\cos\theta - t\sin\theta,\; s\sin\theta + t\cos\theta)\,dt
$$

where $(s,t)$ is a rotated coordinate system. Take the 1D Fourier transform of $p_\theta(s)$:

$$
P_\theta(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} p_\theta(s)\,e^{-j\omega s}\,ds
$$

Substitute and change variables $(s,t) \to (x,y)$ via rotation matrix. After algebraic manipulation, one obtains:

$$
P_\theta(\omega) = F(\omega\cos\theta,\; \omega\sin\theta)
$$

**Implication for FBP:** If we collect projections at all angles $\theta \in [0, \pi)$, the union of all $P_\theta(\omega)$ values covers the entire 2D frequency plane $(u,v)$. So $F(u,v)$ is fully known, and we can invert via 2D inverse FFT, *or* equivalently, filter each projection and back-project. Both are mathematically equivalent.

---

### 3.5 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) for a Lesion of Contrast C Against Background of Attenuation μ_b

For an object of diameter $d$ and contrast $C$ (in HU), embedded in background of HU $\mu_b$, observed with slice thickness $T$ and dose $D$:

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{C \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{T \cdot D}}{k}
$$

where $k$ is a constant that depends on system geometry and reconstruction algorithm.

**Rose Criterion (Detectability threshold):**

$$
\text{SNR} \geq 3 \text{ to } 5 \quad \text{(for reliable lesion detection)}
$$

This is used in **low-dose CT protocols** to balance image quality against radiation dose.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level CT System Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HV["High Voltage Generator 80 to 140 kVp"]
    XRT["X ray Tube Rotating Tungsten Anode"]
    COLL["Beam Collimation System"]
    PAT["Patient on Motorized Couch"]
    FILT["Bow Tie Filter Compensates Body Shape"]
    DET["Detector Array Solid State Scintillator plus Photodiode"]
    DAS["Data Acquisition System DAS and ADC"]
    REC["Reconstruction Computer FBP or Iterative"]
    DISP["Display Console 512 by 512 Matrix"]
    ARCH["PACS Archival"]

    HV --> XRT
    XRT --> COLL
    COLL --> FILT
    FILT --> PAT
    PAT --> DET
    DET --> DAS
    DAS --> REC
    REC --> DISP
    DISP --> ARCH
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **HV generator** energizes the **X-ray tube**, producing a polyenergetic fan-beam.
- The **bow-tie filter** pre-attenuates peripheral rays so the detector receives approximately uniform flux (reduces dynamic range, lowers skin dose).
- After attenuation through the patient, the **detector array** converts photons to electrical signals.
- The **DAS** digitizes, applies corrections (dark current, gain, beam hardening), and feeds **sinogram** data to the reconstruction computer.
- The **reconstruction computer** applies FBP or IR to produce a $512 \times 512$ image, displayed and archived.

---

### 4.2 Data Flow from Detector to Image (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Scan Acquisition Projections at Multiple Angles"]
    B["Sinogram Generation 2D Plot of Detector Column versus Projection Angle"]
    C["Preprocessing Log Amplification, Beam Hardening Correction, Scatter Correction"]
    D["Reconstruction Filtered Back Projection FBP or Iterative IR"]
    E["Image Matrix 512 by 512 Pixel HU Values"]
    F["Post Processing Multiplanar Reformats MPR, 3D Volume Rendering, MIP"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> A **sinogram** is the raw data representation: each row is the detector readings for one projection angle. Plotting all rows together forms the characteristic sinusoidal pattern, hence the name.

---

### 4.3 Generations Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G1["1st Generation Translate Rotate Pencil Beam 1 Detector 4 to 5 min per slice"]
    G2["2nd Generation Translate Rotate Fan Beam 5 to 30 Detectors 20 s per slice"]
    G3["3rd Generation Rotate Rotate Wide Fan Beam 600 to 1000 Detectors 1 to 5 s"]
    G4["4th Generation Rotate Stationary 1200 to 4800 Detectors in Ring 1 to 5 s"]
    G5["5th Generation Electron Beam CT Cardiac Imaging 50 ms per slice"]
    G6["6th Generation Helical Spiral CT Slip Ring Continuous Table Motion"]
    G7["7th Generation Multi Detector CT MDCT 64 to 320 Slices Cone Beam 3D"]

    G1 --> G2
    G2 --> G3
    G3 --> G4
    G4 --> G5
    G5 --> G6
    G6 --> G7
```

---

### 4.4 Hounsfield Scale Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Air minus 1000 HU"]
    B["Fat minus 100 HU"]
    C["Water 0 HU"]
    D["CSF plus 15 HU"]
    E["White Matter plus 20 to 30 HU"]
    F["Grey Matter plus 35 to 45 HU"]
    G["Blood Clot plus 60 to 100 HU"]
    H["Muscle plus 35 to 55 HU"]
    I["Bone Cortical plus 700 to 3000 HU"]

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H --> I
```

> This linear scale is **calibrated such that water = 0 HU** and **air = $-1000$ HU** under the reference scanning protocol of $120$ kVp.

---

### 4.5 Helical CT Geometry (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["X ray Source Rotates around Z axis"]
    H2["Detector Array Opposite to Source"]
    H3["Table Moves Continuously at Velocity v"]
    H4["Beam Describes Helical Path around Patient"]

    H1 --> H4
    H2 --> H4
    H3 --> H4
```

> Pitch = $v \cdot T_{\text{rot}} / (N \cdot T)$ where $v$ is table velocity, $T_{\text{rot}}$ is rotation period, $N \cdot T$ is total collimation.

---

### 4.6 Common CT Artifacts (Mermaid Mind Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ART["CT Artifacts"]
    ART --> ST["Streak Artifacts Beam Hardening High Density Object"]
    ART --> MT["Motion Artifact Patient Breathing or Heartbeat"]
    ART --> RV["Ring Artifact Mis-calibrated Detector Element"]
    ART --> PV["Partial Volume Artifact Voxel Spans Multiple Tissues"]
    ART --> WL["Windmill Artifact Helical CT at High Pitch"]
    ART --> ZA["Zebra Artifact Helical Pitch Mismatch"]
    ART --> META["Metal Artifact High Density Metal Implant"]
    ART --> AL["Aliasing Under-sampling of Projections"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define CT number (Hounsfield Unit). What are the CT numbers of air, water, and cortical bone?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:**
> The CT number (Hounsfield Unit, HU) of a tissue is a quantitative, normalized measure of its radiodensity, calculated relative to water attenuation:
> $$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$
>
> **Standard Values:**
>
> | Tissue | CT Number (HU) |
> |---|---|
> | Air | $-1000$ |
> | Water | $0$ |
> | Cortical Bone | $+700$ to $+3000$ |
>
> **Key Point:** Water is defined as $0$ HU because the scale is normalized to water. Air is $-1000$ HU by definition (almost zero attenuation).

---

#### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**List the different generations of CT scanners and briefly mention the key advancement of 3rd generation CT.**

**Model Answer:**

> CT scanners have evolved through **7 generations**:
>
> 1. **1st Gen** – Translate-rotate, pencil beam, 1 detector.
> 2. **2nd Gen** – Translate-rotate, narrow fan beam, 5–30 detectors.
> 3. **3rd Gen** – **Rotate-rotate**, wide fan beam, 600–1000 detectors.
> 4. **4th Gen** – Rotate (tube only), stationary ring of detectors.
> 5. **5th Gen** – Electron beam CT (no mechanical motion).
> 6. **6th Gen** – **Helical/Spiral CT** with slip-ring and continuous table motion.
> 7. **7th Gen** – **Multi-detector CT (MDCT)** with cone-beam geometry.
>
> **Key Advancement of 3rd Generation CT:**
> The X-ray tube and the detector array rotate together around the patient, eliminating the need for translational motion. This reduced scan time from minutes (1st/2nd gen) to **1–5 seconds per slice**, enabling routine clinical body imaging and reducing motion artifacts.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU Pattern):** Answer **both** sub-parts. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Use diagrams and equations wherever necessary.

---

#### **Question B1 (14 Marks) — Option A**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style QP]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the CT number (Hounsfield Unit) of a tissue, starting from the Beer-Lambert law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the 3rd generation and 4th generation CT scanners with neat block diagrams. Explain the principle of helical CT and define pitch. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for B1(a):**

**Step 1: Start from Beer-Lambert Law.**
For an X-ray beam passing through a small slice of tissue:
$$I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$$

**Step 2: For a heterogeneous medium**, the beam passes through many tissues of different attenuation along a line $L$:
$$I = I_0 e^{-\int_L \mu(x,y)\,ds}$$

**Step 3: Take the natural logarithm** to obtain the projection:
$$p = \ln(I_0 / I) = \int_L \mu(x,y)\,ds$$

**Step 4: Calibrate using water** (a reference scan with only water in the path):
$$\int_L \mu_{\text{water}}\,ds = p_w = \ln(I_{0,w}/I_w)$$

**Step 5: The CT number is defined** as the difference normalized to water:
$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

**Step 6: In practice, after reconstruction**, each voxel in the image is assigned a HU value using this formula, allowing direct tissue characterization (air $\approx -1000$, water $\approx 0$, bone $\approx +1000$).

---

**Model Solution for B1(b):**

| Parameter | 3rd Generation CT | 4th Generation CT |
|---|---|---|
| Motion | Tube + detector array both rotate | Only tube rotates; detector ring is stationary |
| Beam | Wide fan beam | Fan beam |
| Detectors | ~600–1000 in curved array | ~1200–4800 in stationary ring |
| Scan Time | 1–5 s/slice | 1–5 s/slice |
| Slip Ring | Required | Required |
| Ring Artifacts | Can occur | Possible (if detector drifts) |
| Cost | Moderate | Higher (many detectors) |

**Helical CT Principle:**
The patient table translates continuously while the gantry rotates, producing a **helical (spiral) X-ray source path** relative to the patient. Slip-ring technology allows continuous rotation without cables tangling.

**Pitch Definition:**
$$\text{Pitch} = \frac{\text{Table Travel per Rotation}}{\text{Total Beam Collimation}}$$

- Pitch = 1.0 → contiguous
- Pitch > 1.0 → faster, lower dose, wider effective slices
- Pitch < 1.0 → overlapping, better z-resolution, higher dose

---

#### **Question B2 (14 Marks) — Option B**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style QP]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the working of a CT scanner. List the major components and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**. Why is simple back-projection insufficient? What role does the **Central Slice Theorem** play in CT reconstruction? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for B2(a):**

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1):**

```
HV Generator → X-ray Tube → Collimator → Bow-Tie Filter →
Patient → Detector Array → DAS + ADC → Reconstruction Computer →
Display Console → PACS
```

**Major Components and Functions:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High-Voltage Generator** | Produces 80–140 kVp to drive the X-ray tube |
| 2 | **X-ray Tube** | Generates X-ray photons (rotating anode, tungsten target) |
| 3 | **Collimator** | Restricts beam to desired fan/cone geometry; reduces scatter |
| 4 | **Bow-Tie Filter** | Pre-attenuates peripheral rays; flattens detector dynamic range |
| 5 | **Patient Couch** | Translates patient through gantry bore; precision-controlled |
| 6 | **Detector Array** | Converts transmitted X-rays to electrical signals (scintillator + photodiode) |
| 7 | **DAS + ADC** | Amplifies, integrates, digitizes detector signals |
| 8 | **Reconstruction Computer** | Applies FBP or IR; outputs image matrix |
| 9 | **Display Console** | Renders 512×512 HU-mapped image |
| 10 | **PACS** | Archives and transmits images |

**Working:** The patient lies on the couch. The X-ray tube and detector rotate around the patient, acquiring projections at multiple angles. These projections are processed and reconstructed into cross-sectional images of the body.

---

**Model Solution for B2(b):**

**Simple Back-Projection (SBP) — Why it is insufficient:**

> For each projection angle $\theta$, the projection $p_\theta(s)$ is "smeared" (back-projected) uniformly along its ray paths. Adding the smeared images from all angles produces a reconstructed image.
>
> **Problem:** SBP produces a **blurred image** with a characteristic $1/r$ blurring kernel (the point spread function of back-projection). High-frequency details are washed out; image looks "fuzzy."

**Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) — The Solution:**

> Before back-projecting each projection, apply a **high-pass ramp filter** in the frequency domain (e.g., Ram-Lak, Shepp-Logan). The filter compensates for the $1/r$ blur of SBP.
>
> Steps:
> 1. Take 1D Fourier transform of projection $p_\theta(s)$.
> 2. Multiply by ramp filter $\vert \omega \vert$ (or smoother windowed version).
> 3. Inverse Fourier transform to obtain **filtered projection**.
> 4. Back-project the filtered projection across the image.
> 5. Repeat for all angles and sum.

**Role of Central Slice Theorem (CST):**

The CST states:
$$\mathcal{F}_1\{p_\theta(s)\} = F(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)$$

That is, the 1D Fourier transform of a projection at angle $\theta$ is a 1D slice through the 2D Fourier transform of the object along the same angle. This means:

- Collecting projections at all $\theta \in [0, \pi)$ is equivalent to filling the entire 2D Fourier space $F(u,v)$ of the object.
- Image reconstruction is then a 2D inverse Fourier transform, which can be performed either directly (slow) or via back-projection of filtered projections (fast, equivalent mathematically).

The CST provides the **theoretical justification** for FBP and is the cornerstone of analytic CT reconstruction.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in CT Questions:**
>
> 1. **CT Number Formula Sign Error:** Students often write $HU = 1000 \times \dfrac{\mu_w - \mu_{\text{tissue}}}{\mu_w}$ (wrong sign). Correct: $HU = 1000 \times \dfrac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_w}{\mu_w}$. A dense tissue (high $\mu$) must yield **positive** HU; air (low $\mu$) yields **negative** HU.
> 2. **Forgetting the Natural Log in Beer-Lambert:** Many students write $I = I_0 \cdot \mu x$ (linear). The correct form is **exponential**: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.
> 3. **Confusing Pitch Definition:** Some textbooks define pitch as the inverse ratio. KTU standard definition (above) is **table travel / total collimation**. Use the standard.
> 4. **Mixing up 3rd and 4th Generation:** In **3rd gen**, both tube AND detectors rotate. In **4th gen**, only the tube rotates (detectors stationary in a ring). Reversing these is a 7-mark killer in Part B.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" of FBP:** Examiners will not give full marks for just stating "filter then back-project." You MUST explain **why** (the $1/r$ blur of SBP requires correction) and connect it to the **Central Slice Theorem**.
> 6. **Units of HU:** CT number is **dimensionless** by definition (it's a ratio). Don't write "HU per cm" or any unit — just "HU."
> 7. **Tissue CT Values:** Memorize **air $(-1000)$, water $(0)$, fat $(-100)$, muscle $(+40)$, blood $(+40)$, bone $(+700$ to $+3000)$**. Mixing these up loses marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — X-ray Computed Tomography**

- [ ] **CT Number / Hounsfield Unit** formula and sign convention:
  $$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_w}{\mu_w}$$
- [ ] **Standard HU reference values:** Air $= -1000$, Water $= 0$, Fat $\approx -100$, Muscle/Blood $\approx +40$, Cortical Bone $\approx +1000$.
- [ ] **Beer-Lambert Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — *exponential*, not linear.
- [ ] **Radon transform** = projection; CT reconstruction = inverse Radon transform.
- [ ] **Central Slice Theorem (CST):** 1D Fourier transform of projection = slice through 2D Fourier transform of object.
- [ ] **FBP > SBP:** FBP applies a high-pass ramp filter to remove the $1/r$ blur of SBP.
- [ ] **Iterative Reconstruction (IR):** Modern lower-dose alternative (ART, SIRT, MLEM, OSEM, ASIR, SAFIRE).
- [ ] **7 Generations of CT** — know motion type, beam geometry, detector count, and scan time for each.
- [ ] **3rd Gen** = rotate–rotate; **4th Gen** = rotate (tube) + stationary detector ring.
- [ ] **Helical CT:** Continuous table motion + rotating gantry + slip-ring technology.
- [ ] **Pitch:** $p = \dfrac{\text{Table Travel per Rotation}}{\text{Total Beam Collimation}}$.
- [ ] **MDCT (7th Gen):** Multiple detector rows (64, 128, 256, 320), cone-beam geometry, 3D reconstruction.
- [ ] **Electron Beam CT (5th Gen):** No mechanical motion; used for cardiac imaging; ~50 ms temporal resolution.
- [ ] **Image Quality Parameters:** Spatial resolution, contrast resolution, noise $\sigma_{HU} \propto 1/\sqrt{\text{mAs}}$, artifacts, linearity.
- [ ] **CT Artifacts:** Streak, beam hardening, motion, ring, partial volume, metal, windmill, aliasing.
- [ ] **Pitch & Slice Thickness:** $T_{\text{eff}} \approx \text{Pitch} \times T_{\text{nominal}}$ (first-order).
- [ ] **Dose Metric:** CTDI (CT Dose Index), DLP, Effective Dose.
- [ ] **Nobel Laureates:** Hounsfield & Cormack, **1979 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine**.
- [ ] **Rose Criterion:** $\text{SNR} \geq 3$ to $5$ for reliable lesion detection.
- [ ] **Clinical Windowing:** Window width (WW) and window level (WL) control displayed HU range.
- [ ] **Modern scanner types:** Dual-source CT (Siemens FLASH), 320-row Toshiba Aquilion ONE, photon-counting CT (Siemens NAEOTOM Alpha) — research and viva favourites.

---

> **End of Module 4 — X-ray Computed Tomography Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear Medical Imaging Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nuclear Medical Imaging Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Nuclear Medical Imaging (NMI)** is a branch of medical imaging that uses **radiopharmaceuticals** (radioactive tracers administered into the patient's body) and external radiation detectors to produce functional, metabolic, and physiological images of organs and tissues. Unlike X-ray or CT imaging, which primarily reveal *anatomy*, NMI visualises *function* by detecting gamma photons emitted from within the body.

The two principal modalities covered under this domain in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

- **SPECT (Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography)** — uses single gamma-ray emitters (e.g., **Tc-99m**, half-life ≈ **6 hours**).
- **PET (Positron Emission Tomography)** — uses positron-emitting radioisotopes (e.g., **F-18** as fluorodeoxyglucose, half-life ≈ **110 minutes**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "The Glowing Sugar Cubes":** Imagine a patient eating a *glowing sugar cube* (the radiotracer). The body cannot tell the difference between ordinary sugar and the radioactive sugar, so the cube travels exactly the same biochemical path. Wherever metabolism is high (e.g., a tumour, the brain, the heart), the glowing cubes cluster. A sensitive camera placed *outside* the body photographs the glow and reveals *where* and *how strongly* the body is using energy. The camera is non-invasive; the only "messenger" is invisible light (gamma rays).

In simpler terms:

- **X-ray / CT** = "Taking a photograph of the body's *shape*."
- **MRI** = "Mapping the body's *water content*."
- **Ultrasound** = "Listening to the body's *echoes*."
- **Nuclear Imaging (SPECT/PET)** = "Watching the body's *chemistry in action*."

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants and Units

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Speed of light (in vacuum) | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}}$ |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J\cdot s}}$ |
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | $\mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}}$ |
| 1 Curie (activity unit) | Ci | $\mathbf{3.7 \times 10^{10}\ \text{decays/s}}$ |
| 1 Becquerel (SI unit) | Bq | $\mathbf{1\ \text{decay/s}}$ |
| Electron rest mass | $m_e c^{2}$ | $\mathbf{511\ \text{keV}}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 511 keV?** When a positron ($e^{+}$) meets an electron ($e^{-}$) inside the body, they **annihilate**. By Einstein's mass-energy equivalence, the rest mass of an electron-positron pair is converted into *two* 511 keV gamma photons emitted in opposite directions (≈ 180° apart). PET scanners exploit this strict back-to-back geometry for *electronic collimation*.

## 1.4 Why Nuclear Imaging Matters

- Detects disease at the **cellular / metabolic level**, often *years* before structural changes appear.
- Indispensable in **oncology** (cancer staging), **cardiology** (myocardial perfusion), and **neurology** (Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, epilepsy).
- Quantitative — produces **Standardised Uptake Values (SUV)** in addition to visual images.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Radioactive Decay

The nucleus of an unstable isotope (radionuclide) spontaneously emits particles or photons to reach a more stable state. The relevant decay modes for medical imaging are:

- **Isomeric transition (γ-emission):** The nucleus drops from a metastable excited state to a lower state, releasing a **gamma photon**. The nucleus does not change its proton number — only its energy. This is the mechanism in **SPECT** (e.g., Tc-99m).
- **β⁺ decay (positron emission):** A proton-rich nucleus converts a proton into a neutron, a positron, and a neutrino. The positron annihilates with a nearby electron to produce two 511 keV photons. This is the mechanism in **PET** (e.g., F-18, C-11, N-13, O-15).
- **β⁻ decay (electron emission):** A neutron converts to a proton + electron + antineutrino. Used less commonly in imaging (e.g., therapeutic I-131, diagnostic I-131 thyroid uptake).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Always remember that **Tc-99m** is the *workhorse* of SPECT because of its ideal 140 keV gamma energy (good for collimator penetration & NaI crystal detection), short half-life (6 hours, low patient dose), and easy on-site production from a **Mo-99/Tc-99m generator**.

## 2.2 The Radioactive Decay Equation

Let $N(t)$ be the number of undecayed nuclei at time $t$. Each nucleus has a fixed *probability* per unit time of decaying — the **decay constant** $\lambda$ (units: s⁻¹). The rate of decay is therefore:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$$

Solving this first-order ordinary differential equation with the initial condition $N(0) = N_0$ yields the famous **exponential decay law**.

## 2.3 Activity (A) — The Clinically Useful Quantity

The **activity** $A$ is the *number of decays per second* in a sample. By definition:

$$A = \lambda N$$

Because $N$ decreases exponentially, activity also decreases exponentially. The clinical unit of activity is the **Becquerel (Bq)** in the SI system and the **Curie (Ci)** in the older system.

$$1\ \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Bq}$$

## 2.4 Half-Life Derivation Concept

The **half-life** $T_{1/2}$ is the time required for activity to fall to half its initial value. Setting $N(T_{1/2}) = N_0/2$ in the decay equation gives the cornerstone relation:

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mean (Average) Life:** $\tau = 1/\lambda = T_{1/2}/0.693$. The mean life is the average lifetime of a single radioactive atom before it decays.

## 2.5 Attenuation of Gamma Photons in Tissue

When a gamma photon travels through tissue, the **Beer–Lambert Law** governs its attenuation:

$$I(x) = I_0\, e^{-\mu x}$$

where $\mu$ is the **linear attenuation coefficient** (units: cm⁻¹ or m⁻¹) and $x$ is the path length. Half-value layer (HVL) is the thickness that reduces intensity by half:

$$\text{HVL} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering reality:** A typical NaI(Tl) crystal in a gamma camera is only about **9.5 mm thick**. To stop 140 keV photons with high efficiency, the crystal must be dense — hence the use of **thallium-doped sodium iodide** (ρ ≈ 3.67 g/cm³, high atomic number of iodine Z = 53).

## 2.6 Collimator Physics — The Heart of SPECT

Because gamma photons are emitted isotropically, a **collimator** is required to project them onto the detector like a lens. A collimator is essentially a lead plate perforated by thousands of parallel holes. Only photons travelling nearly parallel to the holes reach the crystal — the rest are absorbed in the lead septa. The **geometric efficiency** $\eta$ of a parallel-hole collimator is:

$$\eta = \frac{K^{2}}{(K + d + t)^{2}}$$

where $K$ = hole length, $d$ = hole diameter, $t$ = septal thickness, and $K^{2}$ is the hole cross-section. **Resolution and efficiency trade off inversely** — making holes longer (higher $K$) improves resolution but drastically reduces sensitivity.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Number of undecayed nuclei at time $t$ | dimensionless count |
| 2 | $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Activity at time $t$ | Bq or Ci |
| 3 | $A = \lambda N$ | Activity = decay constant × number of atoms | Bq |
| 4 | $T_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \lambda$ | Half-life in terms of decay constant | seconds |
| 5 | $\tau = 1/\lambda$ | Mean life of a radioactive atom | seconds |
| 6 | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | Gamma photon attenuation (Beer–Lambert) | photons/s |
| 7 | $\text{HVL} = \ln 2 / \mu$ | Half-value layer of absorber | cm or m |
| 8 | $\eta = K^{2}/(K + d + t)^{2}$ | Geometric efficiency of parallel collimator | dimensionless (fraction) |
| 9 | $E = mc^{2} = 2m_e c^{2} = 1022\ \text{keV}$ | Annihilation photon energy (2 × 511 keV) | keV |
| 10 | $\text{SUV} = c_{\text{tissue}} / (A_{\text{injected}}/m_{\text{patient}})$ | Standardised Uptake Value (PET) | g/mL |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students frequently confuse $A = \lambda N$ with $A = \lambda N_0$. Remember — the activity at *time t* uses the *current* number of atoms $N(t)$, not the initial $N_0$. Using $N_0$ is correct **only at t = 0**.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital nuclear medicine departments** use these equations daily to calculate patient radiation dose, calibrate dose calibrators (Capintec CRC-25), and schedule imaging time after injection.
- **Radiopharmacy cyclotrons** rely on $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ to schedule the synthesis of F-18-FDG (a short-lived PET tracer) just minutes before patient injection.
- **Image reconstruction** algorithms (Filtered Back-Projection, OSEM) mathematically invert the *attenuated* projections using the Beer–Lambert relationship.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Radioactive Decay Law (Exponential Decay)

**Step 0 — Empirical fact:** A large population of identical radioactive nuclei does *not* decay all at once. Each individual atom has a fixed probability per unit time of decaying, regardless of its age. The probability per unit time is the **decay constant** $\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Express the rate of change:** In a small time interval $dt$, the expected number of decays is $\lambda N(t)\,dt$. Thus the change in the number of undecayed nuclei is:

$$dN = -\lambda N(t)\,dt$$

The negative sign indicates that $N$ decreases as $t$ increases.

**Step 2 — Separate variables:** Rearrange the equation so that all $N$ terms are on the left and all $t$ terms are on the right:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides from time $0$ to $t$:** Apply initial condition $N(0) = N_0$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals:** The left side gives $\ln N \big\vert_{N_0}^{N(t)} = \ln[N(t)] - \ln N_0 = \ln[N(t)/N_0]$. The right side gives $-\lambda t$.

$$\ln\left[\frac{N(t)}{N_0}\right] = -\lambda t$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate both sides to remove the logarithm:**

$$N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the **fundamental law of radioactive decay**. Multiplying both sides by $\lambda$ (and using $A = \lambda N$) gives the activity form:

$$A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Half-Life from the Decay Law

**Step 1 — Definition of half-life:** By definition, at $t = T_{1/2}$, the activity (or number of remaining atoms) falls to *half* of its initial value. Therefore:

$$A(T_{1/2}) = \frac{A_0}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the decay law:**

$$\frac{A_0}{2} = A_0 e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $A_0$ (which is non-zero):**

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

**Step 5 — Use the identity $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2$:**

$$-\ln 2 = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel the negatives and solve for $T_{1/2}$:**

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} \approx \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

This is the **cornerstone formula** linking the microscopic decay constant $\lambda$ to the experimentally measured half-life $T_{1/2}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean (Average) Life of a Radioactive Atom

The *mean life* $\tau$ is the average lifetime of an individual atom in a large population, given by:

$$\tau = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$$

where $f(t)\,dt$ is the probability that an atom survives to time $t$ and decays in the next $dt$. The survival function is $e^{-\lambda t}$ and the conditional decay probability is $\lambda\,dt$, so $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\tau = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 1 — Apply integration by parts with $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\,dt$:** Then $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$.

$$\tau = \left[-t e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — The boundary term:** As $t \to \infty$, $t e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$ (exponential decay dominates). At $t=0$, the term is 0. So the first part vanishes.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the remaining integral:**

$$\tau = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \left[\frac{-1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Therefore $\tau = 1/\lambda$, and combining with $T_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \lambda$ gives the elegant ratio:

$$\tau = \frac{T_{1/2}}{\ln 2} \approx 1.44\, T_{1/2}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gamma Photon Attenuation (Beer–Lambert Law)

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a narrow beam of monoenergetic gamma photons with intensity $I_0$ (photons per second) entering an absorber of thickness $x$. Let $\mu$ be the probability per unit length that a photon undergoes an interaction (photoelectric, Compton, or pair production).

**Step 2 — Differential equation:** In a thin slice $dx$, the fractional loss in intensity is:

$$dI = -\mu I(x)\, dx$$

**Step 3 — Separate and integrate:** Same procedure as the decay derivation:

$$\int_{I_0}^{I(x)} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_{0}^{x} dx$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$\ln\!\left[\frac{I(x)}{I_0}\right] = -\mu x$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate:**

$$I(x) = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$$

**Step 6 — Half-value layer:** Setting $I(\text{HVL}) = I_0/2$:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\mu\,\text{HVL}} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \text{HVL} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Standardised Uptake Value (SUV) in PET

PET images report *quantitative* metabolic information via the **SUV**:

$$\text{SUV} = \frac{c_{\text{tissue}}}{c_{\text{whole-body avg}}} = \frac{c_{\text{tissue}}}{A_{\text{injected}} / m_{\text{patient}}}$$

**Derivation rationale:** If the tracer were *uniformly* distributed throughout the body, the concentration in any tissue would equal $A_{\text{injected}} / m_{\text{patient}}$ (decay-corrected to scan time). SUV is therefore the *fold-difference* between actual local uptake and this hypothetical uniform distribution. A region with SUV > 2.5 is clinically suspicious for malignancy in FDG-PET oncology.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A patient is injected with **20 mCi** of Tc-99m. The imaging begins **2 hours** after injection. If the half-life of Tc-99m is **6 hours**, calculate the activity remaining at the start of imaging. (1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq.)

**Solution:**

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{6\ \text{h}} = 0.1155\ \text{h}^{-1}$$

$$A(2) = A_0 e^{-\lambda \cdot 2} = 20\ \text{mCi} \cdot e^{-0.1155 \times 2} = 20 \cdot e^{-0.231}$$

$$e^{-0.231} \approx 0.794$$

$$A(2) \approx 20 \times 0.794 = 15.88\ \text{mCi}$$

**Answer:** Approximately **15.9 mCi** of Tc-99m remains at scan time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Gamma Camera (SPECT Head) Architecture

The gamma camera (also called the Anger camera, after its inventor Hal Anger) is the workhorse detector of SPECT. It uses a large-area NaI(Tl) scintillation crystal viewed by an array of photomultiplier tubes (PMTs). The light distribution among PMTs is processed to compute the $X$ and $Y$ position of the interaction and the deposited energy $Z$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    patA[Patient with radiotracer] --> collA[Lead collimator with parallel holes]
    collA --> scinA[NaI Tl scintillation crystal 9.5 mm thick]
    scinA --> pmtA1[PMT 1]
    scinA --> pmtA2[PMT 2]
    scinA --> pmtA3[PMT 3]
    scinA --> pmtA4[PMT 4]
    pmtA1 --> preA[Preamplifier]
    pmtA2 --> preA
    pmtA3 --> preA
    pmtA4 --> preA
    preA --> posA[X Y position logic circuit]
    preA --> sumA[Summing amplifier Z pulse]
    posA --> pca[Pulse height analyser PHA window]
    sumA --> pca
    pca --> dacA[Digital to analog converter and image memory]
    dacA --> monA[Display monitor and computer]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram (left → right):** Gamma photons leave the patient's body → pass through the lead collimator (which removes off-axis photons) → strike the NaI(Tl) crystal → produce flashes of visible light → light is collected by the PMT array → PMT signals are amplified → the position logic computes the X, Y location of the scintillation event → the summing amplifier produces a Z pulse proportional to deposited energy → the PHA filters out Compton-scattered photons → the digital image is stored and displayed.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — PET Scanner Architecture

A PET scanner detects *two* 511 keV annihilation photons in *coincidence*. Multiple rings of detectors surround the patient. A coincidence event is recorded only when two detectors on opposite sides of the patient fire within a few nanoseconds of each other. This **electronic collimation** eliminates the need for a physical lead collimator and gives PET much higher sensitivity than SPECT.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    patB[Patient with F 18 FDG] --> ringB1[Detector ring 1 BGO or LSO crystals]
    patB --> ringB2[Detector ring 2]
    patB --> ringB3[Detector ring 3]
    patB --> ringB4[Detector ring 4]
    ringB1 --> coinB[Coincidence processor window 6 to 12 ns]
    ringB2 --> coinB
    ringB3 --> coinB
    ringB4 --> coinB
    coinB --> sortB[Sinogram data sorting]
    sortB --> recoB[Image reconstruction OSEM or FBP]
    recoB --> atnB[Attenuation correction CT or C 137 source]
    atnB --> dispB[Quantified SUV image on display]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Radiopharmaceutical Synthesis & Imaging Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cycA[Cyclotron or Mo 99 generator] --> radA[Radiochemistry synthesis of radiotracer]
    radA --> calA[Dose calibrator activity and purity check]
    calA --> injA[Intravenous injection into patient]
    injA --> waitA[Uptake period 30 to 90 minutes]
    waitA --> scanA[SPECT or PET scanner image acquisition]
    scanA --> recoA[Reconstruction FBP or OSEM]
    recoA --> viewA[Physician interpretation and clinical report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this workflow important for KTU exams?** The KTU board examiner frequently asks students to *trace the journey* of a radiotracer from cyclotron to diagnosis. Memorising this sequence and the role of each block will fetch full marks in Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

> Define the term **radiopharmaceutical**. List **two** commonly used radiopharmaceuticals and state the imaging modality (SPECT or PET) in which each is used.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **radiopharmaceutical** is a compound consisting of a *radioactive isotope* (radionuclide) bound to a *biologically active molecule* (pharmaceutical) that targets a specific organ, tissue, or metabolic pathway after intravenous administration. (2 marks)

| Radiopharmaceutical | Imaging Modality |
| :--- | :--- |
| Technetium-99m MDP (methylene diphosphonate) | SPECT — used in **bone scintigraphy** |
| Fluorine-18 FDG (fluorodeoxyglucose) | PET — used in **oncology, neurology, cardiology** | (1 mark for the table)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> Differentiate between **SPECT** and **PET** imaging modalities in terms of (a) type of radiotracer used, (b) photon energy detected, and (c) method of localisation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SPECT | PET |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (a) Radiotracer type | Single gamma emitters (e.g., Tc-99m) | Positron emitters (e.g., F-18) |
| (b) Photon energy | Typically 140 keV (Tc-99m) | Always 511 keV (annihilation) |
| (c) Localisation method | **Physical lead collimator** | **Electronic collimation via coincidence detection** |

(1 mark per correctly addressed difference)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part B provides an **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions (Q1 vs Q2, Q3 vs Q4, etc.). Both questions below are written as full 14-mark items with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, and complete model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Explain the working principle of a **gamma camera (Anger camera)** with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of the **collimator**, **NaI(Tl) crystal**, and **photomultiplier tubes (PMTs)**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the various **types of collimators** used in nuclear medicine imaging. Explain the trade-off between **resolution and sensitivity** with a relevant formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** The gamma camera, invented by **Hal Anger in 1957**, is a 2-D imaging device that detects gamma photons emitted from a patient injected with a radiotracer. It forms the detector head of a SPECT system.
2. **Collimator (2 marks):** A lead plate (≈ 4–5 cm thick) with thousands of parallel or converging holes. Its function is to act as a *directional filter* — only photons travelling nearly perpendicular to the detector reach the crystal. Off-axis photons are absorbed in the lead septa.
3. **NaI(Tl) Crystal (2 marks):** A large-area (≈ 40 × 50 cm) thallium-doped sodium iodide scintillator (≈ 9.5 mm thick). When a 140 keV gamma photon strikes the crystal, it produces thousands of visible-light photons via the scintillation process. The light is proportional to the energy deposited.
4. **Photomultiplier Tubes (2 marks):** An array of 50–100 PMTs is optically coupled to the back of the crystal. Each PMT converts the light pulse into a proportional electrical pulse and amplifies it by a factor of 10⁶. The relative signal amplitudes among the PMTs are used to compute the X, Y position of the scintillation event, while the sum of all PMT signals gives the Z-pulse (energy) for scatter rejection in the pulse-height analyser (PHA).

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Types of collimators (4 marks):**
   - **Parallel-hole collimator** — the most common; produces a 1:1 image of the source. Used in planar and SPECT bone scans.
   - **Converging-hole collimator (fan-beam / cone-beam)** — magnifies the image; used for small organs (e.g., thyroid, heart).
   - **Diverging-hole collimator** — minifies a large organ (e.g., lung) onto a small detector.
   - **Pinhole collimator** — single small aperture; produces a magnified, inverted image. Used for thyroid and small-animal SPECT.
2. **Resolution vs Sensitivity trade-off (3 marks):** Using the geometric efficiency formula

$$\eta = \frac{K^{2}}{(K + d + t)^{2}}$$

   - To *improve resolution*, the hole diameter $d$ must be decreased and the hole length $K$ increased. But the numerator shrinks faster than the denominator, **reducing $\eta$** (sensitivity). Hence resolution and sensitivity *cannot* both be maximised — improving one degrades the other. This is the **central engineering trade-off** in collimator design.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Internal Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Explain the **principle of PET imaging**. Discuss the **positron–electron annihilation process** and derive the energy of the emitted photons. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **PET and SPECT** in terms of sensitivity, resolution, radiotracer availability, cost, and clinical applications. Mention any **two hybrid imaging systems** used today. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **PET principle (2 marks):** PET (Positron Emission Tomography) uses *positron-emitting* radiotracers (F-18, C-11, N-13, O-15). The emitted positron travels a short distance (≈ 1–3 mm, the *positron range*), then **annihilates** with a nearby electron, producing *two* 511 keV gamma photons emitted in nearly opposite directions (≈ 180° apart).
2. **Annihilation energy derivation (3 marks):** Using Einstein's mass–energy equivalence, the rest mass of an electron and a positron (each 511 keV/c²) is converted entirely into photon energy:

$$E_{\text{total}} = 2 m_e c^{2} = 2 \times 511\ \text{keV} = 1022\ \text{keV}$$

   By conservation of momentum (the two photons travel in opposite directions), each carries exactly half the total energy:

$$E_{\gamma} = \frac{1022\ \text{keV}}{2} = 511\ \text{keV}$$

3. **Coincidence detection (2 marks):** A ring of scintillation detectors (BGO, LSO, or LYSO) surrounds the patient. A valid event is recorded only when *two* detectors on opposite sides fire within a **coincidence window of 6–12 nanoseconds**. The line connecting them is the **line of response (LOR)**. The LOR data are binned into *sinograms* and reconstructed into tomographic images using FBP or iterative (OSEM) methods.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

| Parameter | PET | SPECT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensitivity | **High** (~1–2 % photon detection efficiency) | **Low** (~0.01–0.1 %) due to collimator |
| Spatial resolution | 4–6 mm (limited by positron range) | 6–10 mm (limited by collimator) |
| Radiotracer availability | Cyclotron needed; **short half-lives** (F-18: 110 min) | **Generator-produced** (Tc-99m: 6 h) |
| Cost | **Very high** (cyclotron + scanner) | **Lower** |
| Quantification | Fully quantitative (SUV) | Semi-quantitative |
| Clinical use | Oncology, neurology, cardiology | Bone scan, thyroid, cardiac perfusion |

**Hybrid systems (2 marks):**
- **PET/CT** — combines metabolic (PET) with anatomical (CT) information. Gold standard in oncology.
- **SPECT/CT** — same principle; widely available. Improves lesion localisation in bone and parathyroid imaging.
- **(Bonus)** **PET/MRI** — emerging hybrid with superior soft-tissue contrast and reduced radiation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical problems.** Always state the unit of activity (mCi, MBq) and time (s, min, h). A correct number with the wrong unit gets **zero marks** in KTU valuation.
> 2. **Confusing Tc-99m with Tc-99.** The 'm' denotes a *metastable* nuclear isomer. The ground state Tc-99 is essentially **not used** in imaging — it is a low-energy beta emitter. Many students lose 1 mark by omitting the "m".
> 3. **Writing 511 keV instead of 1022 keV total annihilation energy** (or vice versa). State clearly whether you mean *each* photon's energy or the *total* pair energy.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram** in gamma-camera or PET questions. Even a hand-drawn block diagram (collimator → crystal → PMT → PHA → display) is mandatory and fetches at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning the Pulse Height Analyser (PHA)** when describing the Anger camera. It is the *scatter-rejection* stage and is a favourite sub-question.
> 6. **Writing SUV formula without the decay-correction note.** Examiners may deduct marks if you do not mention that $A_{\text{injected}}$ must be *decay-corrected to scan time*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Nuclear Medical Imaging Systems**

- [ ] **Definition:** NMI visualises *function* using radiopharmaceuticals, not anatomy like X-ray.
