- [ ] **Decay law:** $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$; $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.
- [ ] **Half-life:** $T_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \lambda \approx 0.693/\lambda$.
- [ ] **Mean life:** $\tau = 1/\lambda = 1.44\,T_{1/2}$.
- [ ] **Activity unit conversions:** $1\ \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Bq} = 37\ \text{GBq}$.
- [ ] **Beer–Lambert:** $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$; HVL $= \ln 2 / \mu$.
- [ ] **Gamma camera (Anger) block order:** Collimator → NaI(Tl) crystal → PMT array → Position & Z logic → PHA → Display.
- [ ] **Tc-99m** — 140 keV gamma, $T_{1/2} = 6\ \text{h}$, generator-produced, SPECT workhorse.
- [ ] **PET tracer F-18 FDG** — 110-min half-life, cyclotron-produced, glucose analogue.
- [ ] **Annihilation:** $e^{+} + e^{-} \to 2\gamma$ (511 keV each, 180° apart).
- [ ] **PET uses electronic coincidence collimation** (no physical collimator) — this is the *key difference* from SPECT.
- [ ] **SUV = $c_{\text{tissue}} / (A_{\text{injected}}/m_{\text{patient}})$**, decay-corrected; SUV > 2.5 is suspicious for malignancy.
- [ ] **Collimator trade-off:** Smaller holes + longer length = better resolution but **lower** efficiency (sensitivity).
- [ ] **Hybrid systems:** PET/CT (gold standard for cancer), SPECT/CT, PET/MRI.
- [ ] **Clinical applications:**
  - **Bone scan** = Tc-99m MDP, SPECT.
  - **Thyroid** = Tc-99m pertechnetate or I-123, SPECT.
  - **Myocardial perfusion** = Tc-99m sestamibi / Tl-201, SPECT.
  - **Tumour imaging** = F-18 FDG, PET.
  - **Brain function (epilepsy, dementia)** = F-18 FDG, PET.
- [ ] **Linear no-threshold (LNT) model** is the regulatory basis for radiation safety; *ALARA* (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) is the governing principle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasound Imaging Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ultrasound Imaging Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ultrasound Imaging Systems** are non-invasive diagnostic medical devices that use high-frequency sound waves (typically **1 MHz to 20 MHz**, well above the human audible range of 20 Hz – 20 kHz) to visualize internal body structures such as muscles, tendons, blood vessels, and internal organs. The system operates on the **Pulse-Echo Principle**, where a piezoelectric transducer emits ultrasonic pulses into the body and detects the reflected echoes from tissue interfaces of differing acoustic impedance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Ultrasound imaging is a real-time, radiation-free, non-ionizing diagnostic modality that maps acoustic impedance mismatches in soft tissues using the time-of-flight and amplitude of reflected mechanical waves to construct a 2D or 3D anatomical image.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a long, dark cave and you shout loudly. By listening to the echoes that bounce back from the cave walls, stalactites, and hidden obstacles, your brain can construct a mental picture of the cave's shape and the location of objects inside it — even though you cannot see them.

**Ultrasound imaging works on exactly this principle:**

- The "shout" is the **high-frequency sound pulse** sent into the body.
- The "echoes" are the **reflected sound waves** from tissue boundaries (e.g., muscle–bone interface, blood–vessel wall).
- The "dark cave" is the **body interior**, which is acoustically opaque to light but transparent to sound.
- The "brain constructing a picture" is the **ultrasound scanner's computer**, which uses the time delay and strength of each echo to draw a grayscale anatomical image.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted for KTU Board Exams):**
> - Diagnostic ultrasound frequency range: **1 MHz – 20 MHz**
> - Speed of sound in soft tissue: **c ≈ 1540 m/s** (a constant examiners love to test)
> - Acoustic impedance of soft tissue: **Z ≈ 1.6 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)** (Rayl)
> - Frame rate for real-time imaging: **15 – 60 frames per second (fps)**
> - Wavelength in tissue at 5 MHz: **λ ≈ 0.308 mm** (governs axial resolution)

## 1.3 Why Ultrasound Matters in Biomedical Engineering

| Feature | Clinical Advantage |
|---|---|
| **Non-ionizing radiation** | Safe for pregnant women and fetuses |
| **Real-time imaging** | Guides surgical procedures and biopsies |
| **Portable & low-cost** | Ideal for point-of-care and rural diagnostics |
| **Doppler capability** | Measures blood flow velocity non-invasively |
| **No contrast agents (usually)** | Reduces allergic risk to patients |

---

## 1.4 Basic Acoustics Refresher — The Building Blocks of Ultrasound

Before we dive into imaging systems, we must master the physics of sound, because every ultrasound formula is built on these foundations.

### 1.4.1 Sound as a Mechanical Longitudinal Wave

Sound is a **mechanical longitudinal pressure wave** that requires a physical medium (solid, liquid, or gas) to propagate. In soft biological tissue, particles oscillate parallel to the direction of wave travel, creating alternating regions of **compression** (high pressure) and **rarefaction** (low pressure).

### 1.4.2 The Three Defining Parameters of a Sound Wave

A sound wave is fully described by three intrinsic parameters:

1. **Frequency (f)** — Number of pressure oscillations per second, measured in Hertz (Hz). In ultrasound, f is in MHz.
2. **Wavelength (λ)** — Physical distance over which one complete cycle occurs, measured in meters (m).
3. **Velocity (c)** — Speed at which the wavefront travels through the medium, measured in m/s. In soft tissue, **c = 1540 m/s** is the gold-standard assumption.

These three are bound by the universal wave equation:

$$c = f \cdot \lambda$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is `c = 1540 m/s` a constant in soft tissue?** Because the velocity of sound depends only on the medium's density (ρ) and bulk modulus (K), and soft tissues (fat, muscle, liver, blood) have remarkably similar ρ and K values. Bone and air are exceptions — sound moves faster in bone (~4080 m/s) and slower in air (~330 m/s).

### 1.4.3 Acoustic Impedance (Z) — The Heart of Ultrasound Contrast

This is the single most important concept for understanding why ultrasound images look the way they do.

**Acoustic Impedance (Z)** is the resistance offered by a medium to the passage of a sound wave. It is the acoustic analogue of electrical impedance.

$$Z = \rho \cdot c$$

Where:
- **ρ** = density of the medium (kg/m³)
- **c** = velocity of sound in that medium (m/s)
- **Z** = acoustic impedance, unit: **Rayl** (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹)

When an ultrasound wave crosses a boundary between two tissues with different Z values, part of the wave is **reflected** and part is **transmitted**. The fraction reflected is determined by the **Reflection Coefficient (R)**, derived from acoustic impedance mismatch:

$$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

Where **Z₁** and **Z₂** are the acoustic impedances of the two media at the interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The larger the difference in Z between two tissues, the stronger the echo, and the brighter that boundary appears in the image. This is why **bone–soft tissue** and **air–soft tissue** interfaces produce extremely bright (hyperechoic) reflections, while interfaces between similar tissues (e.g., liver–kidney) produce weak reflections.

### 1.4.4 Sample Acoustic Impedance Values (Memorize These)

| Medium | Density ρ (kg/m³) | Velocity c (m/s) | Acoustic Impedance Z (×10⁶ Rayl) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Air** | 1.2 | 330 | 0.0004 |
| **Fat** | 952 | 1450 | 1.38 |
| **Water** | 1000 | 1480 | 1.48 |
| **Soft tissue (avg)** | 1060 | 1540 | **1.63** |
| **Kidney** | 1040 | 1560 | 1.62 |
| **Blood** | 1060 | 1570 | 1.66 |
| **Muscle** | 1080 | 1580 | 1.70 |
| **Bone (cortical)** | 1910 | 4080 | 7.80 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Air has Z ≈ 0.0004 × 10⁶ Rayl, while soft tissue has Z ≈ 1.63 × 10⁶ Rayl. The mismatch is enormous (over 4000×), which is why ultrasound **cannot image through air or lungs** and why a coupling gel (water-based, Z ≈ 1.5 × 10⁶ Rayl) is mandatory between the transducer and the skin to eliminate the air gap.

### 1.4.5 Attenuation — Why Deep Tissues Need Higher Energy

As ultrasound travels through tissue, its intensity decreases due to three mechanisms: **absorption** (conversion to heat, the dominant cause), **scattering** (redirection by small reflectors like red blood cells), and **reflection** (at major interfaces). The combined effect is called **attenuation**, and it follows an exponential decay law:

$$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}$$

Where:
- **I₀** = initial intensity at the transducer face
- **I(x)** = intensity at depth x (meters)
- **α** = attenuation coefficient (Np/m or dB/cm)
- **x** = depth into tissue (m)

A useful practical approximation is the **half-value layer (HVL)** — the depth at which intensity drops to half. In soft tissue, the HVL is approximately **0.5 cm per MHz**, which means a 5 MHz probe can usefully image to a depth of roughly:

$$d_{max} \approx \frac{\text{HVL} \times f_{MHz}}{1} = \frac{0.5 \times 5}{1} \approx 12 \text{ to } 15 \text{ cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off rule (frequently tested):** Higher frequency = better axial resolution (smaller λ), but shallower penetration. Lower frequency = deeper penetration, but poorer resolution. This is why **2–5 MHz probes** are used for abdominal/cardiac imaging, while **7–15 MHz probes** are used for vascular and superficial structures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pulse-Echo Principle — The Operating Heart of Every Ultrasound Scanner

Every clinical ultrasound machine, from a ₹50,000 portable unit to a ₹2 crore premium cart-based system, operates on the **pulse-echo principle**. The logic unfolds in a strict sequence:

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Pulse Generation (Transmit Phase):** The system applies a brief high-voltage electrical pulse (typically 50–300 V, 0.5–3 µs duration) across a **piezoelectric crystal** housed in the transducer. The crystal vibrates at its resonant frequency, launching a short ultrasonic pulse into the body.
2. **Wave Propagation:** The pulse travels through tissue at ~1540 m/s. As it crosses each tissue interface, a small portion is reflected back toward the transducer, while the rest continues deeper.
3. **Echo Reception (Receive Phase):** Returning echoes strike the same piezoelectric crystal, mechanically deforming it. By the **direct piezoelectric effect**, the mechanical stress generates a small voltage (µV range) across the crystal's electrodes.
4. **Signal Processing:** The system amplifies these weak echo voltages (using a Time-Gain Compensator to compensate for depth-dependent attenuation), filters them, and digitizes them.
5. **Image Reconstruction:** A scan converter maps each echo's **time-of-flight** to a **depth coordinate** and the echo's **amplitude** to a **brightness (grayscale) value**.
6. **Display:** A monitor displays the brightness-modulated spots in their correct spatial positions, refreshing 15–60 times per second to produce a real-time "live" image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a single crystal is used for both transmit and receive:** The pulse duration (a few microseconds) is far shorter than the listening time (hundreds of microseconds), allowing the same transducer to be reused as a receiver during the "off" interval. This is called a **duplexer** or **transmit/receive switch** in the scanner architecture.

### 2.2 The Piezocrystal — Where Electricity Becomes Sound

**Pierre and Jacques Curie** discovered the piezoelectric effect in 1880. The transducer's heart is a thin disc (often 10–30 mm diameter, 0.2–1 mm thick) of polarized ceramic, most commonly **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)**.

- **Direct effect:** Mechanical stress → electrical charge (used for echo reception).
- **Converse effect:** Applied voltage → mechanical strain (used for pulse transmission).

The crystal's **resonant frequency** is set by its thickness, following the half-wavelength resonance condition:

$$f_r = \frac{c_{crystal}}{2 \cdot t}$$

Where:
- **c_crystal** = speed of sound in the PZT material (~4000 m/s)
- **t** = thickness of the crystal (m)
- **f_r** = resonant frequency (Hz)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** To double the frequency, the crystal must be halved in thickness. This is why very high-frequency probes (15–20 MHz) have extremely thin, fragile crystals, and why low-frequency probes can be larger and more robust.

### 2.3 Image Formation Modes — A, B, M, and Doppler

Ultrasound imaging has evolved through four canonical display modes, each serving a distinct clinical purpose. KTU examiners **love** asking for the difference between A-mode and B-mode.

#### 2.3.1 A-Mode (Amplitude Mode) — The Oldest Form

- The transducer is held stationary at one point on the body.
- Echoes are displayed as **vertical spikes** on an oscilloscope, where the **x-axis represents depth** (from time-of-flight) and the **y-axis represents echo amplitude**.
- **Clinical use:** Ophthalmology (measuring eye axial length), echoencephalography (historically), and basic tissue boundary detection.
- **Information conveyed:** Distance between reflecting surfaces and relative strength of reflections.

#### 2.3.2 B-Mode (Brightness Mode) — The Workhorse of Modern Ultrasound

- The transducer is **scanned mechanically or electronically** across the body.
- Each echo is displayed as a **dot** whose **brightness is proportional to echo amplitude**.
- A line of dots forms a 2D cross-sectional image.
- **Clinical use:** Abdominal imaging, obstetrics, cardiology, vascular, and virtually every modern ultrasound exam.
- **Information conveyed:** 2D anatomical map of tissue interfaces.

#### 2.3.3 M-Mode (Motion Mode) — The Cardiologist's Favorite

- A single scan line is held stationary, and the system displays **successive A-mode traces stacked side-by-side over time**.
- The x-axis is **time**; the y-axis is **depth**; brightness is echo amplitude.
- **Clinical use:** Cardiac valve motion, fetal heart rate, and any rapidly moving structure.
- **Information conveyed:** Time-motion plot of moving boundaries.

#### 2.3.4 Doppler Mode — The Blood Flow Detector

- Exploits the **Doppler Effect**: the frequency of echoes from moving red blood cells is shifted in proportion to their velocity.
- **Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler:** Two crystals — one always transmitting, one always receiving. Can measure very high velocities but has no range resolution.
- **Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler:** Uses pulsed ultrasound (like B-mode) to sample a specific depth (the "sample volume"). Range-resolved but limited to lower velocities.
- **Color Doppler:** PW Doppler repeated across many scan lines, with velocity encoded as color (red = toward, blue = away). Overlaid on B-mode image.
- **Spectral Doppler:** Displays velocity vs. time as a waveform.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE. Master this and you are guaranteed to score the 3-mark definition/numerical questions.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $c = f \cdot \lambda$ | c = velocity, f = frequency, λ = wavelength | m/s, Hz, m | Universal wave relation |
| 2 | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | Z = acoustic impedance, ρ = density, c = velocity | Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) | Tissue characterization, reflection |
| 3 | $R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$ | R = reflection coefficient (intensity) | dimensionless | Echo strength at interface |
| 4 | $T = 1 - R$ | T = transmission coefficient | dimensionless | Energy transmitted across interface |
| 5 | $I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}$ | I = intensity at depth x, I₀ = initial intensity, α = attenuation coeff. | W/m², m, Np/m | Depth-dependent energy loss |
| 6 | $d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | d = depth, c = sound speed, t = round-trip time | m, m/s, s | **Pulse-echo depth calculation** (factor of 2 for round trip) |
| 7 | $\text{PRF}_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot d_{max}}$ | PRF = pulse repetition frequency, d_max = max imaging depth | Hz, m/s, m | Maximum unambiguous depth |
| 8 | $f_r = \frac{c_{crystal}}{2 \cdot t}$ | f_r = transducer resonant frequency, t = crystal thickness | Hz, m/s, m | Transducer design |
| 9 | $\Delta f = \frac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}$ | Δf = Doppler shift, f₀ = transmit freq., v = blood velocity, θ = angle between beam and flow | Hz, Hz, m/s, m/s, degrees | **Doppler blood flow measurement** |
| 10 | $\text{Axial Resolution} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f_{bw}}$ | Δf_bw = transducer bandwidth | m, m/s, Hz | Minimum distinguishable depth difference |
| 11 | $\text{Lateral Resolution} = \lambda \cdot \frac{z}{D}$ | z = depth, D = transducer aperture | m, m, m | Beam width determines lateral resolution |
| 12 | $\text{Frame Rate} = \frac{\text{PRF}}{N_{lines}}$ | N_lines = number of scan lines per frame | fps, Hz | Real-time imaging speed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** The reflection coefficient is always **non-negative** because it is a square. Do not write $R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$ — that is the *amplitude* reflection coefficient, not the *intensity* coefficient. KTU examiners will deduct marks for confusing the two.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Ultrasound engineering is a vast and growing field. Beyond clinical diagnosis, the same principles are used in:

- **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** Detecting cracks in aircraft wings, pipelines, and railway rails.
- **SONAR (Sound Navigation and Ranging):** Submarine detection, ocean floor mapping.
- **Industrial Cleaning:** Ultrasonic baths for cleaning jewelry, surgical instruments, and electronic components.
- **Therapeutic Ultrasound:** High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound (HIFU) for non-invasive tumor ablation, and physiotherapy deep heating.
- **Acoustic Microscopy:** Imaging at GHz frequencies to see cell-level structures.
- **Photoacoustic Imaging:** A hybrid technique where pulsed laser light creates ultrasonic waves inside tissue, combining optics and acoustics.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section delivers the **exhaustive derivations** that KTU examiners expect for 14-mark Part B questions. Every algebraic transition is written out, with plain-English commentary.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Depth Calculation Using Pulse-Echo Time

### Problem Statement
A 5 MHz ultrasound pulse is sent into soft tissue. If the round-trip time for an echo from a tissue interface is 26 µs, calculate the depth of the interface. Take c = 1540 m/s.

### Step 1: Understand the Geometry
The pulse leaves the transducer, travels a depth `d` into the tissue, hits a reflector, and returns the same distance `d` back to the transducer. Therefore, the **total path length** travelled by the sound is `2d`, not `d`. This is the single most important "factor of 2" in all of ultrasound physics.

### Step 2: Write the Basic Kinematic Relation
Distance equals speed multiplied by time, applied to the total path:

$$\text{Total path} = c \cdot t$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Round-Trip Path Length
Because the wave travels down and back, the total path is `2d`:

$$2d = c \cdot t$$

### Step 4: Solve Algebraically for Depth `d`

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

### Step 5: Insert Numerical Values
Convert all quantities to SI base units:
- c = 1540 m/s (already in SI)
- t = 26 µs = 26 × 10⁻⁶ s

$$d = \frac{1540 \cdot 26 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

### Step 6: Compute the Numerator First
We separate the arithmetic to avoid errors:

$$1540 \times 26 = 40040$$

$$40040 \times 10^{-6} = 0.04004 \text{ m·s/s}$$

### Step 7: Divide by 2

$$d = \frac{0.04004}{2} = 0.02002 \text{ m}$$

### Step 8: Convert to Centimeters for Clinical Intuition
Ultrasound depths are conventionally reported in centimeters:

$$d = 0.02002 \text{ m} = 2.002 \text{ cm} \approx 2 \text{ cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The tissue interface is located at a depth of **2 cm** from the transducer surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students who forget the factor of 2 (i.e., write `d = c × t`) will get **4 cm**, which is exactly double the correct answer. This is the #1 cause of lost marks in ultrasound numericals. Always draw a quick sketch: "down + up = round trip."

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reflection Coefficient at a Tissue Interface

### Problem Statement
An ultrasound wave traveling in fat strikes a muscle layer. Calculate the fraction of intensity reflected at the interface.

Given:
- Z_fat = 1.38 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)
- Z_muscle = 1.70 × 10⁶ kg/(m²·s)

### Step 1: Recall the Intensity Reflection Coefficient
The fraction of incident intensity that is reflected at a planar interface is given by:

$$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

This formula assumes normal (perpendicular) incidence. KTU 2024 syllabus is restricted to normal incidence.

### Step 2: Identify the Two Media
- Medium 1 (incident side): **Fat**, Z₁ = 1.38 × 10⁶ Rayl
- Medium 2 (transmission side): **Muscle**, Z₂ = 1.70 × 10⁶ Rayl

### Step 3: Compute the Difference in Impedance

$$Z_2 - Z_1 = (1.70 - 1.38) \times 10^6 = 0.32 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayl}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Sum of Impedances

$$Z_2 + Z_1 = (1.70 + 1.38) \times 10^6 = 3.08 \times 10^6 \text{ Rayl}$$

### Step 5: Form the Fraction

$$\frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} = \frac{0.32 \times 10^6}{3.08 \times 10^6} = \frac{0.32}{3.08}$$

### Step 6: Simplify the Decimal

$$\frac{0.32}{3.08} = 0.103896...$$

### Step 7: Square to Get the Intensity Coefficient

$$R = (0.1039)^2 = 0.01079$$

### Step 8: Express as a Percentage

$$R = 0.01079 \times 100\% \approx 1.08\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Only about **1.08%** of the incident intensity is reflected at the fat–muscle interface; the remaining **~98.92%** is transmitted into the muscle. This is why soft tissue imaging requires sensitive receivers and high dynamic range.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Some students forget to square and report the amplitude coefficient (~0.104 or 10.4%) instead of the intensity coefficient (~1.08%). These differ by a factor of 10 in percentage terms, and KTU examiners will deduct marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Doppler Shift for Blood Flow Measurement

### Problem Statement
A 5 MHz ultrasound beam is directed at a blood vessel. The Doppler-shifted echo returns at 5.002 MHz. The angle between the beam and the flow direction is 60°. Calculate the blood flow velocity. Take c = 1540 m/s.

### Step 1: Write the General Doppler Equation for Ultrasound
For a moving reflector, the frequency shift of the received wave is:

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 \cdot f_0 \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c}$$

The factor of 2 appears because the wave undergoes **two** Doppler shifts: once when the moving blood cells receive the wave (transmitter→moving target), and once when they re-emit (moving target→receiver). For a pulse-echo system, this doubling is automatic.

### Step 2: Compute the Frequency Shift

$$\Delta f = f_{received} - f_0 = 5.002 \times 10^6 - 5.000 \times 10^6 = 0.002 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 2000 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 3: Substitute All Known Values
- Δf = 2000 Hz
- f₀ = 5 × 10⁶ Hz
- θ = 60° → cos 60° = 0.5
- c = 1540 m/s

$$2000 = \frac{2 \cdot (5 \times 10^6) \cdot v \cdot 0.5}{1540}$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Numerator Coefficient
The `2 × 0.5` simplifies to `1`, which is a clean algebraic step:

$$2000 = \frac{(5 \times 10^6) \cdot v}{1540}$$

### Step 5: Solve Algebraically for v

$$v = \frac{2000 \cdot 1540}{5 \times 10^6}$$

### Step 6: Compute the Numerator

$$2000 \times 1540 = 3{,}080{,}000 = 3.08 \times 10^6$$

### Step 7: Divide by the Denominator

$$v = \frac{3.08 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^6}$$

### Step 8: Simplify the Powers of Ten

$$v = \frac{3.08}{5} = 0.616 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 9: Convert to cm/s (Clinical Convention)

$$v = 0.616 \text{ m/s} = 61.6 \text{ cm/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The blood is flowing at approximately **0.616 m/s (61.6 cm/s)**. This is within the normal range for arterial blood flow in large vessels (typically 40–100 cm/s).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Common Mistake:** Forgetting the factor of 2 (i.e., the double Doppler shift) will give v = 1.232 m/s, which is **double** the true value. Also, students often write `cos θ` in the denominator by mistake — it is always in the **numerator** for transmitted frequency formulas.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Imaging Depth and Pulse Repetition Frequency

### Problem Statement
A scanner uses a PRF of 5 kHz. Calculate the maximum unambiguous imaging depth in soft tissue. Take c = 1540 m/s.

### Step 1: Understand the Unambiguous Range Constraint
The scanner fires a pulse and then "listens" for echoes. The next pulse cannot be fired until the echoes from the deepest target of interest have returned, otherwise new and old echoes will overlap (called **range ambiguity**).

### Step 2: Write the Constraint
The round-trip time to the maximum depth `d_max` and back is `2d_max / c`. This must equal the pulse repetition period `T_PRF = 1 / PRF`:

$$\frac{2 \cdot d_{max}}{c} = \frac{1}{\text{PRF}}$$

### Step 3: Solve for d_max

$$d_{max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot \text{PRF}}$$

### Step 4: Insert Values
- c = 1540 m/s
- PRF = 5000 Hz

$$d_{max} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 5000}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Denominator

$$2 \times 5000 = 10000$$

### Step 6: Final Division

$$d_{max} = \frac{1540}{10000} = 0.154 \text{ m} = 15.4 \text{ cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The maximum unambiguous imaging depth is **15.4 cm** — sufficient for most abdominal and obstetric scans.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Transducer and Mode

| Clinical Application | Mode | Frequency (MHz) | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First-trimester obstetrics** | B-mode | 5–7 | Moderate depth, good detail of fetus |
| **Third-trimester obstetrics** | B-mode | 3–5 | Deeper penetration needed |
| **Cardiac (adult TTE)** | B + M + Doppler | 2–4 | Must penetrate ribs and lungs |
| **Vascular (carotid, leg)** | B + Color Doppler | 7–10 | Superficial arteries need high resolution |
| **Thyroid, breast lumps** | B-mode | 10–15 | Very superficial, high resolution critical |
| **Ophthalmology** | A-mode + B-mode | 10–20 | Eye is small, requires fine resolution |
| **Musculoskeletal** | B-mode | 10–18 | Tendons and ligaments are superficial |
| **Endocavitary (transvaginal)** | B-mode | 5–9 | Short path, high frequency acceptable |
| **Emergency FAST scan** | B-mode | 2–5 | Quick abdominal survey, deep penetration |
| **Fetal heart rate** | M-mode | 3–5 | Captures rapid motion of valve leaflets |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical: Attenuation in Tissue

### Problem Statement
Ultrasound at 5 MHz enters soft tissue with intensity 10 mW/cm². After traveling 4 cm, the intensity is measured as 3.2 mW/cm². Calculate the attenuation coefficient α in dB/cm.

### Step 1: Write the Attenuation Equation in Linear Form

$$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}$$

Where α here is in Np/m (Neper per meter), the natural-log form.

### Step 2: Solve for α

$$e^{-\alpha x} = \frac{I(x)}{I_0}$$

$$-\alpha x = \ln\left(\frac{I(x)}{I_0}\right)$$

$$\alpha = -\frac{1}{x} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I(x)}{I_0}\right)$$

### Step 3: Compute the Intensity Ratio

$$\frac{I(x)}{I_0} = \frac{3.2}{10} = 0.32$$

### Step 4: Take the Natural Logarithm

$$\ln(0.32) = -1.1394$$

### Step 5: Substitute Values
- x = 4 cm = 0.04 m

$$\alpha = -\frac{1}{0.04} \cdot (-1.1394) = \frac{1.1394}{0.04} = 28.486 \text{ Np/m}$$

### Step 6: Convert Np/m to dB/cm
The conversion factor is: 1 Np = 8.686 dB, and 1 m = 100 cm, so:

$$\alpha = 28.486 \text{ Np/m} \times \frac{8.686 \text{ dB}}{1 \text{ Np}} \times \frac{1 \text{ m}}{100 \text{ cm}} = 2.47 \text{ dB/cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The attenuation coefficient is approximately **2.47 dB/cm** at 5 MHz. This is consistent with the rule of thumb that soft tissue attenuates at **~0.5 dB/cm per MHz**, giving 0.5 × 5 = 2.5 dB/cm ✓.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The "0.5 dB/cm/MHz" rule of thumb is one of the most frequently asked numerical sub-questions. The above detailed derivation is the standard expected solution structure.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete Ultrasound Imaging System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clockA[Master Clock and Timing Generator] --> pulserA[Pulser and Transmit Beamformer]
    pulserA --> tcvrA[Transmit Receive Switch Duplexer]
    tcvrA --> trandA[Ultrasound Transducer Piezoelectric Crystal]
    trandA -- Pulse into tissue --> bodyA[Patient Body Tissue Interfaces]
    bodyA -- Reflected echoes --> trandA
    trandA --> tcvrA
    tcvrA --> receiveA[Receive Beamformer and Preamplifier]
    receiveA --> tgcA[Time Gain Compensation Amplifier]
    tgcA --> filterA[Bandpass Filter and Envelope Detector]
    filterA --> digA[Analog to Digital Converter]
    digA --> scanA[Scan Converter Memory]
    scanA --> dispA[Display Monitor with Grayscale Mapping]
    clockA --> scanA
    clockA --> dispA
    userA[Sonographer Input Keyboard Trackball] --> clockA
    userA --> scanA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The system operates in a synchronous loop. The **master clock** orchestrates every event. During transmit, the pulser excites the transducer through the duplexer. The same transducer then becomes a receiver, the duplexer routes echoes to the preamp, the TGC corrects for depth-dependent attenuation, the scan converter maps echo data to a 2D pixel grid, and the monitor displays the image. This is the architecture used in every modern cart-based and portable ultrasound system.

---

## 4.2 Pulse-Echo Round-Trip Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    trandB[Transducer Surface] -- Pulse travels down distance d --> interfaceB[Reflecting Tissue Interface at depth d]
    interfaceB -- Echo travels up distance d --> trandB
```

This minimal diagram illustrates the round-trip path that justifies the factor of 2 in the depth formula `d = c·t / 2`. The total distance covered by the wave is `2d`, while the time `t` measured by the system is for the entire round trip.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram of a Doppler Ultrasound Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    oscB[Oscillator at f0 MHz] --> mixerB[Mixer and Phase Detector]
    mixerB --> txdopB[Transmit Crystal for Doppler]
    txdopB -- CW into vessel --> bloodB[Moving Red Blood Cells in Vessel]
    bloodB -- Shifted echo f0 plus delta f --> rxdopB[Receive Crystal for Doppler]
    rxdopB --> mixerB
    mixerB --> lpfB[Low Pass Filter to extract delta f]
    lpfB --> audbopB[Audio Amplifier and Speaker]
    lpfB --> spectB[Spectral Display Velocity vs Time]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler uses **two separate crystals** — one always transmitting, one always receiving — to detect blood flow without any depth gating. The mixer subtracts the transmitted frequency from the received frequency, leaving the audible Doppler shift (typically 100 Hz to 10 kHz), which can be heard as "swishing" sounds and displayed as a spectrogram.

---

## 4.4 A-Mode, B-Mode, and M-Mode Display Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    amodea["A-MODE: vertical spikes on horizontal time line. X axis equals depth, Y axis equals amplitude. Single stationary line."]
    bmodea["B-MODE: 2D grayscale cross section. X axis equals lateral position, Y axis equals depth, brightness equals echo amplitude. Scanned image."]
    mmodea["M-MODE: vertical stripes representing successive A-Mode traces stacked over time. X axis equals time, Y axis equals depth. Motion pattern."]
    amodea --> bmodea --> mmodea
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Progression:** A-mode (1D spikes) → B-mode (2D grayscale) → M-mode (motion of a single A-line over time). M-mode is mathematically equivalent to photographing the A-mode oscilloscope screen repeatedly and stacking the photos side-by-side.

---

## 4.5 Piezocrystal Transducer Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    housingA[Plastic Housing and Handle] --> matchingA[Matching Layer Z equals geometric mean of PZT and tissue]
    matchingA --> crystalA[Lead Zirconate Titanate PZT Crystal Active Element]
    crystalA --> backingA[Damping Backing Material Absorbs Backward Wave and Shortens Pulse]
    backingA --> airA[Air Rear Medium]
    crystalA -- Two Electrodes --> electrodel[Front Electrode Connected to Matching Layer]
    crystalA --> electroder[Rear Electrode Connected to Backing]
    elecsysA[Coaxial Cable from Scanner] --> electrodel
    elecsysA --> electroder
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Function of Each Layer (High-Yield for 14-Mark Questions):**
> - **Matching layer:** Made of material with Z = √(Z_PZT × Z_tissue), typically a loaded epoxy. Minimizes the large PZT–tissue impedance mismatch and improves energy transfer into the body.
> - **PZT crystal:** The active piezoelectric element. Its thickness sets the resonant frequency.
> - **Backing material:** A dense, lossy composite (often tungsten–epoxy) that absorbs backward-travelling acoustic energy. It **shortens the pulse length**, which improves **axial resolution** at the cost of sensitivity.
> - **Electrodes:** Thin metallic coatings (often silver) that deliver the excitation voltage and pick up the received signal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define acoustic impedance. Explain its role in ultrasound imaging.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Acoustic impedance (Z) of a medium is the product of its density (ρ) and the velocity of sound (c) in that medium. It represents the resistance offered by the medium to the propagation of sound waves.

$$Z = \rho \cdot c$$

**Unit:** Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹).

**Role in ultrasound imaging (2 marks):** When an ultrasound wave encounters a boundary between two tissues with acoustic impedances Z₁ and Z₂, a portion of the wave is reflected. The fraction of incident intensity reflected is given by:

$$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$

The **larger the difference in Z**, the **stronger the echo** and the **brighter the boundary** in the image. This is the fundamental reason why ultrasound images show excellent contrast at soft-tissue interfaces, while air-tissue and bone-tissue interfaces produce very strong reflections.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between A-mode and B-mode ultrasound imaging.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | A-Mode (Amplitude Mode) | B-Mode (Brightness Mode) |
|---|---|---|
| **Display** | Vertical spikes (deflections) on an oscilloscope | 2D grayscale cross-sectional image |
| **Axes** | X-axis = depth, Y-axis = echo amplitude | X-axis = lateral position, Y-axis = depth, brightness = echo amplitude |
| **Transducer motion** | Stationary at one point | Mechanically or electronically scanned across the body |
| **Information** | One-dimensional; depth and strength of reflectors | Two-dimensional; anatomical map of tissue interfaces |
| **Clinical use** | Ophthalmology, echoencephalography (obsolete) | Obstetrics, abdominal, cardiac, vascular — all modern scans |
| **Image form** | Spike trace | Real-time 2D tomographic image |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing that A-mode gives a 2D image or that B-mode uses stationary transducer. Both statements are wrong and will cost marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the basic principle of pulse-echo ultrasound imaging. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (3 marks):** The pulse-echo principle is the fundamental operating concept of all ultrasound scanners. The system transmits a short burst of high-frequency sound (1–20 MHz) into the body using a piezoelectric transducer. The pulse propagates through tissues, and wherever it encounters an interface between two media of differing acoustic impedance, a portion of the wave is reflected back as an echo. The transducer receives these echoes and the system measures:

1. The **time of flight** (t) between transmission and reception — used to compute the depth of the reflecting interface.
2. The **amplitude** of the echo — used to assign a brightness value in the image.

**Depth formula (2 marks):**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

where c = 1540 m/s in soft tissue, and the factor of 2 accounts for the round-trip path.

**Block diagram and explanation (2 marks):** The major blocks of the system are: pulser → transmit/receive switch → piezoelectric transducer → TGC amplifier → signal processor → scan converter → display monitor. The pulser excites the transducer with a short high-voltage pulse. The same transducer receives returning echoes. The TGC amplifier compensates for attenuation with depth. The scan converter maps each echo to a pixel whose brightness is proportional to echo amplitude, and the monitor displays the resulting 2D image at 15–60 frames per second.

---

**(b) An ultrasound system operates at 3.5 MHz. Calculate the wavelength in soft tissue and the depth of a reflector if the echo returns in 39 µs. Take c = 1540 m/s. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Calculate wavelength using c = fλ (3 marks):**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{3.5 \times 10^6}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1540}{3.5} \times 10^{-6} = 440 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 0.44 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the depth using the pulse-echo formula (4 marks):**

$$d = \frac{c \cdot t}{2}$$

$$d = \frac{1540 \times 39 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$

**Step 3: Compute the numerator:**

$$1540 \times 39 = 60060$$

$$60060 \times 10^{-6} = 0.06006 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Divide by 2:**

$$d = 0.03003 \text{ m} = 3.003 \text{ cm} \approx 3 \text{ cm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** Wavelength λ = 0.44 mm; Depth d = 3.0 cm.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]

**(a) Explain the Doppler principle as applied to ultrasound blood flow measurement. Derive the Doppler shift equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (3 marks):** The Doppler Effect, discovered by Christian Doppler in 1842, states that when a wave source and receiver move relative to each other, the observed frequency differs from the emitted frequency. In diagnostic ultrasound, the "source" is the stationary transducer, the "moving target" is the cloud of red blood cells, and the "receiver" is the same transducer. The frequency shift (Δf) is proportional to the velocity of blood flow.

The Doppler shift is detected by mixing (heterodyning) the transmitted frequency f₀ with the received frequency f_r. The difference, Δf = f_r − f₀, is in the audible range (typically 100 Hz – 10 kHz) and can be heard through a speaker and displayed as a spectrogram.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Consider a transducer emitting ultrasound at frequency f₀ toward a red blood cell moving with velocity v at angle θ to the beam.

The blood cell "sees" an incident frequency:

$$f_1 = \frac{f_0}{(1 - v \cos\theta / c)}$$

(For v << c, this can be linearized, but we will use the standard textbook Doppler formula directly.)

The blood cell re-emits (scatters) at the same frequency f₁ as it receives (assuming the cell is a passive scatterer). The transducer now receives a frequency from a moving source:

$$f_r = f_1 \cdot \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right) = f_0 \cdot \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)$$

Since v cos θ << c, applying the approximation $\frac{1}{1-x} \approx 1 + x$ for small x:

$$f_r \approx f_0 \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{v \cos\theta}{c} \right) = f_0 + \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

Wait — this approximation gives a single factor, but the correct two-way (double) Doppler shift emerges when we use the non-relativistic two-step reasoning. The cleaner pedagogical form is:

The blood cell moving with velocity component v cos θ toward the transducer experiences both an increase in received frequency (when acting as receiver) and a further increase in transmitted frequency (when acting as moving source). The two effects add, giving a total shift of:

$$\Delta f = f_r - f_0 = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

This is the **Doppler shift equation** for pulse-echo ultrasound. The factor of 2 reflects the double Doppler shift. For typical blood flow (v < 1 m/s) and c = 1540 m/s, the approximation v << c is valid to better than 0.1% accuracy.

---

**(b) A 4 MHz Doppler ultrasound probe is used to measure blood flow. The angle between the beam and the flow direction is 45°. If the observed Doppler shift is 2 kHz, calculate the blood flow velocity. Take c = 1540 m/s. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Write the Doppler equation:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 v \cos\theta}{c}$$

**Step 2: List the known values:**
- Δf = 2 kHz = 2000 Hz
- f₀ = 4 MHz = 4 × 10⁶ Hz
- θ = 45° → cos 45° = 1/√2 ≈ 0.7071
- c = 1540 m/s

**Step 3: Substitute into the equation:**

$$2000 = \frac{2 \cdot (4 \times 10^6) \cdot v \cdot 0.7071}{1540}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the coefficient in the numerator:**

$$2 \times 4 \times 10^6 \times 0.7071 = 5.6568 \times 10^6$$

So:

$$2000 = \frac{5.6568 \times 10^6 \cdot v}{1540}$$

**Step 5: Solve for v:**

$$v = \frac{2000 \times 1540}{5.6568 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 6: Compute the numerator:**

$$2000 \times 1540 = 3{,}080{,}000 = 3.08 \times 10^6$$

**Step 7: Final division:**

$$v = \frac{3.08 \times 10^6}{5.6568 \times 10^6} = 0.5444 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 8: Convert to cm/s for clinical reporting:**

$$v = 0.5444 \text{ m/s} \approx 54.4 \text{ cm/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The blood flow velocity is approximately **0.544 m/s (54.4 cm/s)**, which lies within the normal range for arterial flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the Doppler equation is the most common error. This would give v ≈ 0.272 m/s, exactly half the correct value. Always check whether your system is continuous wave (single Doppler shift) or pulse-echo (double Doppler shift). Diagnostic scanners use pulse-echo and always carry the factor of 2.
> 2. **Using sin θ instead of cos θ** is a frequent mistake. The Doppler equation uses the cosine of the angle because it is the **component of velocity along the beam axis** that produces the shift, not the perpendicular component.
> 3. **Not converting µs, MHz, kHz to SI units** (seconds, Hz) will produce answers that are off by 3, 6, or 9 orders of magnitude. Always convert first, then calculate.
> 4. **Mixing up the depth formula** — students sometimes write `d = c·t` instead of `d = c·t/2`, doubling the depth.
> 5. **Confusing the intensity and amplitude reflection coefficients** — squaring the impedance difference ratio is required for intensity, not for amplitude.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Ultimate KTU Ultrasound Cheat Sheet — Memorize This Before the Exam:**

- [x] **Diagnostic ultrasound frequency range:** 1 MHz – 20 MHz (above human hearing).
- [x] **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** c = 1540 m/s (universal constant for ultrasound).
- [x] **Acoustic impedance formula:** Z = ρ · c, measured in Rayl.
- [x] **Reflection coefficient (intensity):** R = [(Z₂ − Z₁) / (Z₂ + Z₁)]² — always square, always non-negative.
- [x] **Transmission coefficient:** T = 1 − R.
- [x] **Attenuation law:** I(x) = I₀ · e^(−αx); rule of thumb α ≈ 0.5 dB/cm/MHz in soft tissue.
- [x] **Depth formula (pulse-echo):** d = c·t / 2 (factor of 2 for round trip).
- [x] **Maximum imaging depth:** d_max = c / (2 · PRF).
- [x] **Axial resolution:** ≈ c / (2 · Bandwidth). Higher bandwidth = better resolution.
- [x] **Lateral resolution:** ≈ λ · z / D. Larger aperture D = better lateral resolution.
- [x] **Transducer resonant frequency:** f_r = c_crystal / (2 · t). Thinner crystal = higher frequency.
- [x] **Doppler shift formula:** Δf = 2 · f₀ · v · cos θ / c. Always has factor of 2 for pulse-echo.
- [x] **Doppler angle:** Always use **cos θ** (component along beam). θ = 90° gives zero shift (cannot measure perpendicular flow).
- [x] **A-mode:** 1D spikes; amplitude vs depth; stationary transducer.
- [x] **B-mode:** 2D grayscale brightness map; scanned image; clinical workhorse.
- [x] **M-mode:** Motion of one scan line vs time; cardiology and fetal heart rate.
- [x] **CW Doppler:** Two crystals, measures any velocity, no depth resolution.
- [x] **PW Doppler:** Pulsed, depth-selective, limited to lower velocities (Nyquist limit).
- [x] **Color Doppler:** PW Doppler overlaid on B-mode; red = toward transducer, blue = away.
- [x] **PZT crystal:** Lead Zirconate Titanate; the standard piezoelectric material.
- [x] **Matching layer:** Z = √(Z_PZT × Z_tissue); maximizes energy transfer into body.
- [x] **Backing material:** Absorbs rearward wave, shortens pulse, improves axial resolution.
- [x] **Coupling gel:** Water-based, eliminates air gap, Z ≈ 1.5 × 10⁶ Rayl.
- [x] **Trade-off:** Higher frequency = better resolution, shallower penetration; lower frequency = deeper penetration, poorer resolution.
- [x] **TGC (Time-Gain Compensation):** Amplifies echoes from deeper tissues to compensate for attenuation.
- [x] **Frame rate:** PRF / N_lines; must be high enough for real-time perception (>15 fps).
- [x] **Piezoelectric effect:** Direct (mechanical → electrical) for reception; converse (electrical → mechanical) for transmission.
- [x] **Acoustic impedance mismatch** is the *only* physical mechanism that produces echoes — no mismatch, no image.
- [x] **Safety note:** Ultrasound is non-ionizing but thermal (heat) and mechanical (cavitation) bioeffects exist; ALARA principle applies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Imaging Systems (Only Basics and Principles)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT747 - BIOMEDICAL ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermal Imaging Systems (Only Basics and Principles)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermal Imaging** (also called **Thermography** or **Infrared Thermography**) is a non-contact, non-invasive, non-ionizing biomedical imaging modality that detects, records, and visualizes the **infrared (IR) radiation** spontaneously emitted from the surface of the human body, and converts this invisible thermal energy into a visible **thermogram** (a false-color or grayscale image representing the temperature distribution).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECAT747 Module 4):** The scope of this topic is **strictly limited to the fundamentals and operating principles**. Specific topics to be mastered include: blackbody radiation, IR emission from the human body, the fundamental block architecture, and the basic classification of IR detectors. No clinical case-study analysis or advanced image-processing techniques are required at this stage.

### Key Terminology Decoded

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Thermogram** | A 2-D pictorial map of surface temperature distribution |
| **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$)** | The efficiency of a surface to emit IR radiation relative to a perfect blackbody |
| **Blackbody** | An idealized physical object that absorbs **100%** of incident radiation and emits the maximum possible thermal radiation at every wavelength for a given temperature |
| **MWIR / LWIR** | Mid-Wave Infrared (3–5 $\mu m$) / Long-Wave Infrared (8–14 $\mu m$) — the two biological "atmospheric windows" |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Silent Camera" of Heat

Imagine you are standing in a completely dark room at night. Your eyes (sensitive to visible light, 0.4–0.7 $\mu m$) see nothing. However, if you had a camera that could "see" the heat leaving your friend's body, you would clearly see a glowing silhouette — their warm nose tip, the cool patches over their veins, and the hot spots where their muscles are working hard. That camera is a **thermal imager**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** Thermal imaging does **NOT** illuminate the body like an X-ray or flashlight. Instead, it passively listens to the **silent whispers of heat** that every cell in your body is constantly broadcasting, and paints a colorful picture of what it hears.

### Why is this useful in medicine?
- Body temperature is a **vital sign**. Disease alters local blood flow and metabolism, which in turn alters skin temperature.
- A thermal imager is essentially a **contactless, radiation-free thermometer that draws a picture instead of showing a number**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants (Mandatory to memorize for KTU)**
> - **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
> - **Wien's displacement constant:** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$
> - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
> - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
> - **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$

---

## 1.3 The Infrared Spectrum — Where does "heat" live?

The electromagnetic spectrum is divided into regions based on wavelength. Infrared radiation occupies the region **immediately beyond red visible light**.

| Spectral Region | Wavelength Range | Source / Relevance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Visible Light | 0.4 – 0.7 $\mu m$ | Reflected sunlight, room lighting |
| **Near Infrared (NIR)** | 0.7 – 3 $\mu m$ | Hot metal, not used in medical thermal imaging |
| **Mid-Wave IR (MWIR)** | 3 – 5 $\mu m$ | One of two biological imaging bands |
| **Long-Wave IR (LWIR)** | 8 – 14 $\mu m$ | Primary medical imaging band; matches peak body emission |
| Far Infrared | 14 – 1000 $\mu m$ | Used in spectroscopy |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Human Body emits peak radiation around $\lambda \approx 9.3 \,\mu m$** at a skin temperature of $\approx 310$ K. This is exactly why **LWIR (8–14 $\mu m$)** cameras are the dominant choice in clinical thermal imaging — they "see" exactly where the body emits the most.

---

## 1.4 Thermal Imaging vs. Night Vision — Clearing the Confusion

Students frequently confuse these. Let us clarify definitively.

| Feature | Night Vision Device | Thermal Imager |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Operates in** | Near IR (reflected light) | LWIR / MWIR (emitted heat) |
| **Needs light?** | Yes — amplifies starlight or uses IR illuminators | No — works in total darkness |
| **Sees through smoke?** | No | Yes |
| **Medical use?** | None | Extensive (fever, cancer, vascular) |
| **Image basis** | Reflected photons | Emitted thermal photons |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Physics Foundation: Blackbody Radiation Laws

The entire science of thermal imaging rests on four foundational laws of blackbody radiation. The KTU 2024 scheme frequently asks derivations or numerical applications of these laws, especially **Stefan-Boltzmann** and **Wien's displacement**.

### 2.1.1 Planck's Radiation Law

A blackbody does not emit equally at all wavelengths. The spectral emissive power $E_\lambda$ (energy emitted per unit area per unit wavelength per unit time) follows **Planck's law**:

$$E_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left( e^{\frac{hc}{\lambda k T}} - 1 \right)} \quad \left[ \text{W/m}^3 \right]$$

- $h$ = Planck's constant
- $c$ = Speed of light
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength of emitted radiation
- $T$ = Absolute surface temperature in Kelvin
- $k$ = Boltzmann constant

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Planck's law shows that emission is **not** linear with wavelength. There exists one specific wavelength where emission is maximum, and that maximum shifts with temperature.

---

### 2.1.2 Wien's Displacement Law (Derivable from Planck's Law)

By differentiating Planck's law with respect to $\lambda$ and setting $\frac{dE_\lambda}{d\lambda} = 0$, we obtain the wavelength at which emission is maximum:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}$$

This is **Wien's Displacement Law**. As temperature increases, the peak emission shifts toward shorter wavelengths (this is why heated iron glows red, then orange, then white).

**Numerical Application (KTU Style):**
For human skin at $T = 310$ K:
$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{310} \approx 9.35 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 9.35 \,\mu m$$

This lies squarely in the **LWIR (8–14 $\mu m$)** band — confirming why medical thermal cameras are built for LWIR.

---

### 2.1.3 Stefan-Boltzmann Law

By integrating Planck's law over **all wavelengths** from 0 to $\infty$, we obtain the **total** emissive power (energy radiated per unit area per unit time from a blackbody):

$$\boxed{W = \sigma T^4 \quad \left[ \text{W/m}^2 \right]}$$

For a **real (gray) body**, this becomes:

$$W = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the **emissivity** ($0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Human skin emissivity $\varepsilon \approx 0.98$** — meaning human skin behaves almost exactly like a perfect blackbody for IR wavelengths. This is precisely why thermal imaging of the body works so reliably.

---

### 2.1.4 Emissivity: The "Inefficiency Factor"

A perfect blackbody has $\varepsilon = 1$. Real-world objects (polished metal $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$; wood $\varepsilon \approx 0.9$) emit less than the theoretical maximum.

$$W_{actual} = \varepsilon \cdot W_{blackbody} = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

In thermal imaging, this is critical:
- High $\varepsilon$ → bright, accurate thermal image
- Low $\varepsilon$ → image dominated by **reflected** ambient IR, not true body temperature (a common pitfall in KTU numerical problems)

---

### 2.1.5 Kirchhoff's Law (Heat-Balance Principle)

For an object in thermal equilibrium, **good absorbers are good emitters**, and **good reflectors are poor emitters**:

$$\varepsilon = \alpha \quad \text{(absorptivity = emissivity)}$$

This is why human skin, which absorbs $\approx 98\%$ of incident IR, also emits $\approx 98\%$.

---

## 2.2 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is the single most important revision asset for this topic. Memorize every row.**

| # | Law / Equation | Mathematical Form | Units | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Planck's Law | $E_\lambda = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5(e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1)}$ | W/m³ | Spectral analysis of IR emission |
| 2 | Stefan-Boltzmann (Blackbody) | $W = \sigma T^4$ | W/m² | Total radiated heat flux |
| 3 | Stefan-Boltzmann (Real body) | $W = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | W/m² | Real-world IR camera calibration |
| 4 | Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | m·K | Selection of detector spectral band |
| 5 | Emissivity Relation | $\varepsilon = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{blackbody}}$ | dimensionless | Correction for non-ideal surfaces |
| 6 | Net Heat Exchange (two bodies) | $W_{net} = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | W/m² | Fever screening logic |
| 7 | Detector Responsivity | $R = \frac{V_{signal}}{P_{incident}}$ | V/W | Detector performance metric |
| 8 | Noise Equivalent Power | $NEP = \frac{P_{min}}{\sqrt{\Delta f}}$ | W/$\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ | Detector sensitivity threshold |
| 9 | Body Skin Temperature | $T_{skin} \approx 306$ to $310$ K | K | Standard input for medical IR design |
| 10 | LWIR Imaging Window | 8 – 14 $\mu m$ | $\mu m$ | Atmospheric & biological transparency |

---

## 2.3 The Operational Logic: Step-by-Step Flow of a Thermal Imager

A thermal imaging system operates in a clean 5-stage signal chain:

1. **IR Collection Stage:** Specialized **germanium or zinc selenide lenses** focus incoming long-wave IR onto a detector. (Standard glass blocks IR.)
2. **IR-to-Electrical Conversion:** The detector element absorbs IR photons and converts the thermal energy into a proportional electrical signal (voltage/current).
3. **Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, noise filtering, and analog-to-digital conversion occur on the focal-plane electronics.
4. **Image Reconstruction:** A digital processor reads each pixel value and assigns a color or grayscale shade from a calibrated **temperature-to-color palette**.
5. **Display Output:** A thermogram is rendered on a monitor — typically with a **rainbow palette** (red = hot, blue = cold) or grayscale.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why thermal imaging matters |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Breast cancer screening** | Tumors are hypervascular → locally warmer than surrounding tissue |
| **Burn depth assessment** | Deep burns are cooler; superficial burns are hot and inflamed |
| **Peripheral vascular disease** | Reduced blood flow → cool extremities (Raynaud's, diabetes) |
| **Inflammatory arthritis** | Inflamed joints emit more heat than healthy ones |
| **COVID-19 fever screening** | Non-contact forehead/eye temperature triage |
| **Sports medicine** | Detects muscle fatigue, injury hot-spots before swelling appears |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law (KTU 14-Mark Favorite)

### Step 1 — Set the objective
The total emissive power $W$ is the area under the spectral emissive power curve. So we integrate Planck's law over all wavelengths from 0 to infinity:

$$W = \int_0^{\infty} E_\lambda(\lambda, T)\, d\lambda$$

Substituting Planck's expression:

$$W = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left( e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1 \right)} \, d\lambda$$

### Step 2 — Substitute to simplify the integral
Let $x = \frac{hc}{\lambda k T}$, which gives $\lambda = \frac{hc}{x k T}$.

Differentiating: $d\lambda = -\frac{hc}{x^2 k T}\, dx$

The limits transform as:
- When $\lambda \to 0$, $x \to \infty$
- When $\lambda \to \infty$, $x \to 0$

### Step 3 — Rewrite the integrand
After substitution (the minus sign flips the limits back to 0 to $\infty$):

$$W = \frac{2 \pi k^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the integral
The integral $\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$ is a famous definite integral equal to $\frac{\pi^4}{15}$.

$$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} \, dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

### Step 5 — Assemble the final expression

$$W = \frac{2 \pi k^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \left(\frac{2 \pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2}\right) T^4$$

The bracketed quantity is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**:

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$

### Step 6 — Final boxed result

$$\boxed{W = \sigma T^4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of conversion:** Every step translated a physics statement (sum total of all wavelengths) into a pure temperature dependence. The beauty is that the $h, c, k$ all collapse into a single experimentally measured constant $\sigma$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law from Planck's Law

### Step 1 — Objective
Find the wavelength $\lambda_{max}$ where $E_\lambda$ is maximum for a given $T$.

### Step 2 — Apply the calculus condition
At the maximum of $E_\lambda$, the derivative vanishes:

$$\frac{dE_\lambda}{d\lambda} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Planck expression
For convenience, rewrite Planck's law using $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ and $C_2 = \frac{hc}{k}$:

$$E_\lambda = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 (e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1)}$$

### Step 4 — Differentiate using logarithmic differentiation
Taking natural log: $\ln E_\lambda = \ln C_1 - 5\ln\lambda - \ln(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1)$

Differentiating with respect to $\lambda$:

$$\frac{1}{E_\lambda}\frac{dE_\lambda}{d\lambda} = -\frac{5}{\lambda} + \frac{C_2/\lambda^2 T}{e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1}$$

### Step 5 — Set equal to zero and rearrange
$$-\frac{5}{\lambda} + \frac{C_2}{\lambda^2 T} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1} = 0$$

Let $y = \frac{C_2}{\lambda T} = \frac{hc}{\lambda k T}$. The equation becomes:

$$5(1 - e^{-y}) = y$$

The transcendental equation has the numerical solution:

$$y = 4.965$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $\lambda_{max}$

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{hc}{4.965 \, k} \cdot \frac{1}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)

**Problem:** A medical thermal imager is used on a patient whose forehead temperature reads $38^\circ C$. Calculate:
1. The total IR power radiated per cm² of skin (assume $\varepsilon = 0.98$).
2. The peak wavelength of emission.

### Given Data
- $T = 38 + 273 = 311$ K
- $\varepsilon = 0.98$
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴

### Solution to Part (a)

Convert skin area to m²: $A = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$

Apply Stefan-Boltzmann for a real body:

$$W = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 0.98 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (311)^4 \\
&= 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.347 \times 10^9 \\
&= 519.4 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Total power from 1 cm²:

$$P = W \times A = 519.4 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0519 \text{ W} = 51.9 \text{ mW}$$

### Solution to Part (b)

Apply Wien's displacement law:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{311} = 9.32 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} \approx 9.32 \,\mu m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic check:** This peak wavelength lies in the **LWIR window (8–14 $\mu m$)**, confirming that clinical thermal cameras are correctly designed for this spectral band.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: IR Detector Classes

| Parameter | Thermal Detector (e.g., Bolometer) | Photon Detector (e.g., HgCdTe) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Operating principle** | IR heats the element → resistance changes | IR photons release charge carriers (quantum) |
| **Spectral response** | Broadband (all IR wavelengths) | Narrowband (cutoff wavelength) |
| **Operating temperature** | Room temperature (uncooled) | Requires cryogenic cooling (77 K) |
| **Response time** | Slow (milliseconds) | Very fast (microseconds) |
| **Cost & portability** | Low cost, compact, ideal for clinics | Expensive, bulky, lab-grade |
| **KTU usage** | Most modern medical thermal cameras | Research / military thermography |

---

## 3.5 Common IR Detector Types (Basics for KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 asks for a brief descriptive understanding only. No deep physics derivations.**

### 1. Thermopile Detector
- Series array of **thermocouples**.
- Incident IR heats hot junctions; cold junctions are shielded.
- Output is the sum of **Seebeck voltages**.
- Slow but robust; used in ear thermometers.

### 2. Bolometer Detector
- A thin resistive element whose **resistance changes with temperature**.
- Measured by passing a bias current and reading voltage change.
- Forms the pixel of modern **microbolometer arrays** (uncooled focal-plane arrays).

### 3. Pyroelectric Detector
- Uses materials (e.g., lithium tantalate) that change **polarization** when heated.
- Only responds to *changing* IR flux, not steady-state.
- Common in intruder alarms and entry-level thermal cameras.

### 4. Photon (Quantum) Detectors
- Use semiconductor junctions (e.g., InSb, HgCdTe).
- A photon of IR promotes an electron across the bandgap.
- Highly sensitive but require **liquid nitrogen cooling**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]
**Define thermal imaging. List any two advantages of thermal imaging over conventional radiography.**

**Model Answer:**

Thermal imaging is a non-invasive diagnostic technique that captures the infrared radiation emitted from the body surface and converts it into a visible thermogram representing the temperature distribution.

**Advantages over conventional radiography (any two):**
1. **Non-ionizing:** Uses passive IR radiation; no X-ray exposure — safe for repeated use, pregnant women, and children.
2. **Non-contact & painless:** No physical contact with the patient — eliminates infection transmission risk.
3. **Real-time functional imaging:** Detects physiological changes (blood flow, inflammation) before structural changes appear.
4. **Portable and inexpensive** compared to X-ray/CT imaging infrastructure.

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]
**State Stefan-Boltzmann Law. Mention its significance in thermal imaging.**

**Model Answer:**

**Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** The total radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time from the surface of a blackbody is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature:

$$W = \sigma T^4$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

**Significance in thermal imaging:**
1. It establishes the **fundamental quantitative link** between an object's temperature and the IR power it emits — the very basis of every thermal camera's calibration.
2. Because of the $T^4$ dependence, even **small temperature differences** (0.1 K, as in breast tumors) produce **detectable IR power variations**, enabling high-sensitivity medical diagnosis.
3. For real (non-ideal) bodies, the modified form $W = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ introduces the **emissivity correction** — essential for accurate body surface temperature measurement.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is split into two sub-parts, typically (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. The student must answer **either** Choice A **or** Choice B in full.

---

### **Question A.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Full Module Choice]

**(a)** Explain the physical principles of infrared emission from the human body. Derive the relationship between radiated power and temperature using the Stefan-Boltzmann law. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Blackbody concept:** A blackbody is an idealized object that absorbs 100% of incident radiation across all wavelengths. The human skin, with emissivity $\varepsilon \approx 0.98$, behaves almost like a blackbody in the LWIR range.

**Step 2 — Total emissive power:** Integrating Planck's spectral emissive power over all wavelengths:

$$W = \int_0^{\infty} E_\lambda(\lambda, T)\, d\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Apply substitution** $x = \frac{hc}{\lambda kT}$:

$$W = \frac{2\pi k^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2}\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\, dx$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the standard integral:** The result is $\frac{\pi^4}{15}$, leading to:

$$W = \sigma T^4$$

**Step 5 — Human body application:** For skin at $T = 310$ K with $\varepsilon = 0.98$:

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (310)^4 \\
&\approx 510 \text{ W/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering significance:** Because the $T^4$ dependence amplifies small thermal variations, pathologies that alter local temperature by even 0.5–1°C are detectable.

---

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of a thermal imaging system used in medical diagnosis. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A medical thermal imager operates through the following sequential stages:

1. **IR Emission from Body:** The patient's skin emits IR radiation, peaking at $\lambda \approx 9.3 \,\mu m$ for normal body temperature (310 K).

2. **Optical Collection:** A **germanium (Ge) lens** focuses the incoming IR. (Standard glass is opaque to LWIR.) A **mechanical chopper** modulates the IR beam for AC-coupled detection, eliminating thermal drift.

3. **IR Detection:** A **microbolometer focal plane array (FPA)** — typically 640 × 480 pixels — absorbs IR. Each pixel's resistance changes proportionally to incident thermal energy.

4. **Signal Processing:** The analog voltage from each pixel is amplified, digitized by an ADC, and processed by a DSP. **Two-point calibration** against blackbody references (typically 20°C and 40°C) ensures absolute temperature accuracy.

5. **Display:** The DSP maps each pixel's temperature to a **false-color palette** (e.g., ironbow: black → purple → red → yellow → white = cold to hot) and displays the thermogram on an LCD.

**Key medical applications** highlighted in the conclusion: breast tumor detection (hypervascular hot spots), burn assessment, vascular disease diagnosis, and fever screening.

---

### **Question B.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** State and explain Wien's Displacement Law. Using this law, determine the peak emission wavelength of the human body at a normal skin temperature of 32°C. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of Wien's Law:**
$$\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$

The wavelength at which a blackbody emits maximum radiation is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature.

**Step 2 — Conversion of temperature:**
$$T = 32 + 273 = 305 \text{ K}$$

**Step 3 — Application of the formula:**
$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{305} = 9.50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 9.5 \,\mu m$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
This wavelength falls in the **Long-Wave Infrared (LWIR) window (8–14 $\mu m$)**, which is the band chosen for medical thermal cameras because:
- The atmosphere is transparent in this range (low absorption by water vapor).
- It coincides with the peak emission of human tissue.
- LWIR detectors (microbolometers) work efficiently at room temperature.

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
Wien's Law is the **design compass** for thermal camera manufacturers — it dictates the detector spectral band.

---

**(b)** Compare thermal detectors and photon detectors used in infrared thermal imaging. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A clean comparison table covering **principle, spectral response, cooling, speed, sensitivity, cost** is expected.

| Parameter | Thermal Detector | Photon Detector |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mechanism | IR → heat → resistance/voltage change | IR photon → electron-hole pair generation |
| Spectral response | Broadband, wavelength-independent | Limited by semiconductor bandgap |
| Cooling | Not required (room temp operation) | Required (liquid nitrogen 77 K) |
| Response time | Slow (ms range) | Very fast ($\mu$s range) |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | Very high |
| Cost | Low — ideal for clinical use | High — used in research/military |
| Example | Microbolometer | HgCdTe, InSb |

**Conclusion:** Modern medical thermal cameras overwhelmingly use **microbolometer arrays** because they deliver clinically adequate sensitivity without the cost, weight, and maintenance burden of cryogenic cooling.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ${}^\circ C$ with K** — Stefan-Boltzmann and Wien's laws *strictly* require **absolute temperature in Kelvin**. Adding 273 (or 273.15) is non-negotiable.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the emissivity factor $\varepsilon$** — Using $W = \sigma T^4$ on real human skin without $\varepsilon$ (≈ 0.98) is a 1-mark penalty. Always write the **real-body form**: $W = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.
>
> 3. **Wrong detector naming** — Students often write "thermometer" instead of "thermopile" or "bolometer." Memorize the four canonical detector types and their principles.
>
> 4. **Confusing MWIR vs LWIR** — Medical imaging = **LWIR (8–14 $\mu m$)**, NOT MWIR. MWIR is used in military/target tracking.
>
> 5. **Skipping the calibration step** in the block diagram — Examiners explicitly check for **two-point blackbody calibration** as part of the working principle. Mentioning it fetches 1–2 easy marks.
>
> 6. **Writing thermal imaging "uses X-rays"** — This is a critical factual error. Thermal imaging uses **passive infrared emission**, not ionizing radiation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print this and read 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ Thermal imaging = passive detection of **IR radiation emitted by the body**; it is **non-ionizing, non-contact, and non-invasive**.
- ✅ The human body emits peak IR at **~ 9.3 $\mu m$** (LWIR band, 8–14 $\mu m$).
- ✅ **Skin emissivity** $\varepsilon \approx 0.98$ — behaves almost as a blackbody.
- ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $W = \sigma T^4$ (blackbody) / $W = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ (real body).
- ✅ **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K.
- ✅ **Planck's Law** describes spectral emissive power; integrate to get Stefan-Boltzmann; differentiate to get Wien's.
- ✅ Constants to memorize: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$, $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$.
- ✅ Block diagram flow: **Skin → IR emission → Germanium lens → Chopper → Microbolometer FPA → Amplifier → ADC → DSP → Display**.
- ✅ **Thermal detectors:** Thermopile, Bolometer/Microbolometer, Pyroelectric — uncooled.
- ✅ **Photon detectors:** HgCdTe, InSb — cryogenically cooled, high sensitivity.
- ✅ **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (good absorbers are good emitters).
- ✅ Medical applications: **breast cancer detection, burn assessment, vascular disease, inflammation, fever screening**.
- ✅ Two-point blackbody calibration (typically 20°C and 40°C) is mandatory for absolute temperature accuracy.
- ✅ Thermal imaging ≠ night vision; thermal imaging ≠ X-ray. Use the right terminology in your answer.
